Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ ٱلسَّاعَةِ شَىْءٌ عَظِيمٌ (١)
O mankind! Be wary of your Lord! Indeed the quake of the Hour is a terrible thing. (1)
يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَهَا تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّآ أَرْضَعَتْ وَتَضَعُ كُلُّ ذَاتِ حَمْلٍ حَمْلَهَا وَتَرَى ٱلنَّاسَ سُكَـٰرَىٰ وَمَا هُم بِسُكَـٰرَىٰ وَلَـٰكِنَّ عَذَابَ ٱللَّهِ شَدِيدٌ (٢)
The day that you will see it, every suckling female will be unmindful what she suckled, and every pregnant female will deliver her burden, and you will see the people drunk, yet they will not be drunken, but Allah’s punishment is severe. (2)

Content of Surah Al-Hajj

This Surah, named "Surah Al-Hajj" due to the portion of its verses discussing the Hajj pilgrimage, is among those Surahs whose "Makki" or "Madani" status is debated among commentators and historians of the Quran. A group considers it—except for a few verses—to be "Makki," while another group believes all of it—except for a few verses—was revealed in "Madinah." Some also consider it a combination of "Makki" and "Madani" verses.

However, considering our understanding of Makki and Madani Surahs—or in other words, the atmospheres of Madinah and Makkah, the needs of the Muslims, and the nature of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله teachings to them in these two regions—the verses of this Surah resemble Madani Surahs in several aspects. This is because the command for Hajj, including some of its details, as well as the command for Jihad, is consistent with the situation of the Muslims in Madinah, even though the emphasis of the verses on the issues of the Origin (Creation) and the Resurrection is not inconsistent with Makki Surahs.

The author of Tarikh al-Quran, based on the history of "Ibn al-Nadim's Fihrist" and "Nazm al-Durar," states: Surah Al-Hajj, except for a few verses, was revealed in Madinah, and those few verses were revealed between Makkah and Madinah. He adds that this Surah is the 106th Surah revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and, according to the order of revelation, comes after Surah Al-Nur and before Surah Al-Munafiqun.

In any case, overall, the "Madani" status of the Surah appears stronger.

In terms of content, the subjects of this Surah can be divided into several sections:

  1. Numerous verses regarding the "Resurrection" and its logical proofs, and warnings to heedless people about the scenes of the Day of Judgment and the like, which begin from the start of this Surah and comprise a significant portion of it.

  2. A considerable section also speaks of the struggle against polytheism and polytheists, drawing attention to the signs of the Lord in the universe to make humans aware of the Creator's greatness.

  3. A portion of this Surah invites people to examine the cautionary fate of past peoples and the painful divine punishments that descended upon them, including the fates of the people of Nuh, 'Ad, Thamud, and the people of Ibrahim, Lut, Shu'ayb, and Musa علیهم السلام.

  4. Another section concerns the issue of Hajj and its historical background from the time of Ibrahim عليه السلام, followed by the issues of sacrifice, circumambulation (Tawaf), and the like.

  5. A part of it is about the struggle against oppressors and fighting against invading enemies.

  6. Finally, a portion consists of exhortations and advice on various aspects of life, encouraging prayer (Salat), zakat, enjoining good and forbidding evil, and trust in and attention toward Allah.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah Al-Hajj

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Hajj, Allah will give him the reward of a Hajj and an Umrah performed, equal to the number of all those who have performed Hajj and Umrah in the past and those who will do so in the future!" [1] Undoubtedly, this great reward and virtue are not for mere verbal recitation alone; it is a recitation that fosters thought, and a thought that nurtures action.

In reality, one who places this Surah and its content—from the Origin and Resurrection to the devotional and moral commands, and issues related to Jihad and the struggle against oppressors—into the depths of their soul and makes it their practical program, develops a spiritual and soulful connection with all believers of the past and future. This connection makes them a partner in their deeds and makes them partners in his deeds, without anything being diminished from their rewards, and he becomes a link between all people of faith across all centuries and ages.

With these descriptions and conditions, the reward mentioned in the above hadith does not seem surprising.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah Al-Hajj.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ السَّاعَةِ شَيْ‌ءٌ عَظِيمٌ (1) يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَها تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّا أَرْضَعَتْ وَ تَضَعُ كُلُّ ذاتِ حَمْلٍ حَمْلَها وَ تَرَى النَّاسَ سُكارى‌ وَ ما هُمْ بِسُكارى‌ وَ لكِنَّ عَذابَ اللَّهِ شَدِيدٌ (2)

Commentary

The Great Earthquake of Resurrection

This Surah begins with two shocking and stirring verses regarding the Resurrection and its precursors—verses that involuntarily pull a person out of this transient material life and direct them toward the terrifying future that awaits them.

A future that, if one does not think about it today and prepare for it, is truly horrifying; but if one does, it is delightful and soul-refreshing.

First, it addresses all people without exception, saying: "O mankind! Fear your Lord and practice piety, for the earthquake of the Hour is a momentous and great thing" (يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ السَّاعَةِ شَيْ‌ءٌ عَظِيمٌ).

The address "O mankind" (يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ) is clear evidence that there is no difference or discrimination in terms of race, language, eras, centuries, places, geographical regions, sects, or tribes; the believer and the disbeliever, the small and the great, the old and the young, the man and the woman of today and the future are all included in it.

The phrase "Fear your Lord" (اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمْ) is the essence of all programs for salvation, for on one hand, it expresses "Monotheism" (your Lord) and on the other hand, "Piety" (Taqwa); thus, both ideological and practical programs are gathered within it.

By mentioning the phrase "for the earthquake of the Hour is a great thing" (إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ السَّاعَةِ شَيْ‌ءٌ عَظِيمٌ), it briefly recounts a reality mentioned in many verses of the Quran: that the Resurrection begins with a severe revolution and transformation in the structure of the universe. Mountains are uprooted, seas surge together, the earth and sky are crushed, and a new world with a new life begins. People on the threshold of the Resurrection fall into a great terror and lose their composure.

The next verse describes examples of the reflection of this great terror in several phrases, saying: "The day you see the earthquake of Resurrection, such terror will envelop everyone that every nursing mother will be heedless of her suckling child" (يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَها تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّا أَرْضَعَتْ).

"And every pregnant woman (who is in that scene) will miscarry her burden" (وَ تَضَعُ كُلُّ ذاتِ حَمْلٍ حَمْلَها).

The third reflection is that "you will see the people as if they are intoxicated, while they are not intoxicated!" (وَ تَرَى النَّاسَ سُكارى‌ وَ ما هُمْ بِسُكارى‌).

"But the punishment of Allah is severe," which has cast such dread and terror into the hearts and made humans lose themselves (وَ لكِنَّ عَذابَ اللَّهِ شَدِيدٌ).

Key Points

  1. In worldly earthquakes and terrifying events, these phenomena sometimes occur partially—meaning mothers might forget their children, pregnant women might miscarry, and some might lose themselves like intoxicated individuals—but it is not universal or all-encompassing. However, the earthquake of Resurrection is such that upon witnessing it, all people will be afflicted by these states.
  1. These verses might refer to the end of the world, which is the precursor to the Resurrection. In this case, "pregnant women" or "nursing children" would carry their literal meaning. However, the possibility also exists that it refers to the earthquake of the Day of Judgment itself (indicated by the phrase "But the punishment of Allah is severe"). In this case, the mention of the above phrases serves as an illustration—meaning the scene is so terrifying that if there were pregnant women, they would all miscarry, and if there were nursing children, mothers would completely forget them.
  1. We know that in Arabic literature, a woman who is nursing her child is usually referred to as "Murdi'" [1]. However, as a group of commentators and some lexicographers have written, sometimes this word is used in the feminine form (Murdi'ah) to refer specifically to the very moment of breastfeeding. In other words, Murdi' refers to a woman who is capable of nursing her child, but Murdi'ah is specific to a woman who has placed her breast in the mouth of her suckling child and is in the act of nursing! [2] Therefore, the above expression in the verse has a specific point, for it says: the intensity of the terror of the Resurrection earthquake is so great that even if a mother has her breast in the mouth of her suckling child, she will become so terrified that she will involuntarily pull the breast from its mouth and forget the child!

4. The phrase "You see the people as if they are intoxicated" (تَرَى النَّاسَ سُكارى‌)

This indicates that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who is the addressee of this phrase (and possibly the believers with very strong faith who follow in his footsteps), are safe from this great terror, because it says "you see the people" in this state, meaning you yourself are not like that.

  1. Many commentators and narrators of hadith have cited a narration from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the verses under discussion, which is appropriate to mention here: that the first two verses of this Surah were revealed on one of the nights of the "Battle of Banu al-Mustaliq" [3] while the people were moving toward the battlefield. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله called out to the people; they stopped and gathered around him. The Prophet recited these verses to them. The sound of weeping rose from the people, and the Muslims wept greatly that night. By morning, they had become so indifferent toward the world and worldly life that they did not even place saddles on their mounts nor pitch any tents. A group was weeping, and another group sat deep in thought.

The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Do you know what day this is?" They replied: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: "It is the day when out of every thousand people, 999 are sent toward Hell and only one toward Paradise!" This was hard for the Muslims, and they wept intensely, asking: "Then who will be saved?" The Prophet said:

"There are sinners other than you who constitute the majority. I hope that you will be at least one-fourth of the people of Paradise." (The Muslims shouted 'Allahu Akbar'). Then he said: "I hope you will be one-third of the people of Paradise." (They shouted 'Allahu Akbar' again). Then he added: "I hope that two-thirds of the people of Paradise will be from you, for the people of Paradise are 120 ranks, 80 of which are from my Ummah..." [1]

[1]: Because the "feminine marker" is used in cases where both male and female forms exist, but for matters like pregnancy or nursing which are specific to women and do not exist in men, there is no need for the ta' marbuta and the like.

[2]: Refer to Qamus al-Lughah, Tafsir al-Kashshaf, Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi, and Tafsir al-Mizan.

[3]: This battle took place in Sha'ban of the 6th year of the Hijra.

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, and other commentaries (with slight abridgment).

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ مَّرِيدٍ (٣)
Among the people are those who dispute about Allah without any knowledge, and follow every froward devil, (3)
كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُۥ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُۥ يُضِلُّهُۥ وَيَهْدِيهِ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٤)
about whom it has been decreed that he will mislead those who take him for an ally, and conduct them toward the punishment of the Blaze. (4)

Commentary

Followers of Satan!

Since the preceding verses depicted the general terror of the people during the occurrence of the Earthquake of Resurrection, the verses under discussion reflect the state of a group of oblivious individuals—how they remain heedless of such a momentous event.

It says: "And among the people is he who disputes about Allah without any knowledge" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُجادِلُ فِي اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ).

Sometimes they dispute regarding the principle of Monotheism (Tawhid), the Oneness of the Truth, and the negation of any partner for Him; at other times, they dispute regarding the power of Allah concerning the revival of the dead and the Resurrection. In any case, they possess no evidence whatsoever for their claims.

A group of commentators has stated that this verse was revealed concerning "Nadr bin al-Harith," who was among the fanatical, stubborn, and deceitful polytheists. He insisted that the angels were the daughters of Allah! He claimed that the Quran was a collection of "fables of the ancients" attributed to God, and he denied life after death.

Others have considered it a reference to all polytheists who engaged in disputes regarding the issue of Tawhid and the power of Allah.

However, considering that the "occasions of revelation" (shan nuzul) never limit the general scope of a verse, the result of these two views is the same: it includes all those who, through blind imitation, prejudice, or following superstitions and whims, rise to dispute against the Truth.

Then it adds: "These types of individuals, who follow no logic or knowledge, follow every rebellious and defiant devil" (وَ يَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطانٍ مَرِيدٍ).

Not just one devil, but all devils! This includes devils from among humans and jinn, each of whom has their own plan, program, trickery, and trap.

The word "marid" is derived from the root "mard" (rhyming with sard), which originally means a high land devoid of any vegetation. A tree that has lost its leaves is called "amrad," and for the same reason, a youth who has no hair growing on his face is also called "amrad."

Here, "marid" refers to someone who is devoid of any good, felicity, or point of strength; naturally, such a person will be rebellious, defiant, oppressive, and sinful.

It is clear what kind of fate awaits a person who follows a "devil devoid of everything."

Therefore, in the next verse, it says: "It has been decreed for him that whoever takes him as a friend and guardian, he will surely mislead him and lead him to the punishment of the Burning Fire" (كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَنْ تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ وَ يَهْدِيهِ إِلى‌ عَذابِ السَّعِيرِ) [1].

Key Points

1. Disputing for Truth and Falsehood Although the word "mujadalah" (dispute) in common parlance has taken on the meaning of baseless and illogical arguing, in its original linguistic sense, it is not so. Rather, it means any kind of discussion and dialogue, which is sometimes for the sake of truth and sometimes for falsehood. This is why the Quran commands the Prophet: وَ جادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ: "And dispute with them in a way that is best" (An-Nahl, 125).

2. Disputing for Falsehood is the Way of Satan Some great commentators believe that the phrase "disputes about Allah without knowledge" refers to the baseless dialogues of the polytheists, while the phrase "follows every rebellious devil" refers to their incorrect practical programs.

Others consider the first phrase to refer to their corrupt and superstitious beliefs, and the second phrase to their wrong and deviant practical actions.

However, since the verses before and after this revolve around the foundations of belief and the principles of faith, it is not unlikely that both phrases refer to a single reality; in other words, they are two sides—negation and affirmation—of the same subject. In the first phrase, it says: they rise to dispute about Allah and His power without knowledge, purely out of imitation, prejudice, and whim-worship. The second phrase says: it is natural that one who does not seek knowledge will follow every rebellious devil.

3. Why "Every" Devil? It is interesting that He does not say: "such a person follows Satan," but rather says "every rebellious devil." This is an indication that devils do not all follow a single line or a common program; rather, each has chosen a path and a trap for himself. These traps are so diverse that a person becomes lost in identifying them, except for those who, due to their faith and reliance on Allah, have come under His protection and are included in the phrase: "Except Your chosen servants among them" [2].

It is also necessary to mention that rebellion, defiance, and being devoid of good and blessing are inherent in the concept of the word "Satan," but here the word "marid" (lacking any good or felicity) is specifically mentioned afterward for emphasis, so that the fate of those who follow such leaders becomes completely clear.

4. Commentary on the Phrase "كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ" (It has been decreed for him) We know that this expression means to ordain or make mandatory, whether in the realm of creation (takwin) or in the realm of legislation (tashri'), laws, and regulations.

However, in any case, one should not imagine that this phrase implies "determinism" (jabr), as if devils are forced to mislead their followers and send them to the Abode of Ruin. Rather, this is the inevitable result of the program they have chosen by their own will. For example, when Iblis, the chief and leader of the devils, disobeyed Allah's command and by his own will and volition chose the path of rebellion and even objection to the Pure Essence of Allah, such a person could have no fate other than being astray and a misleader—and the same applies to other devils among humans and jinn.

This is exactly like saying: "Whoever becomes addicted to narcotics, misery and wretchedness are written on his forehead." Is this evidence of determinism? [3]

[1]: "Sa'ir" is from the root "sa'r" (rhyming with qa'r), meaning the flaming of fire. Here it refers to the burning fire of Hell, which is more scorching than any other fire. [2]: Surah Al-Hijr, 40. [3]: Regarding the referent of the pronoun in "عليه" (upon him), some have said it refers back to Satan, and some to the followers of Satan (derived from the phrase "And among the people..."). However, it appears that it refers back to Satan, especially since the nearby pronoun (the pronoun in مَنْ تَوَلَّاهُ) also refers back to Satan.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ مِّنَ ٱلْبَعْثِ فَإِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ ثُمَّ مِن مُّضْغَةٍ مُّخَلَّقَةٍ وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ لِّنُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ ۚ وَنُقِرُّ فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ مَا نَشَآءُ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ثُمَّ نُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَشُدَّكُمْ ۖ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُتَوَفَّىٰ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَرْذَلِ ٱلْعُمُرِ لِكَيْلَا يَعْلَمَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ عِلْمٍ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَتَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ هَامِدَةً فَإِذَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَآءَ ٱهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ وَأَنۢبَتَتْ مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ (٥)
O people! If you are in doubt about the resurrection, [consider that] We created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, then from a clinging mass, then from a fleshy tissue, partly formed and partly unformed, so that We may manifest [Our power] to you. We lodge in the wombs whatever [fetus] We wish for a specified term, then We bring you forth as infants, then [We rear you] so that you may come of age. [Then] there are some of you who are taken away, and there are some of you who are relegated to the nethermost age, such that he knows nothing after [having possessed] some knowledge. And you see the earth torpid, yet when We send down water upon it, it stirs and swells, and grows every delightful kind [of plant]. (5)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنَّهُۥ يُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَأَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٦)
All that is because Allah is the Reality and it is He who revives the dead, and He has power over all things, (6)
وَأَنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ ءَاتِيَةٌ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهَا وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْعَثُ مَن فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ (٧)
and because the Hour is bound to come, there is no doubt in it, and Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves. (7)

Commentary

Proofs of Resurrection in the Embryonic World and Plants

Since the preceding verses discussed the doubts of the opponents regarding the "Origin" and the "Resurrection," the verses under discussion argue two firm and logical proofs to establish the physical Resurrection: one through observing the transformations of the embryonic stages, and the other through the transformations of the earth during the growth of plants.

In truth, the Quran intends to describe scenes of the Resurrection that people deal with in this very worldly life and constantly witness with their own eyes yet remain heedless of, so they may know that life after death is not only not impossible, but that they perpetually observe similar scenes in their daily lives.

First, addressing all of humanity, It says: "O people! If you are in doubt about the Resurrection, consider this point: that We created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid (nutfah), then from a clinging clot ('alaqah), then from a lump of flesh (mudghah), partly formed and partly unformed" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِنَ الْبَعْثِ فَإِنَّا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ مِنْ تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ مُضْغَةٍ مُخَلَّقَةٍ وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ) [^1] "All of this is so that We may make clear to you" (لِنُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ) the truth that We are capable and powerful over all things.

"And We settle in the wombs whom We will for an appointed term" (وَنُقِرُّ فِي الْأَرْحَامِ مَا نَشَاءُ إِلَىٰ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى) so they may complete their developmental stages, and those We will, We cause to miscarry and exit the orbit halfway.

From then on, a new revolutionary period begins: "then We bring you out as a child" (ثُمَّ نُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا).

In this way, your limited and dependent life cycle in the mother's womb ends, and you step into a wider environment, full of light, purity, and far greater possibilities.

Again, the wheels of your evolutionary movement do not stop, and you continue to advance rapidly on this path: "then [We tend you] that you may reach your maturity" (ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوا أَشُدَّكُمْ) in body and intellect.

[^1]: "Mudghah" is derived from the root "madgh," meaning to chew. This term refers to a small piece of meat that a person can chew in one mouthful, and this is a striking metaphor for the embryo in the stage following the 'alaqah.

At this stage, ignorance turns into knowledge, weakness and inability turn into power and capability, and dependency turns into independence.

However, this wheel does not stop, even though "some of you are taken in death [early], while others are brought back to the most abject stage of life" (وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُتَفَىٰ وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰ أَرْذَلِ الْعُمُرِ), which is the extreme of old age.

Indeed, he reaches a stage "so that he will not know a thing after having known" (لِكَيْلَا يَعْلَمَ مِنْ بَعْدِ عِلْمٍ شَيْئًا). Veils of forgetfulness and oblivion cover the pages of his intellect and thought, and in fact, a state similar to childhood overtakes him!

This weakness, inability, and withering are evidence of the arrival of a new transitional stage, just as the loosening of a fruit's bond with the tree is evidence of its ripening and reaching the stage of separation.

These wondrous and successive transformations, which indicate the infinite power of the Lord, clarify the truth that everything—even the reviving of the dead—is easy and simple for Him.

Of course, regarding these various stages of life, there are many discussions that will follow in the "Key Points."

Then, proceeding to state the second proof, which is the life and vitality of plants, It says:

"In the winter season, you look at the earth and see it dry and dead, but when We send down life-giving drops of rain upon it and the spring season arrives, a movement and stir pervades it all, and it grows, and brings forth every kind of beautiful growth" (وَتَرَى الْأَرْضَ هَامِدَةً فَإِذَا أَنْزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا الْمَاءَ اهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ وَأَنْبَتَتْ مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ بَهِيجٍ) [^1]

In the following two verses, as a general summary and conclusion, the primary objective of stating these two proofs is explained through five points:

1- First, It says: "What was recounted in the previous verses regarding the various stages of life for humans and the world of plants is so that you may know that Allah is the Truth" (ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ).

And because He is the Truth, the system He has created is also the truth; therefore, it cannot be in vain or without purpose, just as we read elsewhere: "And We did not create the heaven and the earth and that between them aimlessly. That is the assumption of those who disbelieve" (وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَاطِلًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) (Saad: 27).

And since this world is not without purpose, and on the other hand, we do not find the ultimate goal within itself, we become certain that there is a Resurrection and a Day of Judgment.

2- This system governing the world of life tells us: "It is He who gives life to the dead" (وَأَنَّهُ يُحْيِي الْمَوْتَىٰ).

[^1]: "Hamidah" originally refers to a fire that has been extinguished, and it is said of land whose plants have dried up and remained motionless (Mufradat al-Raghib). Others have said "Hamidah" refers to a state that is the boundary between death and life (Tafsir Fi Zilal). "Ihtazzat" is from the root "hazz," meaning intense movement or stir. "Rabat" is from the root "rabw," meaning increase and growth; "Riba" (usury) is also from this root. "Bahij" means beautiful, striking, and joy-inducing.

The same One who clothes the dust in the garment of life, and transforms a worthless drop of fluid into a complete human being, and gives life to dead lands, He grants the dead a new life. With this continuous life-giving program of His in this world, can one still doubt the possibility of the Resurrection? [^1]

3- Another goal is for you to know that "Allah is powerful over all things" (وَأَنَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ) and nothing is impossible before His power.

Can the One who can transform lifeless dust into a nutfah, and advance a worthless nutfah through the stages of life, clothing it every day in a new garment of life, and make dried, depressed, and soulless lands so green and lush that the laughter of life rises from every corner—can such a One not be able to return man to a new life after death?

4- And again, all of this is so you may know that "the Hour [of the end of this world and the beginning of the next] is coming, no doubt about it" (وَأَنَّ السَّاعَةَ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَا).

5- And all these are a prelude to the final conclusion, which is that "Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves" (وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ يَبْعَثُ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ).

[^1]: Some commentators have considered the phrase "He gives life to the dead" as a reference to the life of humans on the Day of Resurrection, even though this meaning is also present in the phrase "Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves," but with the difference that the first phrase refers to the principle of life and the second refers to the manner of the dead being revived. However, the other interpretation, which we have emphasized more above, is that the phrase "He gives life to the dead" refers to Allah's continuous life-creation in this world, to serve as a proof for its possibility in the Resurrection.

Of course, these five results—some of which are preludes, some the objective of the prelude, some referring to possibility, and some to occurrence—complement one another, and all lead to a single point: that the Resurrection and the raising of the dead are not only possible but will certainly take place.

Those who doubt the possibility of life after death have a similar scene in the lives of humans and plants constantly before their eyes, repeated every year and every day.

And if they doubt the power of God, this is something they see clear examples of with their own eyes.

Were humans not created from dust in the beginning? Therefore, what is there to wonder at if they rise from the dust once more?

Do dead lands not come to life before our eyes every year? What is there to wonder at if dead humans, after years, receive life and rise from the dust?

And if they doubt the occurrence of such a thing, they should know that the system governing the creation of this world shows that there is a purpose behind it; otherwise, it would all be void and in vain, whereas this life of a few days, mixed with all these hardships and failures, is not something worthy of constituting the ultimate goal of the universe's creation.

Therefore, there must be another world, a vast and eternal world that is worthy of being considered the goal of creation.

Key Points

1- The Seven Stages of Human Life

In the above verses, to explain the issue of Resurrection and its possibility, the journey of man is described in a seven-stage path:

First, the time when he was dust. It is possible that "dust" here refers to the soil from which Adam was created, and it is also possible it refers to the fact that all humans, regardless of that, are from dust, because all the nutrients that form the nutfah and then the materials that nourish it are all taken from the soil.

Of course, undoubtedly a significant part of the human body is composed of "water" and a part of "oxygen" and "carbon" which are not taken from the soil, but since the main pillar of all body organs is formed by materials taken from the soil, the expression that man is from dust is entirely correct.

The second stage is the "nutfah" (drop of fluid) stage. Dust—this simple, mundane substance devoid of sense, movement, and life—turns into a nutfah, a nutfah composed of mysterious microscopic living beings called "sperm" in the male and "ovum" in the female. These floating microscopic beings are so small that in a single man's nutfah, there may be millions of "sperm"!

Interestingly, after birth, man usually follows a slow and gradual movement that mostly takes the form of "quantitative evolution," whereas his movement in the environment of the womb is accompanied by rapid leaps and qualitative transformations.

The successive and amazing transformations of the embryo in the world of the womb are as wondrous as, for example, a simple small pin turning into an airplane over a few months!

Today, "embryology" has become an extensive science, and scientists in this field have succeeded in examining the embryo at various stages, lifting the veil from the profound secrets of this mysterious phenomenon of the universe and presenting many wonders regarding it.

In the third stage, the nutfah reaches the stage of 'alaqah (clinging clot), and its cells, like a formless mulberry (in the shape of a piece of clotted blood), sit next to each other, which in scientific language is called "morula."

After a short period, the blastocoel, which is the beginning of the division of embryonic regions, appears (and the embryo at this stage is called "blastula").

In the fourth stage, the embryo gradually takes the shape of a piece of chewed meat (mudghah) without the various organs of the body being distinct in it.

But suddenly, changes appear in the "embryo's" shell, and its shape changes according to the function it must perform, and the body organs gradually appear. However, embryos that do not pass this stage and remain as they were or become defective are miscarried and removed from the cycle. The phrase "partly formed and partly unformed" (مُخَلَّقَةٍ وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ) may refer to this stage, meaning "complete in creation" and "incomplete in creation."

Interestingly, the Holy Quran, after mentioning these four stages, brings the phrase "so that We may make clear to you" (لِنُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ), indicating that these rapid and wondrous transformations that cause a small drop of water to turn into a complete human are a clear proof of Allah's power over all things.

Then, it refers to the three stages—fifth, sixth, and seventh—of the embryo that take place after birth, namely "childhood," "maturity," and "old age." [^1]

[^1]: It is noteworthy that regarding the birth of man, the expression used is "then We bring you out as a child" (ثُمَّ نُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا), whereas normally it should be mentioned in the plural (children/atfal). This expression may be because "tifl" (child) has an infinitive meaning, where singular and plural are the same, or it may be because the goal is to state the genus, not the specific characteristics and features of children, as at this stage the differences between humans are hidden and concealed, and they manifest in later stages.

It is also necessary to remember that the birth of man from dust as a living being is itself a great leap, and the various stages of the embryo are all considered successive leaps, and also the birth of man from the mother is itself a very important leap, and the stages of maturity and senescence are also considered leaps.

The Quran's expression in the above verse for "Resurrection" as "Ba'th" (raising/arousing) seemingly refers to this same concept of a leap that also occurs in the Resurrection.

Attention should also be paid to the fact that the Quran's speaking of these various embryonic stages—at a time when neither the science of embryology existed nor did people have significant information about the human embryonic period—is itself a living witness that this great Book originates from the world of revelation and the supernatural.

2- Physical Resurrection

Undoubtedly, wherever the Quran speaks of the return of humans and the Resurrection, it means the return of this human with soul and body in that world. Those who have restricted the Resurrection to its "spiritual" aspect and only believe in the survival of souls have by no means examined the verses of the Quran. For example, it is clear that verses such as the one above explicitly speak of physical Resurrection; otherwise, what similarity does spiritual Resurrection have to the embryonic period and the reviving of dead lands through the growth of plants?

Especially the last sentence of the verses under discussion, which comes as a final conclusion, clearly proves this point where it says: "And Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves" (وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ يَبْعَثُ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ), because the grave is the place of the body, not the soul.

Fundamentally, the entire astonishment of the polytheists was regarding this very issue: how a human who has turned into dust returns to life again; otherwise, the issue of the survival of the soul was not only not strange but was also accepted by the pre-Islamic peoples (reflect on this).

3- What is "Ardhal al-'Umur"?

"Ardhal" is from the root "radhl," meaning something base and undesirable, and "Ardhal al-'Umur" refers to the most undesirable periods of a human's life when he reaches the extreme of old age, and as the Quran says, he completely forgets his sciences and knowledge, becoming exactly like a child. In terms of knowledge, he is like a child; in terms of managing affairs, he is similar to a child; he becomes upset by a trivial thing just like a child and becomes happy and satisfied with a minor matter; he loses his capacity and patience, and sometimes his movements become childlike.

With the difference that people have no expectations from a child but they do from him; furthermore, in the case of children, there is hope that with the growth and development of their body and soul, all these states will disappear, but in the case of the frail and elderly, such hope does not exist. And with the difference that a child had nothing to lose, but this elderly person has lost all his vital capital.

For these reasons, the state of the elderly, compared to the state of children, is much more pathetic and distressing.

In some narrations, "Ardhal al-'Umur" has been interpreted as the age of one hundred years and above, [^1] and this may be looking at the average individual; otherwise, there are those who reach this stage at ages younger than one hundred, just as there are individuals found

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 472.

who are completely alert and aware even at ages higher than a hundred.

Especially among great scholars and scientists who are constantly engaged in scientific discussions, it is less frequently seen that such a situation occurs. In any case, we must seek refuge in Allah from this part of life. Meanwhile, remembering these years is enough to pull us out of pride and heedlessness: what we were in the beginning, what we are now, and what we will become in the future!

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَلَا هُدًى وَلَا كِتَـٰبٍ مُّنِيرٍ (٨)
Among the people are those who dispute concerning Allah without any knowledge or guidance, or an enlightening Book, (8)
ثَانِىَ عِطْفِهِۦ لِيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ ۖ وَنُذِيقُهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (٩)
turning aside disdainfully to lead [others] astray from the way of Allah. For such there is disgrace in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection We will make him taste the punishment of the burning: (9)
ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاكَ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (١٠)
‘That is because of what your hands have sent ahead, and because Allah is not tyrannical to the servants.’ (10)

Commentary

Once Again, Disputing with Falsehood

In these verses, the discussion again concerns those who engage in baseless and unfounded disputes regarding the Origin (Allah) and the Resurrection.

First, it says: "And among the people is he who disputes about Allah without knowledge or guidance or an enlightening Book" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُجادِلُ فِي اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَ لا هُدىً وَ لا كِتابٍ مُنِيرٍ).

The phrase "And among the people is he who disputes about Allah without knowledge" is exactly the same expression that appeared a few verses prior, and its repetition indicates that the first instance referred to one group, while this second instance refers to another group.

A group of commentators have identified the difference between these two groups as follows: the previous verse refers to the state of the ignorant and straying followers, whereas this verse refers to the leaders of this misguided group ^1. The phrase "to mislead [others] from the way of Allah" (لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ) indicates that the program of this group is to lead others astray, serving as a clear indication of this distinction. Similarly, the phrase "and follows every rebellious devil" in the preceding verses, which speaks of following devils, makes this meaning even clearer.

Regarding the difference between "knowledge" (علم), "guidance" (هدى), and "enlightening Book" (كتاب منير), commentators have various discussions. What seems most plausible is that "knowledge" refers to intellectual arguments, "guidance" refers to the direction and instruction of Divine leaders, and "enlightening Book" refers to the Heavenly Scriptures.

In simpler terms, it recounts the three well-known proofs: "the Book," "the Sunnah," and "intellectual reasoning." Given that "consensus" (ijma'), according to the research of scholars, also traces back to "Sunnah," all four proofs are encompassed in this expression.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that "guidance" refers to spiritual insights attained by a person through self-purification, piety, and refinement of the soul (of course, this meaning can be reconciled with what was mentioned above).

^1: Tafsir al-Mizan and Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi, under the verses in question.

In reality, scientific discussion and debate can only be fruitful if they are based on one of these proofs: intellectual reasoning, the Book, or the Sunnah.

Then, in a short and meaningful phrase, it addresses one of the causes of the deviation and misguidance of these leaders of error, saying: "Twisting his side [in pride] to mislead [others] from the way of Allah" (ثانِيَ عِطْفِهِ لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ). "Thani" (ثانِيَ) is derived from the root "thanya" (ثنى), meaning to twist or turn, and "itf" (عِطْفِ) means the side. Twisting the side is an idiom for arrogance, indifference, and turning away from something.

The phrase "to mislead" (ليضل) could be the objective of this turning away—meaning they disregard Divine verses and guidance specifically to lead people astray—or it could be the result of it, meaning the outcome of their indifference is that they prevent people from the path of truth.

In any case, it then describes their severe punishment in this world and the hereafter as follows: "For him in this world is disgrace, and on the Day of Resurrection We will make him taste the punishment of the Burning Fire" (لَهُ فِي الدُّنْيا خِزْيٌ وَ نُذِيقُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ).

And We will say to him: "That is for what your hands have put forth" (ذلِكَ بِما قَدَّمَتْ يَداكَ). "And Allah is never unjust to [His] servants" (وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ).

He neither punishes anyone without cause, nor does He increase the level of anyone's punishment without reason. His program is pure justice and justice alone ^1. This verse is among those that both negate the doctrine of the determinists (Jabriyun) and prove the principle of justice regarding the actions of Allah. (For further explanation, refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 195, under verse 182 of Surah Al-Imran).

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَعْبُدُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ حَرْفٍ ۖ فَإِنْ أَصَابَهُۥ خَيْرٌ ٱطْمَأَنَّ بِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنْ أَصَابَتْهُ فِتْنَةٌ ٱنقَلَبَ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦ خَسِرَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْخُسْرَانُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١١)
And among the people are those who worship Allah on the [very] fringe: if good fortune befalls him, he is content with it; but if an ordeal visits him he makes a turnabout, to become a loser in the world and the Hereafter. That is manifest loss. (11)
يَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَضُرُّهُۥ وَمَا لَا يَنفَعُهُۥ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ٱلْبَعِيدُ (١٢)
He invokes besides Allah that which can bring him neither benefit nor harm. That is extreme error. (12)
يَدْعُوا۟ لَمَن ضَرُّهُۥٓ أَقْرَبُ مِن نَّفْعِهِۦ ۚ لَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْعَشِيرُ (١٣)
He invokes someone whose harm is surely likelier than his benefit. An evil ally indeed, and an evil companion! (13)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ (١٤)
Allah will indeed admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them. Indeed Allah does whatever He desires. (14)

Commentary

Those on the Brink of Disbelief!

In the preceding verses, the discussion concerned two groups: the misled followers and the misleading leaders. However, the verses under discussion speak of a third group, who are the weak in faith.

The Quran describes this group as follows: "And among the people is he who worships Allah only with his tongue, but his heart's faith is very superficial and weak" (وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَعْبُدُ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ حَرْفٍ‌).

The expression "upon an edge" (عَلى‌ حَرْفٍ) may refer to the fact that their faith resides mostly on their tongues, and only a very faint, weak light of faith has shone into their hearts.

It may also indicate that they are not situated within the core of faith and Islam, but rather on its periphery and edge; for one of the meanings of harf is the edge of a mountain or the brink of objects. We know that those standing on the edge of something are not stable or firm; they are displaced from their path by a slight tremor. Such is the state of those weak in faith, whose faith is swept away by the smallest occurrence.

The Quran then proceeds to describe the instability of their faith, saying: "They are such that if the world turns toward them and some benefit or good reaches them, they feel secure and reassured!" They take this as evidence for the truth of Islam. "But if they are tested by afflictions, distress, or the withdrawal of a blessing, they turn away and revert to disbelief!" (فَإِنْ أَصابَهُ خَيْرٌ اطْمَأَنَّ بِهِ وَ إِنْ أَصابَتْهُ فِتْنَةٌ انْقَلَبَ عَلى‌ وَجْهِهِ‌) [^1]. It is as if they have accepted religion and faith merely as a means to attain material gains; if this goal is met, they consider the religion true, otherwise, they deem it baseless!

Ibn Abbas and several other early commentators have narrated regarding the occasion of revelation of this verse that sometimes a group of Bedouins would come to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). If their physical health improved, their horses produced good offspring, their wives gave birth to sons, and their wealth and livestock increased, they would be pleased and believe in Islam and the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family)! However, if they fell ill, their wives gave birth to daughters, and their wealth began to diminish, satanic whispers would fill their hearts, telling them that all these misfortunes were because of this religion they had accepted, and they would turn away! [^2]

It is noteworthy that the Quran uses the term "good" (khayr) regarding the world turning toward these individuals, but regarding the world turning away, it uses the term "trial" (fitnah—a means of testing) rather than "evil" (sharr), indicating that these unpleasant events are not inherent evils, but rather means for testing.

At the end of the verse, it adds: "And in this way, they have lost both this world and the Hereafter" (خَسِرَ الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةَ).

[^1]: The phrase "انْقَلَبَ عَلى‌ وَجْهِهِ‌" (he turns upon his face), considering that inqalaba here means to return or revert, may indicate that he completely turns his back on faith, not even looking back, as if he had always been a stranger to faith.

[^2]: Tafsir al-Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 23, p. 13; Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, p. 4409.

"And this is the clearest loss and ruin, that a person loses both his religion and his worldly life" (ذلِكَ هُوَ الْخُسْرانُ الْمُبِينُ‌).

In reality, such individuals viewed religion only through the lens of their material interests and considered the world's favor as the criterion and touchstone for its truth. This group—whose numbers are not few even in our age and time, and who exist in every society—possesses a faith tainted with polytheism and idolatry; however, their idols are their spouses, children, wealth, property, and livestock. It is obvious that such faith and belief are flimsier than a spider's web!

Of course, some commentators have considered this verse to refer to the hypocrites (munafiqin). If the intended meaning is a hypocrite in whose heart there is no faith at all, this contradicts the apparent meaning of the verse, because the phrases "worships Allah," "reassured by it," and "turns upon his face" indicate that he previously possessed a weak faith. But if the intended meaning is hypocrites who have a very small portion of faith, this does not contradict what we have said and is acceptable.

The next verse refers to the polytheistic beliefs of this group, especially after deviating from monotheism and faith in God, saying: "He calls upon besides Allah that which can neither harm him nor benefit him" (يَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ ما لا يَضُرُّهُ وَ ما لا يَنْفَعُهُ‌). If he truly seeks material benefits and flees from harm—and for this reason takes the world's favor or disfavor as the criterion for the truth of religion—then why does he go toward idols from which there is neither hope of benefit nor fear of harm? Useless beings that lack any effect on human destiny?! Indeed, "that is the far-reaching straying" (ذلِكَ هُوَ الضَّلالُ الْبَعِيدُ). Its distance from the Straight Path is so great that the hope of their return to the truth is very slim.

It takes the matter even further, saying: "He calls upon him whose harm is closer than his benefit!" (يَدْعُوا لَمَنْ ضَرُّهُ أَقْرَبُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِ‌).

This is because these fabricated deities lead their thoughts toward degradation, baseness, and superstitions in this world, and in the Hereafter, they bring them the scorching fire. Rather, as we read in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 98: "These idols themselves are the fuel and firewood of Hell!" (إِنَّكُمْ وَ ما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ‌).

At the end of the verse, it adds: "What a wretched patron and helper are these idols, and what a wretched companion and associate" (لَبِئْسَ الْمَوْلى‌ وَ لَبِئْسَ الْعَشِيرُ).

Here a question arises: the previous verse denies any harm or benefit from the idols, but this verse says their harm is closer than their benefit. Are these two consistent with each other?

In response, it must be said: it is common in discourse that sometimes at one stage a being is considered useless, and then moving further, it is considered a source of harm. It is just like saying: "Do not associate with so-and-so, for he is of no use to your religion or your world," and then we go further and say, "rather, he is the cause of your misery and disgrace." Furthermore, the harm that was denied is the harm to their enemies, as they are unable to strike any harm against their opponents; but the harm that is affirmed is an inevitable harm that afflicts their worshippers.

Additionally, the superlative form (af'al al-tafdil, the word aqrab—closer), as we have mentioned elsewhere, does not necessarily imply the existence of the quality in both sides of the comparison. Often the weaker side lacks it entirely. For example, we say: "One hour of patience and restraint against sin is better than the fire of Hell" (this never means that the fire of Hell is good but patience is better; rather, the fire of Hell lacks any goodness whatsoever).

This interpretation has been chosen by a group of great commentators such as Sheikh Tusi in Al-Tibyan and Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan.

Meanwhile, others like Fakhr al-Razi have also suggested the possibility that each of these two verses refers to a different group of idols. The first verse speaks of inanimate stone and wooden idols, and the second verse refers to tyrants (taghuts) and idol-like humans. The first group has neither benefit nor harm, being completely useless; but the second group, namely the "leaders of misguidance," do cause harm and there is no good in them, and assuming they had a little good, their harm is far greater (they take the phrase "What a wretched patron and what a wretched companion" as evidence for this meaning). In this way, no contradiction remains. [^1]

Since the method of the Quran is to present good and bad matters in comparison with each other so that the conclusion is more complete and clear, in the final verse under discussion, He says: "Indeed, Allah will admit those who believe and do righteous deeds into gardens of Paradise beneath which rivers flow" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ). Their program is clear, their line of thought and action is defined, their Patron is Allah, and their companions and associates will be the Prophets, the martyrs, the righteous, and the angels.

Indeed, "Allah does whatever He wills" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ ما يُرِيدُ). These great rewards are easy for Him, just as the punishment of the stubborn polytheists and their misleading leaders is simple for Him.

In this comparison, in reality, the group whose faith resides only on their tongues stands on the edge of religion and is diverted by a slight whisper, possessing no righteous deeds. But the righteous believers are in the core of Islam, and the fiercest storms do not shake them; the tree of their faith is deep-rooted, and the fruit of righteous deeds is visible on its branches. This is on one hand.

On the other hand, the deities of the first group are useless, or rather, their harm is greater; but the Patron and Guardian of the second group has power over all things and has provided the highest blessings for them.

[^1]: Some commentators, such as the esteemed author of Al-Mizan, have taken the word "yad'u" (calls upon) to mean "yaqul" (says), which is far from the apparent meaning of the verse.

Ayahs

مَن كَانَ يَظُنُّ أَن لَّن يَنصُرَهُ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ بِسَبَبٍ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ثُمَّ لْيَقْطَعْ فَلْيَنظُرْ هَلْ يُذْهِبَنَّ كَيْدُهُۥ مَا يَغِيظُ (١٥)
Whoever thinks that Allah will not help him in this world and the Hereafter, let him stretch a rope to the ceiling and hang himself, and see if his artifice would remove his rage. (15)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يُرِيدُ (١٦)
Thus have We sent it down as manifest signs, and indeed Allah guides whomever He desires. (16)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـِٔينَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ وَٱلْمَجُوسَ وَٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (١٧)
Indeed Allah will indeed judge between the faithful, the Jews, the Sabaeans, the Christians, the Magians and the polytheists on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed Allah is witness to all things. (17)

Circumstances of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated the following regarding the circumstances of the revelation of the first of the aforementioned verses:

A group from the tribes of "Bani Asad" and "Bani Ghatafan," who had entered into a covenant with the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), said: "We fear that Allah may not ultimately grant victory to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله), resulting in our ties being severed with our allies among the Jews, who might then refuse to provide us with food supplies." Consequently, the above verse was revealed as a warning to them and a severe condemnation of their stance.

Others have stated that a group of Muslims, due to their intense anger toward the disbelievers, were becoming restless and impatient for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to prevail, asking: "Why is Allah’s promise in this regard not being fulfilled?" The verse was revealed to reproach them for this lack of patience. [^1]

Commentary

Resurrection, the End of All Disputes

Since the preceding verses spoke of the group with weak faith, the verses under discussion depict another facet of them, stating: "Whoever thinks that Allah will not aid His Prophet in this world and the Hereafter, and is consumed by rage, let him do whatever is in his power; let him stretch a rope to the ceiling of his house and hang himself, cutting off his breath and proceeding to the brink of death, to see if this act will quench his rage" (مَنْ كانَ يَظُنُّ أَنْ لَنْ يَنْصُرَهُ اللَّهُ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ بِسَبَبٍ إِلَى السَّماءِ ثُمَّ لْيَقْطَعْ فَلْيَنْظُرْ هَلْ يُذْهِبَنَّ كَيْدُهُ ما يَغِيظُ).

[^1]: Abu al-Futuh Razi and also Fakhr Razi, under the verses being discussed.

A large group of commentators has chosen this interpretation or mentioned it as a significant possibility. [^1] According to this interpretation, the pronoun in "لَنْ يَنْصُرَهُ اللَّهُ" (will not aid him) refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and "سماء" (heaven/sky) refers to the ceiling of the house (as "sama" is applied to anything situated above). The phrase "ليقطع" (let him cut) refers to suffocation, the cutting off of breath, and reaching the point of death.

Various other possibilities have also been suggested for the interpretation of this verse, which need not all be mentioned, but two among them are noteworthy:

  1. The meaning of "سماء" is the literal "sky," meaning: "Those who imagine Allah will not aid His Prophet should go to the sky, hang a rope from it, and hang themselves between heaven and earth until their breath is cut (or cut the rope they are hanging from so they fall), and see if this act quenches their rage!"

  2. The aforementioned pronoun refers to these individuals themselves (not the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله), meaning: "Those who think Allah will not help them, and that their sustenance will be cut off because of their faith, let them do whatever they can; let them go to the sky and hang themselves with a rope, then cut this rope to fall—will these actions quench their rage?"

The point of interest is that all these interpretations point to a psychological trait regarding impatient, angry, and weak-faithed individuals who, when their affairs seemingly reach a dead end, immediately panic and make irrational decisions:

[^1]: Refer to the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan, al-Tibyan, al-Mizan, Fakhr Razi, Abu al-Futuh Razi, Tafsir al-Safi, and Qurtubi under the verse being discussed.

Sometimes they strike their fists against doors and walls. Sometimes they wish to split the earth and hide beneath it. And finally, to extinguish the fire of their rage, they decide on suicide, whereas none of these irrational acts solve their problem. If they were a bit calm, exercised patience, and rose to fight difficulties with the power of faith in Allah, self-confidence, and steadfastness, a solution would certainly be possible.

The next verse points to a summary of the preceding verses, saying: "Thus We have sent it [the Quran] down as clear signs and manifest proofs" (وَ كَذلِكَ أَنْزَلْناهُ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ). Arguments for the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment, such as the study of the human embryonic stages, the growth of plants, and the reviving of dead lands—which familiarize everyone with the issue of the Resurrection—as well as arguments like the uselessness of idols and the ultimate fate of those who use religion as a means to gain material benefits.

However, despite all this, having clear proofs alone is not enough; rather, the readiness to accept the truth is also necessary. For this reason, at the end of the verse, it says: "And Allah guides whom He wills" (وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يُرِيدُ). We have said many times that Allah’s will is not arbitrary; He is All-Wise, and all His actions are based on wisdom. Whoever strives in His path and sincerely desires guidance from the depths of their soul, Allah guides them through His clear signs. [^1]

[^1]: Regarding the syntax of the phrase "أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يُرِيدُ", the common view is that it has a hidden subject (mubtada), and the estimation is: "The matter is that Allah guides whom He wills." There is also the possibility that "an" (with fatha) is used in the sense of "inna" (with kasra), and there is no hidden element.

The last verse under discussion refers to six groups of followers of different religions—one group being the Muslims and believers, and five non-Muslim groups—stating: "Indeed, those who have believed, and those who were Jews, and the Sabeans, and the Christians, and the Magians, and those who associated others with Allah—Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection and separate truth from falsehood" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا وَ الصَّابِئِينَ وَ النَّصارى‌ وَ الْمَجُوسَ وَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ). Is it not that one of the names of the Resurrection is Yawm al-Fasl (the Day of Sorting), the day truth is separated from falsehood, Yawm al-Buruz (the Day of Manifestation) when hidden things are revealed, and the day disputes come to an end? Yes, Allah will end all these disputes on that day because "He is Witness over all things" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيدٌ).

Key Points

1- The Connection Between the Verses

The connection of this verse with the preceding ones is that the previous verse spoke of divine guidance in ready hearts. However, since not all hearts are ready, and prejudices, stubbornness, and blind imitation are strong barriers against accepting guidance, it states that these groupings and disputes among some will remain until the threshold of the Resurrection, and only on that day will all secrets be revealed and disputes be settled.

Furthermore, the previous verses spoke of three groups: those who argue about Allah and the Resurrection without proof, those whose work is seduction, and those who are weak in faith, tossed this way and that. The verse under discussion points to examples of these groups who stand against the believers.

Beyond all this, the discussion about the Resurrection in the previous verses raises the question: what is the purpose of the Resurrection? In the verse under discussion, one of its purposes—the ending of disputes and the return to unity—is stated.

2- Who are the Magians (Majus)?

The word "Majus" is mentioned only once in the Holy Quran in these verses. Given that they are placed in contrast to the polytheists and in the line of heavenly religions, it appears they possessed a religion, a book, and a prophet.

There is no doubt that today "Majus" refers to the followers of Zoroaster, or at least Zoroastrians constitute a major part of them. However, the history of Zoroaster himself is by no means clear, to the extent that some place his appearance in the 11th century BCE and others in the 6th or 7th century! [^1] This strange difference—five centuries!—shows how dark and ambiguous the history of Zoroaster is.

It is well known that he had a book called the "Avesta," which was destroyed during Alexander's conquest of Persia and later rewritten during the time of some Sassanid kings. [^2]

[^1]: A'lam al-Quran, page 550. [^2]: Tafsir al-Mizan, vol. 14, page 392.

We do not have much information regarding their beliefs, but what is most famous today is the belief in two principles of good and evil, or light and darkness. Thus, they consider the god of goodness and light to be "Ahura Mazda" and the god of evil and darkness to be "Ahriman." They hold the four elements, especially "fire," in great respect, to the extent that they are called "fire-worshippers," and wherever they are, a small or large fire temple exists.

Some consider the word "Majus" to be derived from "Magh," which was the title given to the leaders and clergy of this religion, and "Mobad," which is now used for their clergy, was originally from the root "Maghoud."

In Islamic narrations, they are considered followers of one of the true prophets (who later deviated from the principle of monotheism and turned toward polytheistic thoughts and beliefs).

In some narrations, we read that the polytheists of Mecca asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to take jizya from them and allow them to practice idol worship! The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied: "I do not take jizya except from the People of the Book." They wrote back: "How can you say this when you have taken jizya from the Magians of the region of Hajar?!" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied: "The Magians had a prophet and a heavenly book; they killed their prophet and burned his book!" [^1]

In another tradition from Asbagh ibn Nubata, we read that Ali (عليه السلام) said from the pulpit: "Ask me before you lose me." Ash'ath ibn Qays (the famous hypocrite) stood up and said: "O Commander of the Faithful, how is it that...

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, vol. 11, Chapters on Jihad against the Enemy, Chapter 49, page 96.

...jizya is taken from the Magians when no heavenly book was revealed to them and they had no prophet?" Ali (عليه السلام) replied: "Yes, O Ash'ath, Allah revealed a book to them and sent a prophet to them." [^1] And in a tradition from Imam Sajjad Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام), we read that the Prophet said: "Treat them according to the tradition of the People of the Book," meaning the Magians. [^2] Additionally, it should be noted that the word "Majus" is plural, and its singular is "Majusi."

3- Who are the Sabeans?

From the above verse, it is generally understood that they were also followers of some heavenly religion, especially since they are placed between the Jews and Christians in the verse. Some consider them followers of "John the Baptist" (Yahya ibn Zakariyya), and some believe they took some beliefs from the Jews and some from the Christians and mixed them, thus considering their religion a middle ground between the two.

The "Sabeans" place great importance on running water, and therefore many of them live near large rivers. It is said they also respect some stars, and for this reason, they have been accused of "star-worship," although the apparent meaning of the verse is that they are not in the ranks of the polytheists. (For more explanation, refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 62 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

[^1] and [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, vol. 11, Chapters on Jihad against the Enemy, Chapter 49, page 96.

4- Groups Deviated from Monotheism

In the above verses, five groups of distorted religions are mentioned, and perhaps their order here is according to their deviation from the principle of monotheism. The Jews have the least deviation from monotheism compared to others, and the Sabeans, who are a middle group between Jews and Christians, are in the second stage.

Then the Christians, by accepting the Trinity and three gods, have deviated further, and the Magians, by dividing the entire universe into two parts of good and evil and believing in two principles throughout the world of existence, are in the fourth stage. As for the polytheists and idol-worshippers, who suffer from the greatest deviation, they are mentioned last.

Ayah

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَسْجُدُ لَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ وَٱلنُّجُومُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ وَٱلشَّجَرُ وَٱلدَّوَآبُّ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۗ وَمَن يُهِنِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن مُّكْرِمٍ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۩ (١٨)
Have you not regarded that whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth prostrates to Allah, as well as the sun, the moon, and the stars, the mountains, the trees, and the animals and many humans? And many have come to deserve the punishment. Whomever Allah humiliates will find no one who may bring him honour. Indeed Allah does whatever He wishes. (18)

Commentary

All Beings in the Universe Prostrate Before Him

Since the preceding verses discussed the Origin and the Resurrection, the verse under discussion completes the cycle of the Origin and the Resurrection by presenting the issue of Monotheism and the knowledge of God. Addressing the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), it says: "Do you not see that to Allah prostrates whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth, and the sun, the moon, the stars, the mountains, the trees, and the moving creatures"?! (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَسْجُدُ لَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ الشَّمْسُ وَ الْقَمَرُ وَ النُّجُومُ وَ الْجِبالُ وَ الشَّجَرُ وَ الدَّوَابُ‌).

"And [also] many of the people [prostrate to Him], while many others have deserved the punishment" (وَ كَثِيرٌ مِنَ النَّاسِ وَ كَثِيرٌ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ الْعَذابُ‌).

Then it adds: "These are worthless before the Lord, and whomever Allah humiliates, there is no one to honor him or include him in salvation and reward" (وَ مَنْ يُهِنِ اللَّهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ مُكْرِمٍ‌).

Indeed, "Allah does whatever He wills" and deems wise (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ ما يَشاءُ); He honors the believers and humbles the deniers.

Key Points

1- How is this Universal Prostration?

In various verses of the Holy Quran, mention is made of the universal "prostration" (sujud) of the beings of the world, as well as their "glorification" (tasbih), "praise" (hamd), and "prayer" (salat). It is emphasized that these four types of worship are not exclusive to humans; rather, even seemingly inanimate objects participate in them.

Although we have discussed the universal praise and glorification of the world's beings in detail under the commentary of Surah Al-Isra, verse 44 (in Volume 12), and similarly spoke of the universal prostration of the world's beings in Volume 10 under Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 15, it is necessary to mention this important issue here once again:

Considering what is stated in the verse under discussion, the beings of the world possess two types of prostration: "Generative Prostration" (Sujud Takwini) and "Legislative Prostration" (Sujud Tashri'i).

Their unconditional submission and humility before the Divine Will, the laws of creation, and the governing system of this world constitute their Generative Prostration, which encompasses every particle of existence. Even the brain cells of Pharaohs, Nimrods, and stubborn deniers—every particle of their being—is subject to this Generative Prostration.

According to a group of researchers, all particles in the universe possess a form of perception and consciousness, and parallel to that, in their own realm, they praise and glorify Allah and perform prostration and prayer (the explanation of this was provided under Surah Al-Isra, verse 44). Even if we do not accept this type of perception and consciousness, their submission and humility before all the systems of existence is by no means deniable.

However, "Legislative Prostration" is that ultimate humility realized by possessors of intellect, consciousness, perception, and knowledge before the Lord.

Here, the question arises: if the program of universal prostration includes all humans, why is it restricted to a specific group of humans in the above verse?

Considering that "prostration" in this verse is used in a comprehensive sense encompassing both "legislation" and "generation," the answer becomes clear. For the sun, moon, stars, mountains, trees, and moving creatures, the generative aspect is intended. But regarding humans, it is legislative, which many perform while a group refuses, thus becoming instances of "many who have deserved the punishment" (كَثِيرٌ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ الْعَذابُ‌). We know that using a single word in a comprehensive and general sense while maintaining different applications is not problematic, even for those who do not permit using a word in more than one meaning, let alone for us who consider using a shared term in multiple meanings permissible (reflect on this).

2- Is the Prostration of Angels Legislative?

Undoubtedly, the phrase "whoever is in the heavens" (مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ‌) includes the angels. But is their prostration generative or legislative?

Given that they possess intellect, consciousness, knowledge, and will, their prostration is legislative in nature; that is, it is an act of worship and humility performed with will and choice. Regarding the angels, the Quran says: "They do not disobey Allah in what He commands them but do what they are commanded" (لا يَعْصُونَ اللَّهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَ يَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ‌) (At-Tahrim: 6).

3- Answers to Several Questions

1- Given that "whoever is on the earth" (مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌) includes all humans, why is the phrase "and many of the people" (كَثِيرٌ مِنَ النَّاسِ‌) mentioned afterward?

In response, it can be said that this phrase is actually an explanation for "whoever is on the earth"; meaning the inhabitants of the earth are of two groups: a group that is believing and humble before Allah, and a group that is disbelieving, rebellious, and defiant.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that the expression "whoever is on the earth," which has a general aspect, refers to generative prostration in which all humans—even disbelievers—participate through every part of their being. However, the phrase "and many of the people..." refers to legislative prostration, in which they differ.

There is also the possibility that "whoever is on the earth" refers to the angels inhabiting the earth, just as "whoever is in the heavens" refers to the angels inhabiting the heavens, while the subsequent phrase speaks of the humans living on earth.

2- Why does this verse speak of the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth, rather than the heavens and the earth themselves?

In response, we say that the heavens are the stars themselves, which are gathered in the word "stars" (nujum), and the mention of "mountains" (jibal), which form a significant part of the globe, refers to the earth itself.

3- Finally, the last question is: why does it say "Do you not see" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ), even though the universal prostration of beings is not something seen with the eyes?

Considering that "vision" (ru'yah) in the Arabic language sometimes means "knowledge," the answer to this question becomes clear. Furthermore, we sometimes use the term "observation" for very obvious matters; for example, one might say, "Do you not see that so-and-so is envious and stingy?" or "Do you not see that so-and-so is learned and just?" (even though these qualities are not sensory). The intention is certain perception.

Ayahs

۞ هَـٰذَانِ خَصْمَانِ ٱخْتَصَمُوا۟ فِى رَبِّهِمْ ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ قُطِّعَتْ لَهُمْ ثِيَابٌ مِّن نَّارٍ يُصَبُّ مِن فَوْقِ رُءُوسِهِمُ ٱلْحَمِيمُ (١٩)
These two contending groups contend concerning their Lord. As for those who are faithless, cloaks of fire will be cut out for them, and boiling water will be poured over their heads, (19)
يُصْهَرُ بِهِۦ مَا فِى بُطُونِهِمْ وَٱلْجُلُودُ (٢٠)
with which their skins and entrails will fuse, (20)
وَلَهُم مَّقَـٰمِعُ مِنْ حَدِيدٍ (٢١)
and there will be clubs of iron for them. (21)
كُلَّمَآ أَرَادُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا مِنْ غَمٍّ أُعِيدُوا۟ فِيهَا وَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (٢٢)
Whenever they desire to leave it out of anguish, they will be turned back into it [and told]: ‘Taste the punishment of the burning!’ (22)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيهَا مِنْ أَسَاوِرَ مِن ذَهَبٍ وَلُؤْلُؤًا ۖ وَلِبَاسُهُمْ فِيهَا حَرِيرٌ (٢٣)
Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them, adorned therein with bracelets of gold and pearl, and their dress therein will be silk. (23)
وَهُدُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيِّبِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَهُدُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ (٢٤)
They were guided to chaste belief, and guided to the path of the All-laudable. (24)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of Shi'a and Sunni commentators have narrated an occasion of revelation for the first of the above verses, the summary of which is as follows: "On the day of the Battle of Badr, three men from the Muslims (Ali عليه السلام, Hamzah, and Ubaydah ibn al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib) entered the battlefield and respectively struck down 'Walid ibn Utbah', 'Utbah ibn Rabi'ah', and 'Shaybah ibn Rabi'ah'. The above verse was revealed and described the fate of these combatants."

It is also narrated that "Abu Dharr" used to swear that this verse was revealed concerning the aforementioned men [^1], but as we have stated many times, the existence of a specific occasion of revelation never prevents the generality of the verse's concept.

Commentary

Two Contending Groups Facing Each Other!

In the previous verses, reference was made to the group of believers and various sects of disbelievers, specifically identifying them as six groups. Here, He says: "These two parties—the believers and the non-believers—contended and disputed concerning their Lord" (هذانِ خَصْمانِ اخْتَصَمُوا فِي رَبِّهِمْ) [^1]. The five sects of disbelievers on one side and the true believers on the other. If we look closely, we see that the basis of the differences among all religions traces back to disagreements regarding the Essence and Attributes of Allah, and its result extends to the issues of Prophethood and the Resurrection. Therefore, there is no need for us to assume the word "religion" is implied here and say their contention is "in the religion of their Lord"; rather, the root of the differences truly returns to the disagreement over Monotheism (Tawhid). Fundamentally, all distorted and false religions are afflicted with some form of polytheism (Shirk), the effects of which appear in all their beliefs.

Then, He describes four types of punishment for the disbelievers who knowingly and consciously denied the truth:

First, He begins with their clothing, saying: "Those who disbelieve, garments of fire will be cut out for them" (فَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا قُطِّعَتْ لَهُمْ ثِيابٌ مِنْ نارٍ).

This sentence may refer to the fact that pieces of fire will actually be cut and sewn into the shape of garments for them! Or it may be a metaphor for the fact that the fire of Hell surrounds them from every side like clothing.

Another is that a scalding and boiling liquid, the "Hamim of Hell," will be poured over their heads (يُصَبُّ مِنْ فَوْقِ رُؤُسِهِمُ الْحَمِيمُ) [^2].

[^1]: This occasion of revelation has been cited by Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan," Fakhr Razi in "Tafsir al-Kabir," Alusi in "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Suyuti in "Asbab al-Nuzul," and Qurtubi in his Tafsir.

[^1]: The word "Khasman" (two adversaries) is dual, while "Ikhtasamu" (they contended), which is its verb, appears in the plural form. This is because these two adversaries are not two individuals, but two groups. Furthermore, these two groups do not stand in just two ranks; rather, they are in various ranks, and each group rises to contend with the others.

[^2]: "Hamim" is hot and scalding water.

However, this scalding and boiling water penetrates their bodies in such a way that "it melts what is in their bellies as well as their skins"! (يُصْهَرُ بِهِ ما فِي بُطُونِهِمْ وَ الْجُلُودُ) [^1]

Thirdly, "for them are maces or whips of burning iron" (وَ لَهُمْ مَقامِعُ مِنْ حَدِيدٍ) [^2].

"And whenever they desire to get out of Hell and its grief and anguish, they are driven back into it, and it is said to them: Taste the punishment of the Burning!" (كُلَّما أَرادُوا أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها مِنْ غَمٍّ أُعِيدُوا فِيها وَ ذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ). And this is their fourth punishment.

In the subsequent verses, using the "method of contrast," He describes the state of the righteous believers so that through comparison, the situation of both groups becomes completely clear. Here, He recounts five types of their rewards:

First, He says: "Indeed, Allah will admit those who believe and do righteous deeds into gardens of Paradise beneath which rivers flow" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

The first group is submerged in flames of burning fire, while these are resting in the gardens of Paradise beside flowing rivers.

Then, addressing their adornment and clothing, He says: "They will be adorned therein with bracelets of gold and pearls, and their garments therein will be silk" (يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيها مِنْ أَساوِرَ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ وَ لُؤْلُؤاً وَ لِباسُهُمْ فِيها حَرِيرٌ) [^1]. These are two more of their rewards.

In this way, in Paradise, they wear the most beautiful clothes of which they were deprived in this world, and they have jewel-encrusted bracelets on their hands.
