If they were forbidden from wearing such clothes and ornaments in this world, it was because they were a source of pride and negligence and caused the deprivation of others; but there, where these issues do not exist, these prohibitions are lifted and compensated.

Of course, considering that the alphabet of life in that world is different from this one, these words will have concepts far superior and higher than what we imagine in this world (take note).

Finally, the fourth and fifth blessings that Allah bestows upon them, which are purely spiritual in nature, are that they "are guided to pure speech" (وَ هُدُوا إِلَى الطَّيِّبِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ).

[^1]: "Yusharu" is from the root "Sahr" (rhyming with Qahr), meaning to melt fat, and "Sihr" (rhyming with Fikr) means a son-in-law.

[^2]: "Maqami'" is the plural of "Miqma'ah" (rhyming with Minbar), meaning a whip or an iron pillar used to strike someone for prevention or punishment.

[^1]: "Asawir" is the plural of "Aswirah" (rhyming with Mashwarah), which in turn is the plural of "Siwar" (rhyming with Kitab), meaning a bracelet. It was originally Persian (Dastvar), which later changed into the above form in Arabic.

Soul-nourishing speech, joy-inducing phrases and words, and terms full of purity and spirituality that move the soul through the stages of perfection and soothe and nurture the human heart and soul.

And likewise, "they are guided to the path of the Praiseworthy Lord" (وَ هُدُوا إِلى‌ صِراطِ الْحَمِيدِ) [^1]. The path of knowing Allah and spiritual and soulful proximity to His presence—the path of love and gnosis ('Irfan).

Yes, Allah leads the believers to the highest degree of spiritual pleasures by guiding them toward these meanings.

In a tradition narrated by the famous commentator "Ali ibn Ibrahim" in his Tafsir, we read that "pure speech" (Tayyib min al-Qawl) refers to Monotheism (Tawhid) and sincerity (Ikhlas), and "the path of the Praiseworthy" (Sirat al-Hamid) refers to Wilayah and the acceptance of the leadership of divine leaders (of course, this is one of the clear applications of the verse).

Furthermore, from the various expressions used in the above verses as well as in their occasion of revelation, it appears that those severe and heavy punishments are for a specific group of disbelievers who rise to contend regarding their Lord and strive to mislead others. They are the leaders of disbelief, such as those who rose to fight Ali عليه السلام, Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib, and Ubaydah ibn al-Harith on the battlefield of Badr.

[^1]: The word "Hamid" means "Mahmud" (the Praised One), referring to one who is worthy of praise, and here it refers to Allah. Therefore, "Sirat al-Hamid" means the path to reaching the station of proximity and the pleasure of the Lord. Some have also suggested that "Hamid" here serves as a descriptive adjective for "Sirat" (path), making it an "explanatory addition" (Idafah Bayaniyyah). Thus, the meaning would be: they are guided to a path that is worthy of all praise (Alusi in Ruh al-Bayan). However, the first meaning seems more correct.

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلْنَـٰهُ لِلنَّاسِ سَوَآءً ٱلْعَـٰكِفُ فِيهِ وَٱلْبَادِ ۚ وَمَن يُرِدْ فِيهِ بِإِلْحَادٍۭ بِظُلْمٍ نُّذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢٥)
Indeed those who are faithless and bar from the way of Allah and the Sacred Mosque, which We have made for all people, the native and the visitor being equal therein—whoever wrongfully tries to commit violation in it, We shall make him taste a painful punishment. (25)

Commentary

The Preventers of Allah’s House!

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the disbelievers in general; however, the verse under discussion refers to a specific group among them who are guilty of grave violations and sins, particularly concerning "al-Masjid al-Haram" and the "magnificent rites of Hajj."

First, It says: "Indeed, those who disbelieve and avert [people] from the way of Allah..." (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌).

"And [they also avert] the people of faith from the great center of monotheism, al-Masjid al-Haram, which We have made equal for all people—whether those who dwell therein or those who come from afar—they deserve a painful punishment" (وَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ الَّذِي جَعَلْناهُ لِلنَّاسِ سَواءً الْعاكِفُ فِيهِ وَ الْبادِ).

"And whoever intends therein any deviation [from the truth] through wrongdoing—We will make him taste of a painful punishment" (وَ مَنْ يُرِدْ فِيهِ بِإِلْحادٍ بِظُلْمٍ نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ‌).

In reality, this group of disbelievers, in addition to denying the truth, has committed three major crimes:

  1. Preventing people from the way of Allah, faith, and obedience to Him.
  2. Preventing worshippers and pilgrims of the House of Allah and claiming a special privilege for themselves.
  3. Committing oppression, sin, and deviation (atheism/heresy) in this sacred land.

Allah punishes this group, who are deserving of a painful torment.

Key Points

  1. In this verse, the "disbelief" of this group is mentioned in the "past tense" (كَفَرُوا), while "averting from the way of Allah" is mentioned in the "present/continuous tense" (يَصُدُّونَ). This indicates that their disbelief is long-standing, whereas their efforts to lead people astray are constant and continuous. In other words, the first phrase speaks of their false belief, which is a fixed state, and the second phrase speaks of their action, which is the repeated "averting from the way of Allah."

[What is meant by "Averting from the way of Allah"?]

  1. The meaning of "averting from the way of Allah" (صد عن سبيل اللَّه) is any kind of effort or struggle to prevent people from faith and righteous deeds. All propaganda and practical programs aimed at destroying beliefs and diverting people from correct paths and pure actions are included in this broad concept.

All People are Equal in this Center of Worship

Regarding the interpretation of the phrase "equal for the dweller therein and the visitor" (سَواءً الْعاكِفُ فِيهِ وَ الْبادِ), commentators have offered various explanations: Some have said it means that all people are equal in the rites of worship in this center of monotheism, and no one has the right to disturb another in the matter of Hajj and worship near the House of Allah.

Meanwhile, others have adopted a broader meaning for this phrase, stating that people are not only equal in the rites of worship but also in utilizing the land and the houses around Mecca for rest and their other needs. For this reason, a group of jurists (Fuqaha) has prohibited the buying, selling, and renting of houses in Mecca, citing the above verse as evidence.

Islamic narrations also emphasize that pilgrims of the House of Allah should not be prevented from residing in the houses and dwellings of Mecca; some of these narrations imply a prohibition (tahrim) and others imply it is disliked (karahah).

In Nahj al-Balagha, in a letter written by Ali عليه السلام to Qutham ibn Abbas, the governor of Mecca, we read:

"Command the people of Mecca not to take rent from those who stay there, for Allah, the Sublime, says: 'Equal for the dweller therein and the visitor.' The 'dweller' (al-akif) is the one residing there, and the 'visitor' (al-badi) is the one who comes for Hajj from elsewhere." [^1]

In another tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this same verse, we read:

"In the beginning, the houses of Mecca had no doors. The first person to hang two door-leaves on his door was Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan. It is not proper for anyone to prevent the pilgrims from anything regarding the houses and their dwellings."

It is also understood from some narrations that the pilgrims of the House of Allah have the right to use the courtyards of the houses until the completion of the Hajj rituals.

Of course, this ruling is largely related to the upcoming discussion of whether "al-Masjid al-Haram" in this verse refers specifically to the mosque itself or includes the entirety of Mecca. If we accept the first view, it would not apply to the houses of Mecca. However, if we consider all of Mecca to be included in the concept of the verse, the prohibition of buying, selling, or renting houses in Mecca to pilgrims becomes a topic of discussion. Since this matter is not definitive according to jurisprudential sources, narrations, and interpretation, ruling it as prohibited (haram) is difficult. Nevertheless, without a doubt, it is fitting for the people of Mecca to provide every facility for the pilgrims of the House of Allah and not to claim any priority or privilege for themselves over them, even regarding housing. The narrations from Nahj al-Balagha and similar sources apparently point to this.

In any case, the view of prohibition does not have many supporters among Shia and Sunni jurists (for more explanation, refer to Volume 20 of Jawahir al-Kalam, page 48 onwards, regarding the rulings of Mina).

It is also certain that no one has the right, as a trustee of the House of Allah or under any other title, to cause the slightest disturbance to a pilgrim of this House, or to turn it into an exclusive base for their own propaganda and programs.

What is meant by al-Masjid al-Haram in this verse?

Some have said it means its literal sense, i.e., the House of the Kaaba and the entire Sacred Mosque. However, others consider it a reference to "all of Mecca," citing the first verse of Surah al-Isra regarding the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) Ascension (Mi'raj) as evidence. That verse explicitly states that the start of the Ascension was from al-Masjid al-Haram, while history says it was from the "house of Khadija," "Shi'b Abi Talib," or the "house of Umm Hani." Thus, it is understood that al-Masjid al-Haram refers to all of Mecca. [^1]

However, since it is not certain that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) Ascension began from outside al-Masjid al-Haram—and it is possible it took place from the mosque itself—we have no reason to divert the verse under discussion from its literal meaning. Therefore, the subject of discussion in this verse is al-Masjid al-Haram itself.

If we read in the aforementioned narrations that this verse was used to argue for the equality of people in the houses of Mecca, it is because the said ruling is apparently a recommended (mustahabb) one. In a recommended ruling, expanding the subject based on relevant associations is permissible (reflect on this).

What is "Deviation through Wrongdoing" (Ilhad bi-Zulm)?

"Ilhad" (الحاد) literally means deviation from the limit of moderation. A "Lahd" (niche in a grave) is called such because it is a hole on the side of the grave, outside its center.

Therefore, the above phrase refers to those who, by resorting to wrongdoing, deviate from the limit of moderation and commit offenses in that land. However, some have interpreted "wrongdoing" (zulm) here exclusively as "polytheism" (shirk), and some as deeming the forbidden (haram) as permissible.

Meanwhile, other commentators take it in the broad sense of the word, meaning any kind of sin and commission of the forbidden—even insulting or speaking ill of subordinates is included in its concept. They say that the punishment for committing any sin in that sacred land is more severe and heavier.

In a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that one of his companions asked about the interpretation of this verse. The Imam said:

"Every wrongdoing a man does to himself in Mecca, whether theft, oppressing someone, or any kind of injustice, I consider it 'Ilhad' (and covered by this verse). For this reason, it was discouraged to take the Sanctuary (Haram) as a permanent residence (because sin in this land carries a heavier responsibility)." [^1]

Other narrations have been reported with the same meaning, and it is consistent with the general wording of the verse.

For this reason, some jurists have suggested that if someone commits a sin in the Sanctuary of Mecca for which a legal penalty (Hadd) is prescribed in Islam, they should receive an additional discretionary punishment (Ta'zir) and penalty beyond the Hadd, citing the phrase "We will make him taste of a painful punishment" (نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ‌). [^2]

From what we have said, it becomes clear that those who interpreted the above verse exclusively as a prohibition against hoarding (ihtikar) or entering the Sanctuary area without Ihram intended to state a clear example; otherwise, there is no reason to limit the broad concept of the verse.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 67. [^1]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 1, p. 335. [^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 482, under the verse. [^2]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 1, p. 335.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ بَوَّأْنَا لِإِبْرَٰهِيمَ مَكَانَ ٱلْبَيْتِ أَن لَّا تُشْرِكْ بِى شَيْـًٔا وَطَهِّرْ بَيْتِىَ لِلطَّآئِفِينَ وَٱلْقَآئِمِينَ وَٱلرُّكَّعِ ٱلسُّجُودِ (٢٦)
When We settled for Abraham the site of the House [saying], Do not ascribe any partners to Me, and purify My House for those who circle around it, and those who stand [in it for prayer], and those who bow and prostrate themselves. (26)
وَأَذِّن فِى ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَجِّ يَأْتُوكَ رِجَالًا وَعَلَىٰ كُلِّ ضَامِرٍ يَأْتِينَ مِن كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ (٢٧)
And proclaim the Hajj to all the people: they will come to you on foot and on lean camels, coming from distant places, (27)
لِّيَشْهَدُوا۟ مَنَـٰفِعَ لَهُمْ وَيَذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ مَّعْلُومَـٰتٍ عَلَىٰ مَا رَزَقَهُم مِّنۢ بَهِيمَةِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۖ فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا وَأَطْعِمُوا۟ ٱلْبَآئِسَ ٱلْفَقِيرَ (٢٨)
that they may witness the benefits for them, and mention Allah’s Name during the known days over the livestock He has provided them. So eat thereof and feed the destitute and the needy. (28)

Commentary

A General Invitation to Hajj!

In connection with the discussion in the previous verse regarding Masjid al-Haram and the pilgrims of the House of Allah, the verses under discussion first refer to the history of the construction of the Kaaba by the hand of Ibrahim al-Khalil عليه السلام, then to the obligation of Hajj and its philosophy, and a portion of the rulings of this great act of worship. In other words, the previous verse served as an introduction to the various discussions in these verses.

It begins with the story of the reconstruction of the Kaaba, saying: "And remember when We assigned for Ibrahim the site of the House, so that he might undertake the construction of the Kaaba in that place" (وَ إِذْ بَوَّأْنا لِإِبْراهِيمَ مَكانَ الْبَيْتِ‌).

The word "bawwa’a" (بوأ) is originally derived from the root "bawa’" (بواء), meaning the equality of the parts of a place and its flatness; subsequently, it has been applied to any form of preparing a location.

The meaning of this phrase in the above verse—according to the narrations of commentators—is that Allah showed Ibrahim عليه السلام the site of the House of Kaaba, which had been built in the time of Adam and whose remains had been destroyed and erased during the flood of Nuh. A wind blew and pushed back the dust, revealing the foundations of the House, or a piece of cloud came and cast a shadow there, or by any other means, Allah made the original location of the House known and prepared for Ibrahim, and he reconstructed it with the assistance of his son Ismail [^1].

[^1]: Regarding the details of the construction of the Kaaba, we have discussed it extensively under verse 127 of Surah Al-Baqarah (Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh) and also in Volume 3 under verse 96 of Surah Al-Imran.

Then it adds that when the House was ready, We addressed Ibrahim:

Make this House a center of monotheism, and "do not associate anything with Me, and purify My House for those who perform Tawaf, those who stand in prayer, and those who bow and prostrate" (أَنْ لا تُشْرِكْ بِي شَيْئاً وَ طَهِّرْ بَيْتِيَ لِلطَّائِفِينَ وَ الْقائِمِينَ وَ الرُّكَّعِ السُّجُودِ) [^1]. In reality, Ibrahim عليه السلام was commanded to keep the Kaaba and its surroundings pure and clean from every form of physical and spiritual pollution, and from every idol and manifestation of polytheism, so that the servants of Allah in this pure place would think of nothing but Allah, and perform the most important acts of worship of this land—which are Tawaf and prayer—in an environment purged of all contamination.

Among the pillars of prayer, the above verse refers to three major pillars: "standing" (qiyam), "bowing" (ruku'), and "prostration" (sujud) in order, as the rest fall under their shadow. Although a group of commentators have interpreted "al-qa'imin" (the standing) here to mean "those residing in Mecca," considering the issue of Tawaf, bowing, and prostration mentioned before and after it, there is no doubt that "standing" here means the standing in prayer. This meaning has been chosen or narrated as a commentary by many Shiite and Sunni commentators ^2. Additionally, it should be noted that "rukka'" is the plural of "raki'" (one who bows) and "sujud" is the plural of "sajid" (one who prostrates). The fact that no coordinating conjunction "and" (waw) appears between these two (al-rukka' al-sujud), but rather they are mentioned as a description, is due to the close proximity of these two states to one another.

[^1]: In the above verse, a phrase is implied which many commentators have pointed out, and that phrase is "We revealed" (awhayna). ^2: Tafsir al-Mizan, Tafsir Fi Zilal, Tafsir al-Tibyan, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, and Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi under the verse in question.

After the Kaaba was prepared for the worshippers, Allah commanded Ibrahim عليه السلام: "And proclaim the Hajj among the people; they will come to you on foot and on every lean camel, coming from every distant mountain highway" (وَ أَذِّنْ فِي النَّاسِ بِالْحَجِّ يَأْتُوكَ رِجالًا وَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ ضامِرٍ يَأْتِينَ مِنْ كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ‌).

"Adhin" (أذن) is from the root "adhan," meaning "proclamation." "Rijalan" (رجال) is the plural of "rajil," meaning "on foot." "Damir" (ضامر) means a lean animal. "Fajj" (فج) originally means the space between two mountains and was later applied to wide roads, and "amiq" (عميق) here means distant.

In a narration found in the Tafsir of "Ali ibn Ibrahim," we read: "When Ibrahim received this command, he said: 'O Lord, my voice will not reach the people.' But Allah said to him: 'Your duty is the proclamation, and Mine is the delivery (conveyance)!'" Ibrahim ascended the "Maqam," placed his fingers in his ears, turned toward the East and the West, and called out, saying: "O people! Hajj to the Ancient House has been prescribed for you, so respond to your Lord!"

And Allah made his voice reach everyone, even those in the loins of fathers and the wombs of mothers, and they replied: "Labbayk Allahumma Labbayk!" (Here I am, O Allah, here I am!)...

And all those who participate in the ceremonies from that day until the Day of Resurrection are among those who responded to Ibrahim's call on that day [^1].

[^1]: Summarized from the Tafsir of "Ali ibn Ibrahim" as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 488. "Alusi" in "Ruh al-Ma'ani" and Fakhr al-Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir have narrated similar accounts with slight variations under the verse in question.

The reason pilgrims on foot are mentioned before those riding is that their status before Allah is superior, as they endure more hardship during this journey. For this reason, we read in a narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "One who performs Hajj on foot receives seven hundred rewards for every step, while those riding receive seventy rewards for every step" [^1]. Alternatively, it may be to emphasize the importance of visiting the House of Allah—that they should come toward Him using any available means and not always wait for a riding mount.

The expression "damir" (lean animal) refers to the fact that this path is one that makes animals lean, as it passes through scorching, dry, and barren deserts; it is a warning to endure the hardships of this path.

Or it suggests that they should choose animals that are trained, agile, and highly resilient—animals that have become lean through exercise and have firm, strong muscles, as fattened animals are of no use on such paths (and humans raised in luxury and comfort are likewise not suited for this path).

The expression "from every distant mountain highway" (مِنْ كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ‌) indicates that they must move toward this destination not only from nearby paths but also from distant ones (the word "kull" [every] here does not imply exhaustive enumeration but rather signifies a great number).

The famous commentator "Abu al-Futuh al-Razi," under this verse, narrates an interesting story of a man named "Abu al-Qasim Bishr ibn Muhammad," who says: "While performing Tawaf, I saw an old man in a state of extreme weakness and frailty, with the marks of the journey's hardship visible on his face, performing Tawaf while leaning on a staff. I approached him and asked: 'Where do you come from?' He said: 'From a very distant path, and I have been traveling for five years to reach here! I have become old and weak from the hardship of the journey.' I said: 'By Allah, this is a great hardship, yet it is a beautiful obedience and a sincere love in the presence of the Truth.'"

He became happy upon hearing this, smiled at me, and recited these two verses: "Visit the one you love, even if your home is far, And even if veils and curtains stand in the way! Let not distance prevent you from visiting Him, For the lover is ever a visitor to the one he loves!" Yes, the attraction of the House of Allah is so great that it draws and absorbs hearts filled with faith from all points, near and far. Young and old, small and great, from every race and tribe, from near and far, they come lovingly, chanting "Labbayk," to witness the manifestations of Allah's Pure Essence in that holy land with the eye of the soul and to feel His unceasing mercy within their spirits [^1].

In the following verse, in a very condensed and meaningful phrase, the various philosophies of Hajj are addressed, stating: They should come to this holy land "that they may witness benefits for themselves" (لِيَشْهَدُوا مَنافِعَ لَهُمْ‌).

Commentators have said much regarding the interpretation of the word "benefits" (manafi') here, but it is perfectly clear that there is no restriction in this word. It encompasses all spiritual benefits and blessings, material results, individual and social gains, and political, economic, and moral philosophies.

Indeed, Muslims from all parts of the world and from all strata must turn there to witness and observe these benefits. What a beautiful expression! "To witness and observe"—that they may see with their eyes what they have heard with their ears!

Thus, in the book al-Kafi, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that "Rabi' ibn Khuthaym" asked the Imam for the interpretation of this word. The Imam replied: "It includes both the benefits of this world and the benefits of the Hereafter" [^1]. God willing, we will speak in detail about these various benefits in the "Key Points" of the verse. Then it adds: "And that they may come and sacrifice and mention the name of Allah on known days over the livestock He has provided for them (at the time of slaughter)" (وَ يَذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللَّهِ فِي أَيَّامٍ مَعْلُوماتٍ عَلى‌ ما رَزَقَهُمْ مِنْ بَهِيمَةِ الْأَنْعامِ‌).

Since the primary focus in the Hajj ceremonies is on aspects that connect with Allah and reflect the spirit of this great worship, the above verse mentions only the act of pronouncing Allah's name during the sacrifice, which is one of the conditions. This indicates that during the slaughter of the sacrifice, their entire attention is toward Allah and His acceptance, and the use of the meat is secondary to that.

Sacrificing animals is, in reality, a symbol of readiness to be sacrificed in the way of Allah, just as it appeared in the story of Ibrahim عليه السلام and Ismail عليه السلام and his sacrifice. By this act, they declare that they are ready for any sacrifice in His way, even the giving of their lives.

In any case, the Quran, with this statement, negates the polytheistic practice of the idolaters who would mention the names of idols during sacrifice, thereby polluting this monotheistic ceremony with polytheism.

At the end of the verse, it says: "Then eat of them and feed the miserable and the poor" (فَكُلُوا مِنْها وَ أَطْعِمُوا الْبائِسَ الْفَقِيرَ).

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that the meaning of mentioning Allah's name in the "known days" refers to the Takbir, praise, and glorification of Allah during these days for His endless blessings, especially for the livestock He has provided for humans, from all parts of whose bodies they benefit in their lives [^1].

Key Points

1- What are the "Known Days"?

In the above verses, Allah commands to remember Him during the "known days" (ayyam ma'lumat), and in verse 203 of Surah Al-Baqarah, this same command appears in another form: "And remember Allah during the numbered days" (ayyam ma'dudat).

There is a discussion among commentators as to what the "known days" are, and whether they are the same as the "numbered days" mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah or different. The narrations in this regard also vary:

[^1]: Al-Kafi as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 488. [^1]: According to the first interpretation (mentioning Allah's name at the time of slaughtering the sacrifice), "ala" (over/upon) signifies superiority/attachment, and according to the second interpretation (referring to general remembrance during these days), "ala" means "for/because of." There is a significant difference between these two interpretations, which will be noted in the points.

A group of commentators, following some Islamic narrations, believe that the "known days" refer to the first ten days of Dhu al-Hijjah, and the "numbered days" are the "Days of Tashriq," meaning the 11th, 12th, and 13th of Dhu al-Hijjah—days that are luminous and enlighten all hearts.

Meanwhile, another group, following other narrations, has said: Both refer to the Days of Tashriq. Sometimes they have taken the Days of Tashriq to be those same three days, and sometimes they have added the tenth day, i.e., Eid al-Adha, to them.

The phrase "But whoever hastens [his departure] in two days, there is no sin upon him" (فَمَنْ تَعَجَّلَ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ فَلا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ‌) which appears in Surah Al-Baqarah, shows that the Days of Tashriq are no more than three days, because hastening in them causes one day to be reduced, turning it into two days.

However, considering that in the verses under discussion, the issue of sacrifice follows the mention of "known days," and we know that sacrifice is usually performed on the tenth day, this supports the view that the "known days" are the first ten days of Dhu al-Hijjah, ending with the tenth day, the Day of Sacrifice. Thus, the first interpretation—that the concepts of "known days" and "numbered days" are distinct—is strengthened.

Yet, considering the unity of the expressions used in the two verses, it more readily comes to mind that both refer to the same thing: the goal in both is to focus on the remembrance of Allah and His name during specific days that begin on the 10th of Dhu al-Hijjah and end on the 13th.

Of course, one instance of mentioning Allah's name is mentioning His name at the time of sacrifice [^1].

[^1]: In this way, the contradiction between the two opinions in the interpretation of "And let them mention the name of Allah" is resolved—where some interpreted it as mentioning Allah's name at the time of slaughtering the sacrifice and others as mentioning Allah's name in general. In reality, the first interpretation is an instance of the second, and the second interpretation is a general and broad concept.

2- The Remembrance of Allah in the Land of Mina

In several narrations, we read that the meaning of remembering Allah during these days refers to the specific Takbirs said after the Dhuhr prayer on the day of Eid al-Adha, continuing for fifteen prayers (i.e., ending after the Fajr prayer on the 13th). That remembrance is:

Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar, la ilaha illa Allah, wa Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar wa lillahi al-hamd, Allahu Akbar ala ma hadana, wa Allahu Akbar ala ma razaqana min bahimat al-an'am. [^1]

Additionally, some narrations specify that these fifteen instances of Takbir are specific to those who are in the land of "Mina" during the days of Hajj. However, those in other cities recite these Takbirs only after ten prayers (starting from the Dhuhr prayer on the day of Eid and ending with the Fajr prayer on the 12th) ^2. It is noteworthy that the narrations regarding the Takbir are another piece of evidence that "remembrance" in the above verses has a general aspect and is not limited to mentioning Allah's name during "sacrifice," although this general concept includes that specific instance as well (observe closely).

3- The Philosophy and Deep Secrets of Hajj!

The magnificent ceremonies of Hajj—like other acts of worship—possess numerous blessings and effects for the individual and Islamic society. If performed according to a correct program and utilized properly, they can be the source of a new transformation in Islamic societies every year.

These great rituals actually possess four dimensions, each more deep-rooted and beneficial than the other:

1- The Moral Dimension of Hajj:

The most important philosophy of Hajj is the moral transformation it creates in humans. The "Ihram" ceremony completely strips a person of material attachments, outward distinctions, colorful garments, and gold or ornaments. By prohibiting pleasures and focusing on self-building—which is among the duties of the muhrim (one in Ihram)—it detaches him from the material world and immerses him in a realm of light, spirituality, and purity. It suddenly makes those who, in normal circumstances, feel the heavy burden of imaginary distinctions, ranks, and medals on their shoulders, light-burdened, comfortable, and at peace.

Then, the other ceremonies of Hajj are performed one after another—ceremonies that moment by moment strengthen the spiritual ties of man with his God and make his relationship closer and stronger. They sever him from his dark and sin-polluted past and link him to a bright future full of purity and light.

Especially, considering the fact that the Hajj ceremonies at every step recall the memories of Ibrahim the idol-breaker, Ismail the sacrifice of Allah, and his mother Hajar, and visualize their struggles, sacrifices, and selflessness moment by moment before one's eyes. Also, considering that the land of Mecca in general, and Masjid al-Haram, the Kaaba, and the site of Tawaf in particular, recall the memories of the Prophet of Islam, the great leaders, and the struggles of the early Muslims, this moral revolution becomes deeper. In every corner of Masjid al-Haram and the land of Mecca, one sees the faces of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Ali عليه السلام, and the other great leaders, and hears the echoes of their epic calls.

Yes, all these factors join hands to provide the ground for a moral revolution in receptive hearts. In an indescribable way, they turn the page of a person's life and begin a new chapter in his existence.

It is not without reason that we read in Islamic narrations: "One who performs Hajj completely... yakhruju min dhunubihi kahay'atihi yawma waladat-hu ummuhu! ...comes out of his sins just as the day his mother gave birth to him" [^1]. Yes, Hajj is a second birth for Muslims—a birth that initiates a new human life.

Of course, it goes without saying that these blessings and effects—and what we will mention later—are neither for those who content themselves with only the shell of Hajj and discard its kernel, nor for those who make Hajj a means of recreation, sightseeing, ostentation, hypocrisy, or the acquisition of personal material means, never becoming aware of its spirit. Their share is only what they have reached!

2- The Political Dimension of Hajj:

According to the words of one of the great jurists of Islam, the Hajj ceremonies, while offering the purest and deepest acts of worship, are the most effective means for advancing the political goals of Islam.

The spirit of worship is attention to Allah, and the spirit of politics is attention to Allah's creation. These two are so interwoven in Hajj that they are like the warp and weft of a single fabric!

Hajj is an effective factor for the unity of the ranks of Muslims. Hajj is a factor for combating national prejudices, racism, and being confined within the walls of geographical borders. Hajj is a means for breaking censorships and eliminating the oppression of tyrannical regimes that rule over Islamic countries. Hajj is a means for transferring political news of Islamic countries from one point to another. Finally, Hajj is an effective factor for breaking the chains of captivity and colonialism and liberating Muslims.

For this reason, in those days when tyrannical rulers like the Umayyads and Abbasids ruled over the holy Islamic lands and monitored every contact between Muslim strata to suppress any liberation movement, the arrival of the Hajj season was a window toward freedom, the contact of different strata of the great Islamic society with one another, and the proposal of various political issues.

On this basis, when Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام enumerates the philosophy of the obligations and acts of worship, he says regarding Hajj: Al-Hajju taqwiyatan lil-din: "Allah legislated the Hajj ceremonies to strengthen the religion of Islam" [^1]. It is not without reason that a famous foreign politician says in his meaningful words: "Woe to the Muslims if they do not understand the meaning of Hajj, and woe to their enemies if they do!"

Even in Islamic narrations, Hajj is counted as the Jihad of the weak—a Jihad where even weak old men and women, by being present on its scene, can reflect the glory and greatness of the Islamic Ummah, and make the backs of the enemies of Islam tremble with the concentric circles of worshippers around the House of Allah and the raising of the call of unity and Takbir.

3- The Cultural Dimension:

The connection between Muslim strata during the days of Hajj can serve as the most effective factor for cultural exchange and the transfer of ideas.

Especially considering that the magnificent gathering of Hajj is the natural and real representative of all strata of Muslims in the world (since no artificial factor is effective in selecting individuals to go for the pilgrimage of the House of Allah, and the pilgrims of the Kaaba arise from among all groups, races, and languages spoken by Muslims and gather there).

Therefore, we read in Islamic narrations: One of the benefits of Hajj is the dissemination of the news and traditions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله to the entire Islamic world.

"Hisham ibn al-Hakam," who was among the learned companions of "Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام," says: I asked him about the philosophy of Hajj and the Tawaf of the Kaaba. He said: "Allah created the creation... and commanded them with what pertains to the matter of obedience in religion and their welfare in the matter of their world. So He established therein the gathering from the East and the West, so that they may know one another, and so that every people may bring trade from city to city... and so that the traditions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله may be known and his reports may be recognized, remembered, and never forgotten" [^1]. For this reason, during the oppressive eras when the caliphs and unjust sultans did not allow the dissemination of these rulings to Muslims, they would use this opportunity to solve their problems and, by contacting the Imams of Guidance عليه السلام and the great scholars of religion, unveil the face of Islamic laws and the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

On the other hand, Hajj can be transformed into a great cultural congress where the thinkers of the Islamic world gather during the days they are in Mecca and present their thoughts and innovations to others.

Fundamentally, one of the great misfortunes is that the borders of Islamic countries cause their cultural separation, with the Muslims of each country thinking only of themselves; in such a case, the single Islamic society is fragmented and destroyed. Yes, Hajj can prevent this grim fate.

And how beautifully Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says in the continuation of that same narration of Hisham ibn al-Hakam: "If every people and nation were to speak only of their own lands and what is in them, they would all perish, their countries would be ruined, benefits would fall away, and true news would be hidden..." ^2

4- The Economic Dimension of Hajj:

Contrary to what some think, utilizing the great congress of Hajj to strengthen the economic foundations of Islamic countries not only does not conflict with the spirit of Hajj but, according to Islamic narrations, constitutes one of its philosophies. What is the harm if Muslims, in that great gathering, lay the foundation for a common Islamic market and provide the grounds for exchange and trade among themselves in such a way that their profits do not flow into the pockets of their enemies, nor their economy be dependent on foreigners? This is not worldliness; it is the essence of worship and Jihad.

Thus, in that same narration of "Hisham ibn al-Hakam" from "Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام," while stating the philosophies of Hajj, it was explicitly mentioned that one of the goals of Hajj is to strengthen the trade of Muslims and facilitate economic relations.

In another hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the verse "There is no sin upon you that you seek bounty from your Lord" (Al-Baqarah: 198), we read that he said: The meaning of this verse is seeking livelihood. "When a man comes out of his Ihram and completes [the rituals], let him buy and sell during the season." (And this matter is not only not a sin but carries reward) [^1]. This same meaning appears in the commentary of a hadith narrated from "Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام" which extensively explains the philosophies of Hajj, and at its end, he says: "That they may witness benefits for themselves" ^2, indicating that the verse "That they may witness benefits for themselves" includes both spiritual benefits and material benefits, which from one perspective are all spiritual.

In short, if this great worship is utilized correctly and completely, and the pilgrims of the House of Allah—during those days when they are actively present in that holy land and their hearts are ready—use this great opportunity to solve the various problems of the Islamic society by forming various political, cultural, and economic congresses, this worship can be a problem-solver in every respect. Perhaps it is for this reason that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "The religion will remain standing as long as the Kaaba stands" [^1]. Also, Ali عليه السلام said: Do not forget the House of your Lord, for if you forget it, you will be destroyed. "Allah, Allah, regarding the House of your Lord! Do not leave it empty as long as you remain, for if it is abandoned, the divine respite will be taken away from you!" ^2

It is also because of the importance of this matter that a chapter has been opened in Islamic narrations under the title that if one year the Muslims wish to suspend the Hajj, it is obligatory upon the Islamic government to send them to Mecca by force [^3].

4- The Status of Sacrifice Meat in Our Era

From the above verses, it is clearly understood that the goal of sacrificing—in addition to the spiritual and psychological aspects and seeking proximity to the presence of Allah—is for its meat to reach necessary uses: both the one sacrificing should use it, and a portion should reach the needy poor.

On the other hand, the prohibition of waste (israf) in Islam is not something hidden from anyone, as the Quran, Islamic narrations, and the evidence of reason have proven it.

From the sum of this discussion, we conclude that Muslims are not permitted to throw the sacrifice meat on the ground in the land of Mina to rot or to bury it under the soil. The obligation of sacrifice for pilgrims cannot be a justification for such an act. Rather, if no needy people are found on that day and in that land, it must be transported to other regions and put to use; this is the requirement of reconciling the evidences (observe closely).

But unfortunately, in our age and time, many Muslims have acted upon the first ruling and consigned the second ruling to oblivion. Every year, thousands upon thousands of sacrifices—whose meat could satisfy the nutritional needs of a vast group of the deprived for a long time—are destroyed in that holy land in a very repulsive and undesirable manner. So far, many scholars, thinkers, and other Islamic strata have spoken with the authorities of the Hijazi government in this regard, and have even volunteered to pay the costs of the institutions necessary for their preservation and transportation. However, the rigidity of the Wahhabi clerics on one hand, and the indifference of the Saudi government authorities on the other, still prevent this work from being done.

Regardless of the issue of the prohibition of waste, which is a certain Islamic matter, the scene of the slaughterhouse on the day of Eid al-Adha currently appears so repulsive and illogical that it causes those with weak faith to doubt the essence of this program and gives enemies a strong pretext, without them knowing that this is the result of the mismanagement of the clerics of that region and the regime ruling that land. Therefore, preserving the greatness of Islam and the authenticity of the Hajj rituals requires that Muslims of the world from all points put pressure on the authorities of this country to end this horrific situation and implement the law of Islam.

And if we read in Islamic narrations that taking the sacrifice meat out of the land of Mina or the Sanctuary of Mecca is forbidden, this pertained to times when there were sufficient consumers present there.

Thus, in a Sahih narration found in reliable sources from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that one of the Imam's companions asked about this very subject. The Imam said: "We used to say that nothing should be taken out of it because of the people's need for it. But today, as the people (and their sacrifices) have increased, there is no harm in taking it out" [^1].

Ayahs

ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا۟ تَفَثَهُمْ وَلْيُوفُوا۟ نُذُورَهُمْ وَلْيَطَّوَّفُوا۟ بِٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْعَتِيقِ (٢٩)
Then let them do away with their untidiness, fulfill their vows, and circle around the Ancient House. (29)
ذَٰلِكَ وَمَن يُعَظِّمْ حُرُمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ ۗ وَأُحِلَّتْ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ إِلَّا مَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلرِّجْسَ مِنَ ٱلْأَوْثَـٰنِ وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ قَوْلَ ٱلزُّورِ (٣٠)
That, and whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah, that is better for him with his Lord. You are permitted [animals of] grazing livestock except for what will be recited to you. So avoid the abomination of idols, and avoid false speech, (30)

Commentary

Another Important Part of the Hajj Rites

Following the discussions regarding the Hajj rites in the previous verses, the verses under discussion point to another part of these rites, first stating:

"Then let them complete their prescribed duties (and remove their untidiness and body excesses)" (ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا تَفَثَهُمْ).

"And perform their vows" (وَ لْيُوفُوا نُذُورَهُمْ).

"And circumambulate the Ancient House—the house which Allah has protected from the harm of events and set free" (وَ لْيَطَّوَّفُوا بِالْبَيْتِ الْعَتِيقِ).

According to many lexicographers and famous commentators, "Tafath" means dirt, filth, and body excesses such as nails and extra hair. Some say it originally referred to the dirt under the nails and the like [^1]. Although some lexicographers have denied the existence of such a root in the Arabic language, a statement cited by Raghib in Al-Mufradat—where a desert Arab said to a very dirty and unkempt person: "Ma atfathaka wa adranaka?" ("How dirty and filthy you are!")—proves that this word is Arabic and has roots in the language.

In Islamic narrations, this phrase has been repeatedly interpreted as clipping the nails, cleaning the body, and putting aside the Ihram. In other words, this phrase refers to the program of "Taqsir" (shortening the hair or nails), which is considered one of the Hajj rites.

In some narrations, it has also been interpreted as "shaving the head," which is another method of Taqsir.

In Kanz al-Irfan, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that regarding the interpretation of this verse, he said:

"It means performing all the rites of Hajj" [^2]. However, we have no evidence for Ibn Abbas's statement here.

Interestingly, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he interpreted the phrase "ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا تَفَثَهُمْ" as meeting the Imam. When the narrator asked for clarification and pointed to the interpretation of this verse regarding clipping nails and the like,
