[^2]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 1, p. 270.

the Imam عليه السلام said: "The Quran has an outward and an inward meaning." This means the matter of meeting the Imam here relates to the inward aspect of the verse [^1]. This hadith may point to a subtle point: just as the pilgrims of the House of Allah remove physical impurities after performing the Hajj rites, they must also remove the impurities of their souls and spirits by meeting the Imam عليه السلام and their leader. Especially since in many eras, tyrannical caliphs did not allow such meetings under normal circumstances, and the Hajj ceremony was the best opportunity to achieve this goal.

In this regard, we read in another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام:

"The completion of Hajj is meeting the Imam" [^2]. In reality, both are purifications: one is the purification of the outward from dirt and filth, and the other is the purification of the inward from ignorance and moral vices.

As for the meaning of fulfilling vows (vafa' bi al-nadhr), it refers to the fact that many people would vow that if they were successful in performing Hajj, they would perform additional sacrifices, charities, or good deeds beyond the Hajj rites. Sometimes, after reaching their destination, they would forget their vows. The Quran emphasizes that they should not fall short in fulfilling their vows [^3]. But why is the Kaaba called "al-Bayt al-Atiq"? Considering that "Atiq"

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 492.

[^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 10, p. 255 (Abwab al-Mazar, Chapter 2, Hadith 12).

[^3]: Some commentators have suggested that "vows" refers to performing the Hajj rites themselves, but by referring to the usage of the word nadhr in the Holy Quran, it becomes clear that when the word nadhr is used, its well-known and conventional meaning is intended. Therefore, using it for "Hajj rites" without a contextual clue is contrary to the apparent meaning.

is derived from the root "itq" meaning to be freed from the bond of slavery, it may be from the perspective that the House of the Kaaba is free from the ownership of servants and has never had an owner other than Allah at any time. It has also been freed from the control of tyrants like the Abrahahs.

One of the meanings of "Atiq" is noble and precious, a concept clearly seen in the House of the Kaaba. Another meaning of "Atiq" is "ancient," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Al-Atiq is that which is advanced in time, place, or rank." It is also clear that the House of the Kaaba is the oldest center of monotheism and, according to the Quran, the first house established for mankind (Al-Imran: 96).

In any case, there is no obstacle to applying this word to the House of Allah in consideration of all these distinctions, even though each commentator has pointed to a part of it, or different narrations have highlighted specific points.

As for which circumambulation (Tawaf) is intended in the last phrase of the above verse, there is discussion among commentators. (We know that after the Eid al-Adha ceremonies in Mina, pilgrims must perform two circumambulations: the first is usually called "Tawaf al-Ziyarah" and the second is called "Tawaf al-Nisa").

Some jurists and commentators believe that since the wording of the verse has no restriction or condition, its concept is general and includes Tawaf al-Ziyarah, Tawaf al-Nisa, and even the Tawaf of Umrah [^1]. Meanwhile, others believe it refers only to "Tawaf al-Ziyarah" which becomes obligatory after exiting the Hajj Ihram [^2].

[^1]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 1, p. 271.

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan cites this interpretation under the verse from some commentators without naming them.

However, in several narrations reached to us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, it is explicitly stated that it refers to "Tawaf al-Nisa." Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, in interpreting "وَ لْيُوفُوا نُذُورَهُمْ وَ لْيَطَّوَّفُوا بِالْبَيْتِ الْعَتِيقِ", said: "It means Tawaf al-Nisa" [^1]. The same meaning has been narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام [^2]. This is the same circumambulation that the Sunnis call "Tawaf al-Wada" (Farewell Circumambulation).

In any case, the latter interpretation seems stronger in light of the aforementioned hadiths, especially since the phrase "ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا تَفَثَهُمْ" might also imply that in addition to cleaning the body of dirt and extra hair, one should use perfume to complete it. We know that using perfume in Hajj is only permissible after the Tawaf and Sa'y of Ziyarah, and naturally, at this stage, no other circumambulation remains upon him except Tawaf al-Nisa (reflect on this).

The next verse, as a summary, refers to the discussions of the previous verses and says: "Such is the program of Hajj and its rites as mentioned" ("ذلِكَ") [^3]. Then, to emphasize the importance of the duties described, it adds: "And whoever honors the sacred things of Allah, it is better for him with his Lord" (وَ مَنْ يُعَظِّمْ حُرُماتِ اللَّهِ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَهُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ).

It is clear that "hurumat" (sacred things) here refers to the "acts and rites of Hajj," and the sanctity of the House of the Kaaba specifically and the Sanctuary (Haram) of Mecca generally may also be added to it.

Therefore, interpreting it specifically as "prohibitions"—meaning what is forbidden in general—or all obligatory acts, is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verses.

[^1] and [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 9, p. 390, Abwab al-Tawaf, Chapter 2.

[^3]: This word implies a deleted sentence, and the estimation is: "Such is the matter of Hajj and the rites."

Additionally, it should be noted that "hurumat" is the plural of "hurmah," which originally means something whose sanctity must be preserved and which must not be desecrated.

Then, in connection with the rules of Ihram, it mentions the lawfulness of cattle, saying:

"Cattle (such as camels, cows, and sheep) are lawful for you, except what is recited to you and the order for its prohibition will be issued" (وَ أُحِلَّتْ لَكُمُ الْأَنْعامُ إِلَّا ما يُتْلى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ).

The phrase "إِلَّا ما يُتْلى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ" (except what is recited to you) may refer to the prohibition of hunting for the person in Ihram, which was mentioned in Surah Al-Ma'idah (revealed later) in verse 95: "يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَقْتُلُوا الصَّيْدَ وَ أَنْتُمْ حُرُمٌ" ("O you who have believed, do not kill game while you are in the state of Ihram").

It may also refer to the phrase mentioned at the end of the verse under discussion regarding the prohibition of sacrifices slaughtered for idols, for we know that an animal is lawful only if the name of Allah is mentioned at the time of its slaughter, not the name of idols or any other name.

At the end of this verse, it states two other commands related to the Hajj ceremonies and the struggle against the traditions of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah):

First, it says: "Avoid the filth of idols" (فَاجْتَنِبُوا الرِّجْسَ مِنَ الْأَوْثانِ).

"Authan" is the plural of "wathan" (rhyming with kafan), meaning stones that were worshipped by the people of the Age of Ignorance. Here, "authan" explains the word "rijs" (filth) mentioned earlier. Thus, it says: avoid filth, then it says: the filth is those very idols.

It is also noteworthy that the idolaters of the Age of Ignorance would pour the blood of sacrificed animals over their idols, creating a very ugly, filthy, and repulsive sight, which the above expression may also refer to.

The second command is: "And avoid false and baseless speech" (وَ اجْتَنِبُوا قَوْلَ الزُّورِ).

Key Points What is "Qawl al-Zur"?

Some commentators have considered this an allusion to the manner in which the polytheists recited the "Talbiyah" during the Hajj ceremonies in the Age of Ignorance. They had distorted the "Talbiyah"—which is a perfect reflection of monotheism—to the extent that it included the most offensive polytheistic expressions. They would say: "Labbayk la sharika lak, illa sharikan huwa lak! Tamlikuhu wa ma malak" ("We have answered Your call and come to You, O God Who has no partner, except a partner who belongs to You; You own him and whatever he possesses").

This sentence was certainly false and vain speech, and an instance of "qawl al-zur," which originally means lying, false speech, and that which deviates from the limit of moderation.

Nevertheless, the verse's focus on the actions of the polytheists in the Age of Ignorance during Hajj does not prevent the generality of its concept, which is to avoid every idol in any shape or form, and to avoid every false statement of any type or quality.

Therefore, in some narrations, "authan" (idols) has been interpreted as "chess" (a type of gambling) and "qawl al-zur" has been interpreted as "forbidden singing" (ghina) and "false testimony." These are, in fact, mentions of some instances of this general concept, not a restriction of the verse's meaning to these specific matters.

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: One day he stood up and delivered a sermon to the people, saying:

"O people, false testimony has been equated with associating partners with Allah." Then he recited this verse: "فَاجْتَنِبُوا الرِّجْسَ مِنَ الْأَوْثانِ وَ اجْتَنِبُوا قَوْلَ الزُّورِ".

This hadith also points to the breadth of this verse's concept.

Ayahs

حُنَفَآءَ لِلَّهِ غَيْرَ مُشْرِكِينَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَكَأَنَّمَا خَرَّ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتَخْطَفُهُ ٱلطَّيْرُ أَوْ تَهْوِى بِهِ ٱلرِّيحُ فِى مَكَانٍ سَحِيقٍ (٣١)
as persons having pure faith in Allah, not ascribing partners to Him. Whoever ascribes partners to Allah is as though he had fallen from a height, then [his corpse] is devoured by vultures, or [his remains are] blown away by the wind far and wide. (31)
ذَٰلِكَ وَمَن يُعَظِّمْ شَعَـٰٓئِرَ ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّهَا مِن تَقْوَى ٱلْقُلُوبِ (٣٢)
That. And whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah—indeed that arises from the Godwariness of hearts. (32)
لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ثُمَّ مَحِلُّهَآ إِلَى ٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْعَتِيقِ (٣٣)
You may benefit from them until a specified time. Then their place of sacrifice is by the Ancient House. (33)

Commentary

Venerating Divine Symbols is a Sign of Piety of the Hearts

Since the final verses of the previous discussion emphasized the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the avoidance of every form of idol and idol-worship, the verses under discussion follow this same important matter, stating:

"Perform the Hajj rituals and the recitation of the Talbiyah while your intention and program are pure for Allah, and let no form of polytheism (shirk) find its way into it" (حُنَفاءَ لِلَّهِ غَيْرَ مُشْرِكِينَ بِهِ). [^1] "Hunafa" is the plural of "Hanif," meaning one who inclines from deviation and error toward uprightness and moderation; in other words, one who walks upon the Straight Path (for "Hanaf"—rhyming with Sadaf—means inclination, and the result of inclining away from every deviation is being positioned on the Straight Path).

In this way, the above verse reminds us of the issue of sincerity (Ikhlas) and the intention of proximity (Qurbat) as the primary motive in Hajj and worship in general, for the soul of worship is sincerity, and sincerity exists only when there is no non-divine motive or polytheism involved.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, in response to a question regarding the interpretation of "Hanif," he said:

هى الفطرةالَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها، لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ‌ قال فطرهم اللَّه على المعرفة:

"It is the primordial nature (Fitrah) upon which Allah created mankind; there is no change in the creation of Allah. He then said: Allah has placed Monotheism in the innate nature of human beings." [^2]

[^1]: "Hunafa" and "Ghayra Mushrikin" are both circumstantial qualifiers (Hal) for the pronoun in "Fajtanibu" and "Ijtanibu" in the previous verse.

[^2]: Tawhid al-Saduq, as cited in Tafsir al-Safi.

The interpretation provided in this narration actually refers to the primary root of sincerity, which is the monotheistic nature (Fitrah), from which the intention of proximity and the divine motive also spring.

Then, it presents a very expressive and vivid depiction of the state of the polytheists and their fall, misery, and destruction, stating: "Whoever associates a partner with Allah, it is as if he has fallen from the sky, and in the meantime, birds (scavengers) snatch him in mid-air (and every piece of his flesh falls into the beak of a carrion-eating bird), or (if he passes safely from their talons) a fierce wind hurls him to a remote place" (وَ مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَكَأَنَّما خَرَّ مِنَ السَّماءِ فَتَخْطَفُهُ الطَّيْرُ أَوْ تَهْوِي بِهِ الرِّيحُ فِي مَكانٍ سَحِيقٍ). [^1] In reality, the "sky" is a metaphor for "Monotheism," and polytheism causes a fall from this sky. Naturally, in this sky, stars shine and the moon and sun radiate; blessed is the one who, if not like the sun and moon in this sky, is at least like a brilliant star. However, when a person falls from this height and loftiness, he suffers one of two painful fates: either he becomes prey for scavenger birds in mid-air before hitting the ground—in other words, by losing this secure base, he falls into the clutches of rebellious whims and desires, each of which snatches and destroys a part of his existence.

Or, if he escapes their hands safely, he falls into the grip of a deadly storm that slams him into a distant corner so hard that his body is shattered and...

[^1]: "Takhtafuhu" is from the root "Khataf" (rhyming with Atf), meaning to snatch with speed. "Sahiq" means distant, and "Sahuq" is used for a tall palm tree whose branches reach distant points in the sky.

...every atom of it is flung to a different point. This storm is likely a metaphor for Satan, who lies in wait!

Certainly, one who falls from the sky loses the power of decision-making; he moves toward non-existence with a speed and momentum that increases every moment, and finally, he is obliterated and destroyed.

Indeed, one who has lost the heavenly base of Monotheism can no longer take the reins of his own destiny, and the further he goes on this path, the more his acceleration in falling increases, until finally, he loses all his human capital.

Truly, no metaphor more expressive or vivid than this can be found for "polytheism."

It is also noteworthy that it has been proven today that a person in "free fall" has no weight; hence, free fall is used to train space travelers for the state of weightlessness. The state of extreme anxiety a person feels during moments of falling is due to this very issue of weightlessness.

Yes, one who turns from faith toward polytheism and loses his stable and fixed support system suffers such a state of weightlessness within his soul and spirit, followed by an extreme anxiety that dominates his being.

The next verse once again provides a summary regarding the matters of Hajj and the veneration of Divine Symbols, saying: "The matter is as has been stated" (ذلك). "And whoever honors the symbols of Allah and respects the signs of God's religion and the banners of His obedience, this is from the piety of the hearts" (وَ مَنْ يُعَظِّمْ شَعائِرَ اللَّهِ فَإِنَّها مِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ).

"Sha'a'ir" is the plural of "Sha'irah," meaning a sign or mark. Therefore, "Sha'a'ir Allah" means "the signs of the Lord," which includes the main chapters of the Divine religion, general programs, and whatever is striking upon first encounter with this religion, including the rituals of Hajj that remind a person of Allah.

Although there is no doubt that the "rituals of Hajj" are among the symbols intended in this verse—especially the issue of sacrifice, which is explicitly considered part of the symbols in verse 36 of this same Surah—it is clear that the generality of the verse's concept regarding all Islamic symbols remains strong. There is no reason to restrict it specifically to the sacrifice or all Hajj rituals, especially since the Quran, regarding the Hajj sacrifice, uses the word "min" (from/of) for "partitioning" (tab'id) to point out that the sacrifice is one of those symbols, just as we read regarding "Safa and Marwah" in verse 158 of Surah Al-Baqarah that "they too are among the symbols of Allah" (إِنَّ الصَّفا وَ الْمَرْوَةَ مِنْ شَعائِرِ اللَّهِ).

In short, everything included in religious programs that reminds a person of Allah and the greatness of His religion constitutes the Divine Symbols, and honoring them is a sign of the piety of the hearts.

It is also noteworthy that "honoring"—as some literalist commentators have suggested—does not mean the physical size of the sacrificial animal and the like. Rather, the reality of veneration is to elevate the status and position of these symbols in thoughts, minds, the apparent, and the hidden, and to perform what is worthy of their respect and greatness.

The connection of this act with the piety of the hearts is also clear. Besides the fact that "veneration" is among the intentional concepts (al-anawin al-qasdiyyah), it often happens that ostentatious individuals or hypocrites pretend to venerate the symbols; however, because it does not spring from the piety of their hearts, it is worthless. True veneration and honoring belong to those who possess piety of the heart. And we know...

...that piety, the spirit of God-wariness, and the sense of responsibility toward Divine commands are things whose center is the heart and soul of man, and from there they spread to the body. Therefore, it can be said that respecting and honoring Divine Symbols is among the signs of the piety of the heart. [^1] In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he pointed to his blessed chest and said: "Al-taqwa hahuna": "The reality of piety is here!" (Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 7, p. 4448).

It is understood from some narrations that a group of Muslims believed that when a camel or another head of livestock was designated as a sacrifice and brought from near or far toward the place of Ihram, and from there toward the land of Mecca, one should not ride it, nor should they milk it or utilize it, considering it completely separated from themselves. The Quran negates this superstitious thinking, saying: "For you in these sacrificial animals are benefits and profits until a specified time (i.e., until the day of their slaughter) arrives" (لَكُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى). In a Hadith, we read that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, on the way to Mecca, passed by a man who was walking with great difficulty while he had a camel with him that no one was riding. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Irkabha": "Ride it!"

[^1]: Since there must be a connection between the "condition" (shart) and the "requital" (jaza), and both rulings must apply to one subject, there is an elision in the above verse, and the implied meaning was: وَ مَنْ يُعَظِّمْ شَعائِرَ اللَّهِ‌ فان تعظيمهامِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ. It also seems likely that the requital is entirely elided, and the phrase "فَإِنَّها مِنْ تَقْوَى‌" is a cause that has replaced the effect, the implied meaning being: وَ مَنْ يُعَظِّمْ شَعائِرَ اللَّهِ‌ فهو خير له فان تعظيمهامِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ.

He replied: "O Messenger of Allah, it is a Hady (sacrificial offering)!" The Prophet said:

"Irkabha waylak!": "Woe to you, I say ride it!" [^1] In numerous narrations reached through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, this same point is emphasized. Among them, Abu Basir narrates from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that in interpreting the above verse, he said:

ان احتاج الى ظهرها ركبها، من غير ان يعنف عليها و ان كان لها لبن حلبها حلابا لا ينهكها:

"If he needs its back, he may ride it, without being harsh toward it; and if it has milk, he may milk it, but he should not exhaust it in milking." [^2] In reality, the above instruction is a moderate and middle-path command between two extreme and irregular practices. On one hand, some did not respect the sacrificial animals at all, sometimes slaughtering them before reaching their destination and using their meat, which was forbidden in verse 2 of Surah Al-Ma'idah (لا تُحِلُّوا شَعائِرَ اللَّهِ وَ لَا الشَّهْرَ الْحَرامَ وَ لَا الْهَدْيَ وَ لَا الْقَلائِدَ).

On the other hand, some went to such extremes that as soon as the name of sacrifice was placed on an animal, they would neither use its milk nor ride it, even if they were coming from a long distance to Mecca; in the verse under discussion, this meaning is permitted.

The only objection that might be raised against the above interpretation is that in the preceding verses, there was no mention of sacrificial animals; how then does the pronoun refer back to them?

However, considering that sacrificial animals are certainly one of the instances of the "Symbols of Allah" mentioned in the previous verse and will be mentioned again later, the answer to this objection becomes clear. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 23, p. 33.

[^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 497.

In any case, at the end of the verse, regarding the final destination of the sacrifice, it says: "Then their place of sacrifice is toward the Ancient House (the Kaaba)" (ثُمَّ مَحِلُّها إِلَى الْبَيْتِ الْعَتِيقِ).

Thus, as long as the animals designated for sacrifice have not reached the place of slaughter, one can benefit from them, and upon reaching the place of slaughter, the duty of sacrificing them must be performed.

Of course, according to what jurists have stated based on Islamic evidence, if the sacrifice pertains to the Hajj, it must be slaughtered in the land of "Mina," and if it is for "Umrah Mufradah," in the land of "Mecca." Since the verses under discussion speak of the Hajj rituals, "the Ancient House" (al-Bayt al-Atiq) here must be understood in a broad sense to include the outskirts of Mecca (Mina) as well (take note).

[^1]: What we have stated above is the clear interpretation of the verse, and here two other interpretations have also been mentioned: First, that the pronoun in "fiha" refers to all Hajj rituals; thus, the concept of the verse becomes: "For you in the collective Hajj rituals are benefits until a specified time (the time of the end of Hajj or the end of the world), then the final Hajj ritual where the person in Ihram exits the state of Ihram and becomes muhill is near the Kaaba and after performing the Tawaf al-Ziyarah and Nisa." Thus, the above verse becomes similar to the verse "لِيَشْهَدُوا مَنافِعَ لَهُمْ‌" which was previously interpreted. Second, that the pronoun "fiha" refers to all Divine Symbols and the prominent programs of Islam. In this case, the meaning of the verse becomes: "For you are rewards and valuable benefits in the collective Islamic programs and Divine Symbols until the end of the world, then your reward is with the Creator of the Ancient House, the Kaaba." However, the interpretation we provided in the text is more appropriate, closer, and more harmonious with other verses and Islamic narrations than these two.

Ayahs

وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنسَكًا لِّيَذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَىٰ مَا رَزَقَهُم مِّنۢ بَهِيمَةِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۗ فَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ فَلَهُۥٓ أَسْلِمُوا۟ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُخْبِتِينَ (٣٤)
For every nation We have appointed a rite so that they might mention Allah’s Name over the livestock He has provided them. Your God is the One God, so submit to Him. And give good news to the humble (34)
ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِرَ ٱللَّهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَهُمْ وَٱلْمُقِيمِى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٣٥)
—those whose hearts tremble with awe when Allah is mentioned, and who are patient through whatever visits them, and who maintain the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them. (35)

Commentary

Give Glad Tidings to the Humble

In connection with the previous verses, including the command regarding sacrifice, a question may arise: What kind of worship is this that has been legislated in Islam—to sacrifice animals for God and to seek His pleasure? Does Allah have any need for sacrifice? And was this practice present in other religions, or was it exclusive to the polytheists?

To clarify this matter, the Quran states in the first verse under discussion:

This is not exclusive to you that you have ceremonies of slaughter and sacrifice for God; "And for every nation We have appointed a rite [of sacrifice] that they may mention the name of Allah over what He has provided for them of [sacrificial] animals" (وَ لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنا مَنْسَكاً لِيَذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللَّهِ عَلى‌ ما رَزَقَهُمْ مِنْ بَهِيمَةِ الْأَنْعامِ‌).

Ragheb says in Al-Mufradat: "Nusk" means "worship" and "nasik" means "worshiper," and the manasik of Hajj refers to the stations where this worship is performed, or it refers to the acts themselves.

However, according to Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and Abu al-Futuh Razi in Rawh al-Janan, mansak (on the weight of mansab) is likely to mean specifically the act of sacrifice among all acts of worship [1]. Therefore, although mansak has a general concept that includes other acts of worship, especially the Hajj rituals, in the verse under discussion, it specifically means sacrifice due to the context of "that they may mention the name of Allah..." (لِيَذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللَّهِ‌ ...).

In any case, the issue of "sacrifice" has always been a subject of inquiry. Of course, these questions mostly arose because of superstitious practices that became mixed with this worship, such as the polytheists' sacrifices for idols with their specific programs. However, slaughtering an animal in the name of Allah and to seek His pleasure—which is a symbol of a human's readiness for self-sacrifice and being sacrificed in His path—and then using its meat to feed the poor and the like, is a logical and completely understandable matter.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "For your God is one God" (and His program is also a single program) (فَإِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ).

Since this is the case, "so to Him submit" (فَلَهُ أَسْلِمُوا).

[1]: For this reason, "nasaktu al-shat" means "I slaughtered the sheep."

"And give glad tidings to the humble [before the commands of their Lord]" (وَ بَشِّرِ الْمُخْبِتِينَ‌) [1].

Then, in the next verse, He explains the characteristics of the "mukhbitin" (the humble ones) in four parts, two of which have a spiritual and psychological aspect, and two of which have a physical aspect:

First, He says: "They are those who, when Allah is mentioned, their hearts are filled with fear of their Lord" (الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِرَ اللَّهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ‌).

It is not that they fear His wrath without reason, nor that they have doubt or hesitation regarding His mercy; rather, this fear is because of the responsibilities they carry and perhaps have fallen short in fulfilling. This fear is due to the realization of the magnificent station of Allah, for a human feels awe in the presence of greatness [2]. Secondly, "they are patient over what has afflicted them" (وَ الصَّابِرِينَ عَلى‌ ما أَصابَهُمْ‌).

No matter how great the magnitude of the incident or how heavy its distress, they do not kneel before it; they do not lose their composure, they do not flee the field, they do not become despondent, they do not open their lips in ingratitude, and in short, they stand firm, move forward, and are victorious.

[1]: "Mukhbitin" is derived from the word "ikhbat" from the root "khabt" (on the weight of sabt), which means a flat and vast land where a person walks easily. Later, this root came to mean certainty and humility, because walking on such land is accompanied by certainty, and such land is under the feet of humble and submissive travelers!

[2]: Regarding the motives for the fear and awe of Allah, we have discussed this in detail in Volume 7, page 86 (under the second verse of Surah Al-Anfal).

Third and fourth: "they establish prayer and spend out of what We have provided them" (وَ الْمُقِيمِي الصَّلاةِ وَ مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ‌).

On one hand, their connection with the Creator of the world is firm, and on the other hand, their bond with the creation of Allah is strengthened.

From this explanation, it becomes clearly evident that the matter of ikhbat, submission, and humility—which are among the special attributes of the believers—does not only have an internal aspect; rather, its effects must appear and become manifest in all actions.

Ayahs

وَٱلْبُدْنَ جَعَلْنَـٰهَا لَكُم مِّن شَعَـٰٓئِرِ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ فِيهَا خَيْرٌ ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَا صَوَآفَّ ۖ فَإِذَا وَجَبَتْ جُنُوبُهَا فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا وَأَطْعِمُوا۟ ٱلْقَانِعَ وَٱلْمُعْتَرَّ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ سَخَّرْنَـٰهَا لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٣٦)
We have appointed for you the [sacrificial] camels as part of Allah’s sacraments. There is good for you in them. So mention the Name of Allah over them as they stand. And when they have fallen on their flanks, eat from them, and feed the self-contained needy and the mendicant. Thus have We disposed them for your benefit so that you may give thanks. (36)
لَن يَنَالَ ٱللَّهَ لُحُومُهَا وَلَا دِمَآؤُهَا وَلَـٰكِن يَنَالُهُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ مِنكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ سَخَّرَهَا لَكُمْ لِتُكَبِّرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا هَدَىٰكُمْ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٣٧)
It is not their flesh or blood that reaches Allah; rather, it is your piety that reaches Him. Thus has He disposed them for your benefit so that you may magnify Allah for His guiding you. And give good news to the virtuous. (37)
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدَٰفِعُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ خَوَّانٍ كَفُورٍ (٣٨)
Allah will indeed defend those who have faith. Indeed Allah does not like any ingrate traitor. (38)

Commentary

What is the Purpose of Sacrifice?

In the verses under discussion, the discourse once again turns to the Hajj rituals, the divine symbols, and the issue of sacrifice. First, it states: "And the camels [of sacrifice], We have appointed them for you as among the symbols of Allah" (وَ الْبُدْنَ جَعَلْناها لَكُمْ مِنْ شَعائِرِ اللَّهِ‌).

On one hand, they belong to you, and on the other, they are among the symbols and signs of Allah in this great act of worship, for the Hajj sacrifice is one of the manifest aspects of this worship, the philosophy of which we have previously alluded to.

"Budn" (rhyming with quds) is the plural of "budnah" (rhyming with ajalah), meaning a large, fat, and fleshy camel. Since such an animal is more suitable for the sacrificial rites and for feeding the poor and needy, it has been specifically emphasized; otherwise, we know that the fatness of the sacrificial animal is not an obligatory condition. It is sufficient that it is not emaciated.

Then it adds: "In them is good for you" (لَكُمْ فِيها خَيْرٌ).

On one hand, you benefit from their meat and feed others, and on the other hand, due to this altruism, sacrifice, and worship of the Lord, you will benefit from its spiritual fruits and seek proximity to His presence.

Then, it describes the manner of sacrifice in a short sentence: "So mention the name of Allah over them while they are standing in line" (فَاذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْها صَوافَ‌).

Undoubtedly, mentioning the name of Allah at the time of slaughtering animals or sacrificing a camel does not have a specific required form; any way His name is mentioned is sufficient, as is the apparent meaning of the verse. However, in some narrations, a specific mention is cited here, which is in fact the most perfect form. Commentators have narrated it from Ibn Abbas: Allahu Akbar, la ilaha illa Allahu wallahu Akbar, Allahumma minka wa lak [^1]. But in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, more comprehensive phrases are mentioned. He said:

"When you have purchased the sacrifice, turn it toward the Qibla and at the time of slaughter or sacrifice say:

وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ حَنِيفاً مسلماوَ ما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ‌ إِنَّ صَلاتِي وَ نُسُكِي وَ مَحْيايَ وَ مَماتِي لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ لا شريك له و بذلك أمرت و أنا من المسلمين، اللهم منك و لك بسم اللَّه و باللَّه و اللَّه اكبر، اللهم تقبل منى‌ [^2]"

The word "Sawaff" is the plural of "Saffah," meaning "lined up." As stated in narrations, the intention is to tie the two front legs of the sacrificial camel together from the wrist to the knee while it is standing, so that it does not move excessively or flee during the sacrifice (nahr).

Naturally, when a certain amount of blood leaves its body, its legs become weak and it falls to the ground. Therefore, at the end of the verse, it says: "Then when their sides have fallen down (a metaphor for dying), eat of them and feed the contented poor and the beggar" (فَإِذا وَجَبَتْ جُنُوبُها فَكُلُوا مِنْها وَ أَطْعِمُوا الْقانِعَ وَ الْمُعْتَرَّ).

The difference between "Qani'" (contented) and "Mu'tarr" (beggar/petitioner) is that "Qani'" refers to someone who, if given something, is content and satisfied, showing no objection or anger. However, "Mu'tarr" is one who comes to you, asks, and requests, and perhaps may not be satisfied with what you give and might object.

"Qani'" is derived from "Qana'at" (contentment), and "Mu'tarr" is from the root "Arr" (rhyming with sharr and harr), which originally means the disease of scabies that affects human skin. Subsequently, a petitioner who approaches a person and requests help (and perhaps opens his tongue in objection) is called "Mu'tarr."

Placing "Qani'" before "Mu'tarr" is an indication that those among the deprived who are chaste of soul and self-restrained should be given more attention.

It is also noteworthy that the phrase "eat of them" (فَكُلُوا مِنْها) apparently indicates that it is obligatory for the "pilgrims" to eat something from their own sacrifice, perhaps to maintain equality between them and the needy.

Finally, the verse concludes: "Thus have We subjected them to you that you may be grateful" (كَذلِكَ سَخَّرْناها لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌).

Truly, it is amazing that an animal of such size, power, and strength is so submissive that it allows a child to tie its legs firmly and sacrifice it (the method of nahr is to plunge a knife into the hollow of its neck; bleeding begins and the animal soon dies).

Sometimes, to show the importance of this subjection, Allah removes the command of obedience and submission from these animals, and we have seen a furious and angry camel—which in normal conditions a small child could lead by its halter—turn into a dangerous creature that several strong men cannot handle.

The next verse is, in fact, an answer to the question: What need does God have for sacrifice? And fundamentally, what is the philosophy of sacrificing? Does this act benefit God? It says: "Their meat and their blood shall never reach Allah" (لَنْ يَنالَ اللَّهَ لُحُومُها وَ لا دِماؤُها). Fundamentally, Allah has no need for the meat of the sacrifice; He is neither a physical body nor in need. He is a Being who is perfect and infinite in every respect.

"But what reaches Him is the piety (Taqwa) and righteousness from you" (وَ لكِنْ يَنالُهُ التَّقْوى‌ مِنْكُمْ‌).

In other words: the goal is that by traversing the stages of piety, you place yourselves on the path of a perfect human being and draw closer to Allah day by day. All acts of worship are educational classes for you humans. Sacrifice teaches you the lesson of altruism, self-sacrifice, and readiness for martyrdom in the way of Allah, as well as the lesson of helping the needy and the poor.

The expression that their blood also does not reach Allah—even though blood is not usable—apparently refers to the ugly practices of the pre-Islamic Arabs who, whenever they sacrificed an animal, would splash its blood on the heads of idols and sometimes on the doors and walls of the Kaaba. Some uninformed Muslims were not unwilling to follow them in this superstitious program. The above verse was revealed and forbade them [^1]. Unfortunately, this pagan custom still exists in some regions where, whenever they sacrifice for the building of a house, they splash its blood on the roof and walls of that house, and even in the construction of some mosques, they perform this ugly and superstitious act which causes the pollution of the mosque. Awakened Muslims must fight against this severely.

Then, once again referring to the blessing of the subjection of animals, it says: "Thus has He subjected them to you that you may magnify Allah for His having guided you" (كَذلِكَ سَخَّرَها لَكُمْ لِتُكَبِّرُوا اللَّهَ عَلى‌ ما هَداكُمْ‌).

The ultimate goal is for you to become acquainted with the greatness of Allah—the same God who guided you in the path of legislation and creation. On one hand, He taught you the Hajj rituals and the tradition of obedience and servitude, and on the other hand, He made these large and powerful animals obedient to your command, so that you may use them in the way of obeying Allah, sacrificing, doing good to the needy, and also providing for your own lives.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, it says: "And give glad tidings to the doers of good" (وَ بَشِّرِ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

Those who utilize these divine blessings in the path of His obedience and perform their duties in the best manner, and especially do not fall short in spending in the way of Allah.

These doers of good not only do good to others but also perform the best service to themselves.

And since resisting the superstitions of the polytheists mentioned in the previous verses might arouse the fire of anger in this prejudiced and stubborn group and cause small and large conflicts, Allah, in the last verse under discussion, encourages the believers with His help, saying: "Indeed, Allah defends those who have believed" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُدافِعُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

Let the Arab tribes, Jews, Christians, and polytheists of the peninsula join hands to put the believers under pressure and, in their own imagination, destroy them; but Allah has promised to defend them—a promise of the survival of Islam until the threshold of Resurrection!

This divine promise was not exclusive to the believers of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله era against the polytheists; it is a ruling that is current and pervasive in all ages and centuries. What matters is that we be the manifestation of "those who have believed," for then divine defense is certain and infallible. Yes, Allah defends the believers.

At the end of the verse, He clarifies the position of the polytheists and their like-minded associates before Allah with this phrase: "Indeed, Allah does not love any treacherous ingrate" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ خَوَّانٍ كَفُورٍ).

Those who associated partners with Allah, and even when saying "Labbayk," explicitly mentioned the names of idols, and thus their treachery is established. Likewise, by invoking the names of idols over the sacrifices and forgetting the name of Allah, they showed ingratitude for divine blessings. In such a state, how is it possible for Allah to love these treacherous ingrates?

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse, and Ruh al-Ma'ani under the same verse with slight differences. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 10, p. 138, Chapters on Slaughter, Chapter 37. [^3]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 1, p. 314.

Ayahs

أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا۟ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ (٣٩)
Those who are fought against are permitted [to fight] because they have been wronged, and Allah is indeed able to help them (39)
ٱلَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّآ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَٰمِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَصَلَوَٰتٌ وَمَسَـٰجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ كَثِيرًا ۗ وَلَيَنصُرَنَّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَقَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ (٤٠)
—those who were expelled from their homes unjustly, only because they said, ‘Allah is our Lord.’ Had not Allah repulsed the people from one another, ruin would have befallen the monasteries, churches, synagogues and mosques in which Allah’s Name is much invoked. Allah will surely help those who help Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. (40)
ٱلَّذِينَ إِن مَّكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَمَرُوا۟ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَنَهَوْا۟ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْأُمُورِ (٤١)
Those who, if We granted them power in the land, will maintain the prayer, give the zakat, bid what is right and forbid what is wrong. And with Allah rests the outcome of all matters. (41)

Commentary

The First Command of Jihad

In some narrations, we read that while the Muslims were in Makkah, the polytheists constantly harassed them. Frequently, Muslims who had been beaten, arriving with broken heads, would come to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to complain (and request permission for Jihad). However, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would tell them: "Be patient, for I have not yet been given the command for Jihad." This continued until the Migration (Hijrah) began and the Muslims moved from Makkah to Madinah. Then, Allah revealed the above verse, which contains the permission for Jihad, and this is the first verse revealed regarding Jihad [1]. Although there is discussion among commentators as to whether this verse was the very first command for Jihad—some consider it the first, while others consider the verse: “Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you...” (Al-Baqarah: 190) to be the first, and yet others regard: “Indeed, Allah has purchased from the believers their lives and their properties...” (At-Tawbah: 111) as the first [2]—the tone of this verse is more suited to this subject. This is because the expression "permission" (idhn) is explicitly mentioned in it, whereas it is not present in the other two verses; in other words, the phrasing of this verse is unique.

In any case, considering what was mentioned in the final preceding verses—where Allah promised to defend the believers—the connection of the verses under discussion to that promise becomes clear.

In these verses, He first says: "Permission [to fight] has been given to those who are being fought, because they were wronged" (أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقَاتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا).

Then, He completes this permission with a promise of victory from Allah, the Almighty, saying: "And indeed, Allah is competent to give them victory" (وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَى نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ).

This phrase, which contains the promise of Divine aid through the expression of "Allah's power," may point to the fact that this Divine power comes to your aid only when you yourselves, to the extent of your ability, acquire power and readiness for defense. This is so they do not imagine they can sit in their homes and merely wait for the Lord's help.

In other words, you must employ whatever is in your power within this world of causes and effects, and where your power ends, you should await Allah's help. This was the very program that the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) employed in all his struggles and through which he was victorious.

Then, He provides more explanation regarding these oppressed ones who have been given permission to defend themselves, making the logic of Islam clearer regarding this aspect of Jihad. He says: "[They are] those who have been evicted from their homes without right" (الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيَارِهِمْ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ).

"Their only 'sin' was that they said, 'Our Lord is Allah'!" (إِلَّا أَنْ يَقُولُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ).

Clearly, confessing to the Oneness and Unity of God is an honor, not a sin. This confession was not something that gave the polytheists the right to expel them from their homes and lives and force them to migrate from Makkah to Madinah. Rather, this is a subtle expression used to condemn the opposing party in such cases. For example, to a person who has been ungrateful for a service or a blessing, we say: "Our sin was that we served you." This is an allusion to the ignorance of the other party who has given the punishment of a sin in return for a service [1]. Then, He recounts one of the philosophies of the legislation of Jihad as follows: "If Allah did not defend the believers, and did not repel some people by means of others through the permission of Jihad, then monasteries, churches, synagogues, and mosques in which the name of Allah is much mentioned would have been demolished" (وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُمْ بِبَعْضٍ لَهُدِّمَتْ صَوَامِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَصَلَوَاتٌ وَمَسَاجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا اسْمُ اللَّهِ كَثِيرًا).

Yes, if faithful and zealous individuals were to sit idly by and be mere spectators to the destructive activities of tyrants, the arrogant, and faithless oppressors, and if those oppressors found the field empty, they would leave no trace of temples and centers of Divine worship. This is because temples are places of awakening, the mihrab (prayer niche) is a field of struggle and war, and the mosque is a fortress against the self-willed tyrants. Fundamentally, any call to God-worship is against the tyrants who want people to worship them as gods! Therefore, if they find the opportunity, they will level all these centers to the ground.

[1]: From here it becomes clear that the exception in the above verse is a "connected exception" (istithna' muttasil), but with an allusive meaning and the mention of a claimed instance (reflect on this).

This is one of the objectives of the legislation of Jihad and the permission for fighting and war.

Regarding the difference between sawami‘, biya‘, salawat, and masajid, commentators have various explanations, but what seems most correct is:

"Sawami‘" is the plural of sawma‘ah, meaning a place usually built outside cities and far from the population for ascetics and worshippers who have renounced the world, which in Persian is called deyr (it should be noted that sawma‘ah originally means a building whose upper part is joined together, apparently referring to the four-sided minarets that monks built for their monasteries).

"Biya‘" is the plural of bi‘ah, meaning a "Christian temple," which is also called a kanisah or kilisa (church).

"Salawat" is the plural of salat, meaning a "Jewish temple," and some consider it the Arabized form of salutha, which in the Hebrew language means a house of prayer.

And "Masajid" is the plural of masjid, the temple of the Muslims.

Therefore, sawami‘ and biya‘, although both belong to the Christians, one is the name for a general temple and the other is the name for the center of those who have renounced the world. Some have also considered biya‘ a shared term applied to both Jewish and Christian temples.

Additionally, the phrase "in which the name of Allah is much mentioned" (يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا اسْمُ اللَّهِ كَثِيرًا) is apparently a description specifically for the mosques, because the mosques of the Muslims—considering the five daily prayers performed in them every day of the year—are the most vibrant centers of worship in the world, whereas many other temples are utilized only one day a week or on certain days of the year.

At the end of the verse, He once again repeats and emphasizes the promise of Divine assistance, saying:

"And Allah will surely support those who support Him [and His cause]" (وَلَيَنْصُرَنَّ اللَّهُ مَنْ يَنْصُرُهُ).

And undoubtedly, this promise of Allah is bound to happen because "He is Powerful, Mighty, and Invincible" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ).

This is so the defenders of the line of Monotheism do not imagine that in this field of struggle between truth and falsehood, and against the massive crowd of stubborn enemies, they are alone and without support.

It was in the light of this very Divine promise that the defenders of the way of Allah, in the fields of battle against the enemies—even though in many scenes they were in the minority in terms of numbers and war equipment—were victorious over them. Such a victory could not be interpreted or justified except through Divine help and support.

The final verse, which is an explanation regarding the "helpers of Allah" who were promised assistance in the previous verse, says: "They are those who, if We give them authority in the land, establish prayer and give zakat and enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong" (الَّذِينَ إِنْ مَكَّنَّاهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَقَامُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتَوُا الزَّكَاةَ وَأَمَرُوا بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَنَهَوْا عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ).

After victory, they never engage in luxury, pleasure-seeking, and vanity like the self-willed and the tyrants, nor do they drown in pride and intoxication. Rather, they make victories and successes a ladder for building themselves and society. After gaining power, they do not turn into a new tyrant; their connection with Allah is firm, and their connection with Allah's creation is also strengthened. This is because "prayer" (salat) is the symbol of the link with the Creator, and "zakat" is a symbol for the link with the creation, and enjoining what is right and forbidding what is wrong are considered the fundamental pillars for building a healthy society. These four attributes are sufficient to define these individuals.

In the shadow of these, other acts of worship, righteous deeds, and the characteristics of a faithful and advanced society are provided [1]. It should be noted that "makkanna" is from the root tamkin, and tamkin means providing the means and tools for work, whether it be necessary instruments and equipment or sufficient knowledge, awareness, and physical and intellectual strength.

"Ma‘ruf" means good and truthful deeds, and "munkar" means ugly and false deeds, because the former is recognized by every person of pure nature, while the latter is unrecognized; in other words, the former is harmonious with human nature and the latter is discordant.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And to Allah belongs the outcome of [all] matters" (وَلِلَّهِ عَاقِبَةُ الْأُمُورِ).

Meaning, just as the beginning of every power and victory is from Allah, in the end, all of these return to Him, for "Indeed we belong to Allah, and indeed to Him we will return."

Key Points

1- The Philosophy of the Legislation of Jihad

Although we have discussed this important issue extensively in the past [2], considering that the verses under discussion are likely the first verses that issued the permission for Jihad to the Muslims, and their content points to the philosophy of this ruling, a renewed reminder in this regard seems necessary.

In these verses, two important parts of the philosophies of Jihad are mentioned:

First, the Jihad of the "oppressed" against the "oppressor and tyrant," which is his definite natural, innate, and rational right—not to submit to oppression, to rise up and cry out, to take up arms, to put the oppressor in his place, and to cut off his tainted hand from his rights.

The other is Jihad against the tyrants who intend to erase the name of Allah from hearts and destroy the temples that are centers for the awakening of thoughts.

Against this group, too, one must rise up so they cannot erase the Divine remembrance from memories, anesthetize the people, and make them their servants and slaves.

This point is also noteworthy: destroying temples and mosques is not only by attacking them with means of destruction and smashing them; rather, it is possible to enter through indirect ways and provide so many unhealthy distractions or spread such evil propaganda that the masses are diverted from temples and mosques, thereby practically turning them into ruins.

Therefore, those who say: "Why has Islam allowed Muslims to advance their goals by resorting to force and armed struggle? Why are Islamic goals not implemented by resorting to logic?"—their answer becomes clearly evident from what we have said above.

Can one stand with rational talk against a cruel oppressor who makes people homeless from their houses and dwellings for the crime of saying "There is no god but Allah," who appropriates their existence, and who is not bound by any law or logic?!

Can one speak to such irrational madmen except with the language of weapons?

This is exactly like saying: "Why do you not sit at a table and negotiate with the usurping Israel?" The same Israel that has trampled upon all international laws, warnings, and resolutions of world organizations agreed upon by all nations, and all human and religious laws. Does such a person understand logic and negotiation?! Is an Israel under whose incendiary bombs thousands upon thousands of innocent children, old women, old men, and even hospital patients have been torn to pieces and burned, someone who should be approached through logic?

Likewise, are those who see the existence of a temple or mosque—which causes awareness and movement among the masses—as an obstacle to their illegitimate interests and strive to destroy and erase it at any cost, people with whom one should enter into discussion through peaceful means?

In any case, if we set aside mental abstractions and look at the realities existing in human societies, we will be certain that in some cases there is no choice but to resort to weapons and force. This is not due to the weakness of logic, but rather the lack of readiness of tyrants to accept sound logic. Undoubtedly, wherever logic is effective, it has the right of priority.

2- To Whom Has Allah Promised Help?

It is a mistaken notion that the promise of victory, Divine help, and the defense of the believers mentioned in the above verses and other verses of the Quran is outside the sunnah (tradition) of creation and the laws of life. It is not so. Allah has given this promise only to those who mobilize all their forces and enter the field with all their might. Therefore, in the phrasing of the above verses, we read:

"If Allah did not repel some people by means of others..." Therefore, Allah does not repel the oppressors solely with unseen forces and the power of lightning and earthquakes (except in exceptional cases); rather, He repels their evil by means of the true believers. He takes only such people under the cover of His protection.

Thus, Divine promises should not only not cause lethargy or the removal of the burden of responsibility from one's shoulders, but they should also cause more movement and more extensive activity. Of course, in that case, victory from Allah is guaranteed.

It is also reminded that this group of believers does not go to Allah's door only before victory; rather, after victory as well, according to: "Those who, if We give them authority in the land, establish prayer..." they likewise keep their relationship with Him firm and make victory over the enemy a means for spreading truth, justice, and virtue.

In some Islamic narrations, the above verse is interpreted as referring to Imam Mahdi (عليه السلام) and his companions, or the Family of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) in general. As we read in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) regarding the interpretation of the verse: "Those who, if We give them authority in the land..." he said: "This verse until the end is for the Family of Muhammad and the Mahdi and his companions."

“Allah will make them masters of the East and the West of the earth, and He will make the religion manifest, and through him and his companions, Allah will eliminate innovations and falsehood, just as the wicked had eliminated the truth, until no trace of oppression is seen anywhere (because) they enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong.” [1] Other hadiths have also been narrated in this regard.

[1]: Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim (as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 506).

However, as we have said many times, these hadiths express the clear and manifest instances and do not prevent the generality of the verse's concept. Therefore, the broad concept of the verse includes all faithful, striving, and struggling individuals.

3- The "Muhsinin," the "Mukhbitin," and the "Helpers of Allah"

In the above verses and the verses before them, He sometimes commands: "Give glad tidings to the Muhsinin (the doers of good)," and then defines them as those who have believed and do not commit betrayal or ingratitude.

And sometimes He speaks of the "Mukhbitin" (the humble), and interprets them as those whose hearts tremble when Allah is mentioned, and who are patient and steadfast in the face of calamities, establishers of prayer, and spenders from all [Divine] bounties.

And finally, He counts the characteristics of the "Helpers of Allah" as being those who, at the time of victory, do not take the path of rebellion, but establish prayer, give zakat, and enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong.

The synthesis of these verses shows that the true believers who possess all these characteristics are, on the one hand, very powerful in terms of belief and sense of responsibility, and on the other hand, strong and persistent in terms of action in the aspects of connection with the Creator and the creation, and the struggle against corruption.

Ayahs

وَإِن يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَعَادٌ وَثَمُودُ (٤٢)
If they impugn you, the people of Noah had impugned before them and ‘Ad and Thamud, (42)
وَقَوْمُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَقَوْمُ لُوطٍ (٤٣)
[as well as] the people of Abraham and the people of Lot, (43)
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ مَدْيَنَ ۖ وَكُذِّبَ مُوسَىٰ فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ (٤٤)
and the inhabitants of Midian, and Moses was also impugned. But I gave the faithless a respite, then I seized them and how was My rebuttal! (44)
فَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَا وَهِىَ ظَالِمَةٌ فَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا وَبِئْرٍ مُّعَطَّلَةٍ وَقَصْرٍ مَّشِيدٍ (٤٥)
How many towns We have destroyed when they had been wrongdoers! So they lie fallen on their trellises, their wells neglected and their ruined palaces! (45)

Commentary

The Abandoned Well and the Lofty Palace!

Since the preceding verses spoke of the grueling hardships that the enemies of Islam had created for the believers—harassing them and driving them from their homes and hearths for the crime of monotheism—the command for Jihad against them was issued.

In the verses under discussion, Allah provides comfort to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers on one hand, and clarifies the grim fate of the disbelievers on the other.

First, He says: "If they deny you, do not be grieved, for before them the people of Noah, ‘Ad, and Thamud denied their messengers" (وَ إِنْ يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَ عادٌ وَ ثَمُودُ).

"And also the people of Abraham and the people of Lot denied these two great prophets" (وَ قَوْمُ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ).

And also "the inhabitants of Madyan rose to deny Shu’ayb, and Moses was denied by Pharaoh and the Pharaohs" (وَ أَصْحابُ مَدْيَنَ وَ كُذِّبَ مُوسى‌). Just as these oppositions and denials did not cause these great prophets to falter in their call toward monotheism, truth, and justice, they shall certainly not affect your pure and steadfast spirit.

However, these blind-hearted disbelievers should not imagine that they can continue these shameful programs forever: "In the past, I gave respite to the disbelievers so they might fully undergo their trial, and the proof would be completed against them, and they would be immersed in luxury and blessings; then I seized them with the strikes of punishment" (فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلْكافِرِينَ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ‌).

"You saw how severely I rejected their actions and demonstrated their vileness" (فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكِيرِ) 1. I took away their blessings and gave them affliction and misery; I took their lives and gave death in exchange.

In the last verse under discussion, the nature of Allah's punishment—which was mentioned briefly in the previous sentence—is explained in detail. He says: "How many a town and settlement have We destroyed while they were wrongdoers and oppressors" (فَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها وَ هِيَ ظالِمَةٌ). "They collapsed upon their roofs" (فَهِيَ خاوِيَةٌ عَلى‌ عُرُوشِها).

This means the intensity of the event was such that first the roofs collapsed, and then the walls fell upon the roofs!

"And how many a well full of water whose owners were destroyed and its waters sank into the earth, remaining abandoned and useless; no one draws water from them, and no thirsty person is quenched by them" (وَ بِئْرٍ مُعَطَّلَةٍ).

"And how many a magnificent palace and sky-scraping castle, beautifully plastered, were ruined," and their owners hurried to the realm of non-existence (وَ قَصْرٍ مَشِيدٍ) [2].

1: "Nakir" means "denial" or "rejection," and here it refers to punishment.

[2]: "Mashid" is derived from the root "Shid" (rhyming with "Bid") and has two meanings: first, height/elevation, and second, gypsum/plaster. In the first sense, "Mashid" means lofty and sky-scraping palaces. In the second sense, it means palaces built very sturdily to remain safe from the accidents of time, because in those days most houses were made of mud, and houses built with plaster were much stronger than mud houses.

Thus, both their glamorous, sturdy dwellings remained without owners, and the waters that were the source of their lands' prosperity were lost.

Key Point

Interestingly, in the narrations that have reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, the phrase "an abandoned well" (بئر معطلة) has been interpreted as referring to scholars and sages who remain isolated in society and whose knowledge no one benefits from!

We read from Imam Musa ibn Jafar عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the phrase "وَ بِئْرٍ مُعَطَّلَةٍ وَ قَصْرٍ مَشِيدٍ":

Al-Bi’ru al-Mu’attalatu al-Imamu al-Samit, wa al-Qasru al-Mashidu al-Imamu al-Natiq:

"The abandoned well from which no one benefits is the silent Imam, and the lofty, sturdy palace is the speaking Imam."

A similar theme has been narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام 1. This interpretation is, in fact, a form of metaphor (just as Imam al-Mahdi عليه السلام and his world-encompassing justice are likened in narrations to "flowing water" (ماء معين)). That is, when the Imam sits upon the seat of governance, he is like a lofty, sturdy palace that attracts eyes from near and far and serves as a sanctuary for all. But when he is distanced from the seat of governance and people leave him alone, and the unworthy sit in his place, he becomes like a well full of water that is consigned to oblivion; neither the thirsty benefit from it, nor are trees and plants nourished by it.

1: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 3, p. 30.

In this regard, how beautifully an Arabic poet has composed:

Bi’run mu’attalatun wa qasrun mushrifun Mathalun li-Ali Muhammadin mustatrafu Fal-qasru majduhumu al-ladhi la yurtaqa Wal-bi’ru ‘ilmuhumu al-ladhi la yunzafu

"The abandoned well and the towering palace are a beautiful metaphor for the Family of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله.

The 'palace' is their glory and greatness which no one can scale, and the 'well' is their knowledge and wisdom which never runs dry" 1.

Ayahs

أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِهَآ أَوْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا ۖ فَإِنَّهَا لَا تَعْمَى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَلَـٰكِن تَعْمَى ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلَّتِى فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ (٤٦)
Have they not travelled through the land so that they may have hearts by which they may exercise their reason, or ears by which they may hear? Indeed, it is not the eyes that turn blind, but it is the hearts in the breasts that turn blind! (46)
وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلْعَذَابِ وَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥ ۚ وَإِنَّ يَوْمًا عِندَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ (٤٧)
They ask you to hasten the punishment, though Allah will never break His promise. Indeed a day with your Lord is like a thousand years by your reckoning. (47)
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَمْلَيْتُ لَهَا وَهِىَ ظَالِمَةٌ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهَا وَإِلَىَّ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٤٨)
To how many a town did I give respite while it was doing wrong! Then I seized it, and toward Me is the destination. (48)

Commentary

Journeying through the Earth and the Awakening of Hearts

Since the previous verses spoke of the oppressive and tyrannical nations whom Allah punished for their deeds and whose cities and lands He destroyed, the first verse under discussion emphasizes this matter by saying: "Have they not journeyed through the land so that they may have hearts with which to understand the truth? Or ears with which to hear the call of Truth?" (أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِهَا أَوْ آذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا).

Indeed, the ruins of the palaces of tyrants and the destroyed dwellings of the arrogant and the world-worshippers—who once lived at the peak of power—each possess a thousand tongues despite their silence, and with each tongue, they convey thousands of points.

These ruins are eloquent and living books of the history of these peoples, the consequences of their actions and behavior, and their disgraceful programs and ominous punishments.

These silent lands, and the remains visible in these ruins, breathe such soul-stirring melodies into the human spirit that sometimes studying one of them provides as much of a lesson as studying a thick volume. Given the repetition of history, which is a fundamental principle of human life, they visualize the future before one's eyes. Indeed, studying the remains of those who passed before makes the ear hearing and the eye seeing.

For this reason, in many verses of the Quran, the command for travel has been given—but a divine and moral travel, where the heart that seeks lessons emerges from the eye and regards the Arch of Ctesiphon and the palaces of the Pharaohs as a mirror of warning. Sometimes, by way of the Tigris, one should visit Madain and sometimes shed a flood of tears like the Tigris upon the soil of Madain.

From the battlements of the ruined palaces of tyrannical kings, one should hear ever-new counsels, and from within these particles of dust, perceive this melody with the ear of the soul: "Take two or three steps upon us, and shed two or three tears!" [^1] Then, so that the reality of this statement becomes clearer, the Quran says: How many are those who apparently have seeing eyes and hearing ears but are in reality blind and deaf, "For indeed, it is not the eyes that turn blind, but it is the hearts within the breasts that lose their sight" (فَإِنَّهَا لَا تَعْمَى الْأَبْصَارُ وَلَكِنْ تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ).

In reality, those who lose their physical sight are not the blind; sometimes they are enlightened souls more aware than all others. The truly blind are those whose eye of the heart is blinded and who do not perceive the truth!

Therefore, in a narration from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: The worst blindness is the blindness of the heart! (شر العمى، عمى القلب). And: The blindest of blindnesses is the blindness of the heart. (وأعمى العمى عمى القلب) [^2]. In another narration found in the book "Ghawali al-La'ali," we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "When Allah intends good for a servant, He opens the eyes of his heart so that he may witness through them what was hidden from him." (اذا اراد اللَّه بعبد خيرا فتح عين قلبه فيشاهد بها ما كان غائبا عنه) [^3].

As for the question of how the perception of truths is attributed to the hearts located in the breasts, while we know the heart is nothing but a pump for blood circulation, we have answered this in detail in "Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 1" under verse 7 of Surah Al-Baqarah. A summary and condensation of it is as follows:

One of the meanings of "Heart" (Qalb) is the intellect, and one of the meanings of "Breast" (Sadr) is the essence and nature of man. Furthermore, the heart is the manifestation of emotions; whenever a spark of emotion or perception that causes movement and stir appears in the human soul, its first effect appears in this physical heart. The heartbeat changes, blood reaches all particles of the human body with speed, and it grants it fresh vitality and strength. Therefore, if spiritual phenomena are attributed to the heart, it is because its first manifestation in the human body is the heart (reflect on this).

It is noteworthy that the collection of human perceptions in the above verse is attributed to the "Heart" (intellect) and "Ears," indicating that there are only two ways to perceive truths: either a person must have an internal surge from within their soul and personally analyze matters to reach the necessary conclusion, or they must listen to the words of sincere advisors, guides of the path, the Prophets of Allah, and men of truth—or reach the truths through both paths [^1].

The second verse under discussion portrays another face of the ignorance and unawareness of the faithless blind-hearted. It says: "They challenge you to hasten the punishment, saying: 'If you are truthful, then why does the divine punishment not seize us?!'" (وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالْعَذَابِ). In response to them, say: Do not hurry so much, "Allah will never fail His promise!" (وَلَنْ يُخْلِفَ اللَّهُ وَعْدَهُ).

[^1]: Regarding the study of the history of predecessors through travel and "journeying through the earth," we have had a detailed discussion in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 101 onwards (under verse 137 of Surah Al-Imran). [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 508. [^3]: Ibid, p. 509. [^1]: Adapted from Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 14, p. 426.

One who hurries is one who fears that the opportunity will slip away and his resources will end. But for the God who has been and is capable of all things from eternity to infinity, haste is irrelevant, and He is always capable of fulfilling His promises.

To Him, one hour, one day, and one year make no difference: "For indeed, a day with your Lord is like a thousand years of those which you count" (وَإِنَّ يَوْماً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍ مِمَّا تَعُدُّونَ).

Therefore, whether they repeat this statement out of reality or out of mockery and derision, saying "Why does the punishment of God not descend upon us?!", they must know that the punishment awaits them and will come to them sooner or later. If a respite is given, it is an opportunity for awakening and reconsideration; however, they must note that after the descent of punishment, the doors of repentance and return are completely closed, and there is no way to salvation.

Regarding the phrase "وَإِنَّ يَوْماً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍ مِمَّا تَعُدُّونَ", in addition to the above interpretation (the equality of one day and a thousand years before the power of God), other interpretations have also been mentioned.

Among them is that: You may require a thousand years to perform a task, but Allah performs it in one day (or even less); therefore, His punishment does not require many preliminaries.

Another is that one day of the days of the Hereafter is like a thousand years in this world (and its reward and punishment increase in the same proportion). Thus, we read in a narration: "The poor enter Paradise half a day—five hundred years—before the rich!" (ان الفقراء يدخلون الجنة قبل الاغنياء نصف يوم، خمسمائة عام) [^1].

In the final verse, it emphasizes once again the same point that was stressed a few verses prior, warning the stubborn disbelievers thus: "How many a city and settlement did I give respite to while they were oppressive (I gave respite so they might wake up, and when they did not, I gave respite again so they would be immersed in luxury), but then I suddenly seized them with punishment" (وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَمْلَيْتُ لَهَا وَهِيَ ظَالِمَةٌ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهَا).

They too, like you, complained about the delay of punishment and mocked it, taking it as proof of the falsehood of the Prophets' promise, but eventually they were seized, and no matter how much they cried out, their cries reached nowhere.

Indeed, "To Me is the final return" (وَإِلَيَّ الْمَصِيرُ); all lines lead to Allah, and all these properties and riches remain, and He is the heir of all.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٤٩)
Say, ‘O mankind! I am only a manifest warner to you!’ (49)
فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٥٠)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them will be forgiveness and a noble provision. (50)
وَٱلَّذِينَ سَعَوْا۟ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مُعَـٰجِزِينَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٥١)
But as for those who contend with Our signs, seeking to frustrate [their purpose], they shall be the inmates of hell. (51)

Commentary

Noble Provision

Since the preceding verses discussed the disbelievers' haste in seeking divine punishment—a matter that pertains solely to the Will of the Pure Essence of Allah, and even the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has no authority over it—the first verse under discussion states:

"Say: O people! I am only a plain warner to you" (قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّما أَنَا لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

As for whether the punishment and retribution for defying and violating Allah's command will overtake you sooner or later, that does not concern me.

Undoubtedly, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is both a warner and a bearer of glad tidings; however, the emphasis here on "warning" and the omission of glad tidings is due to its suitability for the audience being addressed—stubborn and faithless individuals who went so far as to mock divine punishment.

In the following two verses, He portrays one aspect of glad tidings and one aspect of warning. Since Allah's vast mercy always precedes His wrath and punishment, He speaks first of the glad tidings: "Those who believe and do righteous deeds, for them is forgiveness and a noble provision" (فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَ رِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ‌).

First, they are washed and purified with the water of divine forgiveness and pardon, attaining a peaceful mind and a tranquil conscience in this regard.

Then, they become recipients of His various favors and precious blessings.

"Noble provision" (رِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ‌)—considering that "Karim" (noble/generous) refers to every honorable and highly valuable entity—has a broad meaning that encompasses all precious spiritual and material blessings.

Indeed, the Noble God, in that Noble Abode, bestows various Noble blessings upon His faithful and righteous servants.

Al-Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat: "Karam" is usually applied to good and highly valuable matters that are very significant; therefore, minor acts of kindness are not called "Karam."

And if some have interpreted "noble provision" as continuous, flawless, and perfect sustenance, and others as worthy provision, all these are gathered within that comprehensive and general meaning: things that are highly valuable and significant.

In the next verse, He adds: "But those who strive against Our signs to frustrate them, and imagined they could overcome the definitive Will of the Lord, they are the companions of the Blazing Fire" (وَ الَّذِينَ سَعَوْا فِي آياتِنا مُعاجِزِينَ أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ‌) [1]. Jahim is derived from the root jahm, meaning the intensity of a fire's blaze; it is also used to describe intense anger. Therefore, Jahim refers to a place that has a flaming and blazing fire, and it is an allusion to Hell.

[1]: "Sa'aw" is from the root sa'y, which originally means running, and here it refers to effort and striving in the path of destruction, subversion, and the elimination of divine signs. "Mu'ajizin" is from the root ajz (incapacity), and here it refers to one who wishes to triumph over the infinite power of Allah.

Some commentators have imagined that such a possibility—that someone would want to render Allah incapable or overcome His Will—is inconceivable for anyone, and for this reason, they have interpreted "mu'ajizin" as being directed toward the Prophet or the believers.

However, this expression is used in other verses of the Quran in relation to Allah (Surah Al-Jinn, verse 12, and Surah At-Tawbah, verses 2 and 3), and its meaning is that a person's actions manifest in such a form.
