Commentary

Satanic Whispers in the Efforts of the Prophets

Since the previous verses discussed the efforts and struggles of the polytheists and disbelievers to erase the Divine religion, as well as their mockery and ridicule of it, the verses under discussion warn that these conspiracies of the opponents are not a new phenomenon; these Satanic suggestions have always existed and continue to exist in opposition to the Prophets.

First, It says: "We did not send any Messenger or Prophet before you, but that whenever he desired and drew up a plan to advance His Divine goals, Satan cast suggestions into his plan" (وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ وَلَا نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا إِذَا تَمَنَّىٰ أَلْقَى الشَّيْطَانُ فِي أُمْنِيَّتِهِ).

However, Allah would not leave His Prophet alone against the onslaught of these Satanic suggestions: "Allah abolishes what Satan casts, then Allah establishes His verses" (فَيَنْسَخُ اللَّهُ مَا يُلْقِي الشَّيْطَانُ ثُمَّ يُحْكِمُ اللَّهُ آيَاتِهِ).

And this task is easy for Allah, because "Allah is All-Knowing and All-Wise," and He is aware of all these conspiracies and sinister plots, and He well knows how to neutralize them (وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ).

Yet, these Satanic conspiracies of the opponents always formed a field of testing for the informed, the believers, and the disbelievers. Therefore, in the next verse, It adds: "These events were so that Allah might make what Satan casts a trial for those in whose hearts is a disease and those whose hearts are hardened" (لِيَجْعَلَ مَا يُلْقِي الشَّيْطَانُ فِتْنَةً لِلَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَالْقَاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ).

"And indeed, the wrongdoers are in extreme enmity and opposition, far from the truth" (وَإِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ لَفِي شِقَاقٍ بَعِيدٍ).

Furthermore, "the purpose of this event was so that those who have been given knowledge may distinguish truth from falsehood, and separate Divine programs from Satanic suggestions, and by comparing them, know that the religion of God is the truth and is from your Lord; consequently, they would believe in it and their hearts would become humble before Him" (وَلِيَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ فَيُؤْمِنُوا بِهِ فَتُخْبِتَ لَهُ قُلُوبُهُمْ).

Of course, Allah does not leave these conscious and truth-seeking believers alone on this perilous path; rather, "Allah guides those who believe to a straight path" (وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَهَادِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ).

Key Points

1- What are Satanic Suggestions?

What we have stated above in the commentary of these verses is consistent with the views of a group of researchers. Nevertheless, other possibilities have also been mentioned in the interpretation of the verse:

Among them is that "tamanna" and "umniyyah" mean recitation and reading, as this meaning has occasionally appeared in Arabic poetry. Therefore, the verse (وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ...) says: "All previous Messengers and Prophets, when they recited the words of God to the people, the Satans (especially human Satans) would cast suggestions amidst their words and speak matters to deviate public opinion in order to neutralize their guiding effects. However, Allah would erase and destroy these Satanic suggestions and establish His verses."

Certainly, this interpretation is consistent with the phrase "then Allah establishes His verses" (ثُمَّ يُحْكِمُ اللَّهُ آيَاتِهِ) and is compatible with the legend of the "Gharaniq" (according to some justifications) which will be discussed later. However, the important point is that "tamanna" and "umniyyah" are rarely used to mean recitation, to the extent that they have not been used in this sense in any instance within the verses of the Quran.

The original root of "tamanna," derived from the material "mana" (rhyming with masha), originally means estimation and assumption. If the semen of humans and animals is called "mani," it is because formation takes place through it. If death is called "maniyyah," it is because the estimated term (ajal) of a human arrives then. Desires are called "tamanna" because a person estimates and visualizes them in their mind. The result is that the original root of this word everywhere returns to "estimation, assumption, and visualization."

Of course, "recitation" can be linked to this meaning in a way, by saying: recitation consists of the estimation and visualization of words. However, this is a very distant connection of which few traces are seen in Arabic vocabulary.

But the previous meaning we mentioned in the commentary of the verse (the plans and programs of the Prophets for advancing Divine goals) has a strong affinity with the root meaning of "tamanna."

A third possibility expressed by some commentators regarding the interpretation of the above verse is that it refers to some "Satanic thoughts and whispers" that would occasionally be cast for a fleeting moment among the pure and luminous thoughts of the Prophets. However, because they possessed the station of infallibility ('ismah) and were strengthened by unseen power and Divine aid, Allah would erase these fleeting thoughts and Satanic suggestions from the canvas of their minds and lead them forward on that same straight path.

This interpretation also does not align well with the second and third verses under discussion, because the Quran considers these Satanic suggestions a means of testing for the disbelievers and awareness for the believers, whereas the fleeting thoughts of the Prophets' hearts—which are quickly erased—could not have such an effect.

From the sum of what we have said, it becomes clear that the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all, which in reality refers to the activities of the Satans and their whispers against the constructive programs of the Prophets, as they always wanted to disrupt these programs and estimations with their suggestions, but Allah prevented it.

2- The Fabricated Legend of the Gharaniq!

In some books of the Ahl al-Sunnah, strange narrations are cited here from Ibn Abbas, stating that: The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله was busy reciting Surah "An-Najm" in Mecca. When he reached the verses mentioning the names of the polytheists' idols (أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ اللَّاتَ وَالْعُزَّىٰ وَمَنَاةَ الثَّالِثَةَ الْأُخْرَىٰ), at this moment, Satan caused these two sentences to flow from his tongue:

(Tilka al-gharaniq al-'ula, wa inna shafa'atahunna la-turtaja!)

("These are high-ranking beautiful birds, and their intercession is hoped for!") [^1] At this, the polytheists became happy and said, "Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله has never mentioned the names of our gods favorably until now." At this point, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله prostrated, and they prostrated as well. Gabriel descended and warned the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "I did not bring these two sentences to you; this was from the suggestions of Satan." At this time, the verses under discussion (وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ وَلَا نَبِيٍّ...) were revealed and warned the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers [^1]!

Although a group of opponents of Islam, in order to weaken the Prophet's programs and thinking they had found a good pretext, have narrated this incident with great exaggeration and added many embellishments, numerous pieces of evidence show that this is a fabricated and forged hadith, created by Satan-like individuals to make the Quran and the words of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله appear discredited. This is because:

Firstly—According to researchers, the narrators of this hadith are weak and unreliable individuals, and its issuance from Ibn Abbas is by no means certain. According to "Muhammad ibn Ishaq," this hadith is among the fabrications of the Zanadiqa (heretics), and he has written a book on this matter [^2].

Secondly—Numerous hadiths regarding the revelation of Surah An-Najm and the subsequent prostration of the Prophet and the Muslims have been narrated in various books, and in none of these hadiths is there any mention of the legend of the Gharaniq. This indicates that this sentence was added to it later ^3.

Thirdly—The opening verses of Surah An-Najm explicitly invalidate these superstitions where it says: وَمَا يَنْطِقُ عَنِ الْهَوَىٰ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْيٌ يُوحَىٰ: "The Prophet does not speak out of desire; what he says is nothing but a Divine revelation." How is this verse compatible with the above legend?

Fourthly—The verses that follow the mention of the idols' names in this Surah are all in condemnation of the idols and their ugliness and baseness, explicitly stating: "These are but names which you have named, you and your fathers, for which Allah has sent down no authority. They follow nothing but conjecture and what their souls desire, while there has surely come to them guidance from their Lord" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْمَاءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَا أَنْتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُمْ مَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ بِهَا مِنْ سُلْطَانٍ إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَمَا تَهْوَى الْأَنْفُسُ وَلَقَدْ جَاءَهُمْ مِنْ رَبِّهِمُ الْهُدَىٰ).

With such severe condemnations, how is it possible that a few sentences prior, the idols were praised? Furthermore, the Quran has explicitly reminded that Allah protects all of it from any kind of distortion, deviation, and loss, as we read in verse 9 of Surah Al-Hijr: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Message [i.e., the Quran], and indeed, We will be its guardian" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ).

Fifthly—The Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله struggle against idols and idol-worship was an irreconcilable, persistent, and non-stop struggle from the beginning to the end of his life. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله showed in practice that he would not show any compromise, settlement, or flexibility toward idols and idol-worship—even in the most difficult circumstances. How is it possible for such words to flow from his blessed tongue?

Sixthly—Even those who do not consider the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to be from God and are not Muslims consider him a thoughtful, aware, and resourceful man who achieved the greatest victories under his management. Would such a person, whose primary slogan was "There is no god but Allah" and who waged an irreconcilable struggle against every kind of polytheism and idol-worship, and who showed in practice that he was not willing to make any compromise regarding the issue of idols—how is it possible for him to abandon his primary program and praise the idols in such a way?!

From the sum of this discussion, it becomes clearly evident that the legend of the Gharaniq is a fabrication of clumsy enemies and ignorant opponents who forged such a baseless hadith to weaken the position of the Quran and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Therefore, all Islamic researchers, both Shi'a and Ahl al-Sunnah, have strongly rejected and weakened this hadith and attributed it to the fabrications of forgers [^1].

Of course, some commentators have mentioned a justification for this hadith which, assuming the origin of the hadith were proven, would be worth considering: "The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله used to recite the verses of the Quran slowly and deliberately, sometimes pausing for moments in between so that the hearts of the people could absorb them well. When he was busy reciting the verses of Surah An-Najm and reached the verse 'Have you then considered Al-Lat and Al-Uzza, and Manat, the other third?' some Satan-like individuals (stubborn polytheists) took advantage of the opportunity and shouted the sentence 'Those are the high-ranking birds, and indeed their intercession is hoped for' in the middle with a specific tone, both to mock the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله words and to confuse the people. However, the subsequent verses answered them well and severely condemned idol-worship" [^2].

From here it becomes clear that those who have tried to interpret the story of the Gharaniq as a kind of flexibility on the part of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله toward the idol-worshippers—due to their stubbornness and the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله desire to attract them toward Islam—have committed a great error. It shows that these "justifiers" have not understood the position of Islam and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding idols and idol-worship, and have not seen the historical documents stating that the enemies were willing to pay any price to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in this regard and he did not accept, nor did he deviate an iota from his program; or they are intentionally feigning ignorance.

3- The Difference Between "Rasul" and "Nabi"

There is much discussion regarding the difference between "Rasul" (Messenger) and "Nabi" (Prophet). What seems most appropriate is that "Rasul" refers to those Prophets who were commissioned to propagate and invite to their religion, and as we read in their biographies, they spared no effort or struggle in this path and embraced all kinds of difficulties.

However, "Nabi," as is evident from the original root of this word, is one who gives news of Divine revelation, even if he is not commissioned for widespread propagation. In reality, he is like a physician whom the suffering seek out to find medicine and treatment. We know that the conditions of environments and Prophets differed, and each had a specific mission [^1].

Commentary

The Provision of Excellence (Rizq Hasan)

Following the previous verses which spoke of the efforts and struggles of the opponents to erase the Divine signs, the verses under discussion point to the continuation of these efforts by stubborn and prejudiced individuals.

First, it says: "The disbelievers will remain in doubt about it (the Quran and the monotheistic faith) until the Hour comes upon them suddenly, or there comes to them the punishment of a barren day—a day when they are unable to make amends" (وَ لا يَزالُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْهُ حَتَّى تَأْتِيَهُمُ السَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذابُ يَوْمٍ عَقِيمٍ‌).

It is obvious that the term "disbelievers" here does not refer to all of them, as many were awakened along the way and joined the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the ranks of the Muslims. Rather, it refers to their leaders and those stubborn, extremely prejudiced, and spiteful individuals who never believed and constantly continued their sabotage.

The word "miryah" (مِرْيَةٍ), which means doubt and uncertainty, indicates that they never had certainty in opposing the Quran and Islam—even if they expressed such in their words—but had at least descended to the stage of doubt. However, their prejudices did not allow them to study further and find the truth.

Regarding the word "Sa'ah" (السَّاعَةُ), although some have suggested it might mean the moment of death or the like, the subsequent verses show that it refers to the end of the world and standing on the threshold of the Resurrection, especially since it is accompanied by the word "baghtatan" (بَغْتَةً - suddenly).

The meaning of "the punishment of a barren day" (عَذابُ يَوْمٍ عَقِيمٍ‌) is the punishment of the Day of Resurrection. Describing the Day of Resurrection as "aqim" (barren/sterile) is an allusion to the fact that they have no other day behind it to rise and compensate for the past or to create a change in their destiny.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 14, p. 148

  • Then, referring to the absolute sovereignty of the Lord on the Day of Resurrection, it says:

"Sovereignty and dominion on that day belong to Allah alone" (الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ‌).

Of course, this is not exclusive to the Day of Resurrection; today and always, the absolute Ruler and Owner is Allah. However, since in this world there are other owners and rulers—even though their realm of governance is very limited and weak, and possesses a formal and outward aspect—this very matter might cause the association of the thought that there is a ruler or owner other than Allah. But on the scene of the Resurrection, where all these matters are dismantled, this truth becomes more apparent than ever that He alone is the Ruler and Owner.

In other words, there are two types of sovereignty and ownership: true sovereignty, which is the sovereignty of the Creator over the created, and conventional or contractual sovereignty, which is common among people. In this world, both exist, but in the Hereafter, all contractual and conventional governments are dismantled, and only the true sovereignty of the Creator of the world remains [1]. In any case, since He is the true Owner, He will also be the true Ruler; therefore, He will judge and rule among all human beings—both believers and disbelievers—and the result is what the Quran states following this: "Those who have believed and done righteous deeds will be in the Gardens of Bliss," gardens...

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 14, p. 433.

...where all blessings are gathered and every good and bounty they desire is present (فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ‌).

"But those who disbelieved and denied Our signs, for them there is a humiliating punishment" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا فَأُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ‌). What an expressive and vivid description! A punishment that makes them lowly and insignificant, and in the face of all that arrogance, self-superiority, and pride against God's creation, it drags them to the lowest stage of humiliation. We know that the descriptions of "punishment" as "painful" (alim), "great" (azim), and "humiliating" (muhin) mentioned in various verses of the Quran are each proportionate to the type of sin committed by the arrogant ones.

It is noteworthy that regarding the believers, it mentions two things: "faith" and "righteous deeds," and in contrast, regarding the disbelievers, it mentions "disbelief" and "denial of Divine signs," which in reality are each a combination of internal belief and its external and practical effects, because human actions often stem from a root of thought and belief.

And since the previous verses spoke of the emigrants (Muhajirun) who were driven out of their homes and dwellings for the sake of the name of Allah and in support of His religion, the next verse mentions them as a distinguished group, saying: "Those who emigrated in the way of Allah, then were killed or died (a natural death), Allah will surely provide for them a goodly provision, and they will enjoy special blessings, for He is indeed the best of providers" (وَ الَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ قُتِلُوا أَوْ ماتُوا لَيَرْزُقَنَّهُمُ اللَّهُ رِزْقاً حَسَناً وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُوَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ‌).

Some commentators have said: "Rizq Hasan" (goodly provision) refers to blessings that, when a person's eye falls upon them, they become so attracted that they cannot take their eyes off them to look at anything else, and only Allah has the power to give such a provision to someone.

Some scholars have mentioned a reason for revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) for this verse, the summary of which is: "When the emigrants came to Medina, some of them died a natural death, while others drank the nectar of martyrdom. At this time, a group attributed all the virtue to the martyrs. The above verse was revealed and introduced both as recipients of the best Divine blessings." Therefore, some commentators have concluded from this expression that the important thing is giving one's life in the way of Allah, whether it be through martyrdom or through natural death; whoever dies for Allah and in the way of Allah is included in the reward of the martyrs (The one killed in the way of Allah and the one who dies in the way of Allah is a martyr) [1].

And in the final verse, it recounts an example of this "goodly provision," saying: "He will surely admit them to an entrance with which they will be well-pleased" (لَيُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ مُدْخَلًا يَرْضَوْنَهُ‌).

If in this world they were painfully exiled and expelled from their homes and shelters, Allah will place them in the other world in a home and shelter that is satisfactory to them in every respect, and in this way, He compensates for their sacrifice and devotion in the most excellent manner.

And at the end, it says: "Allah is All-Knowing and aware of the deeds of these servants of His, and at the same time, He is Forbearing (Halim)" and does not hasten in punishment and retribution, so that the believers may be nurtured and tested in this field of trial (وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَعَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ‌).

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 7, p. 4480.

Ayahs

۞ ذَٰلِكَ وَمَنْ عَاقَبَ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبَ بِهِۦ ثُمَّ بُغِىَ عَلَيْهِ لَيَنصُرَنَّهُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ (٦٠)
So will it be; and whoever retaliates with the like of what he has been made to suffer, and is aggressed against [again] thereafter, Allah will surely help him. Indeed Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving. (60)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (٦١)
So will it be, because Allah makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and because Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (61)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ هُوَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (٦٢)
So will it be, because Allah is the Reality, and what they invoke besides Him is nullity, and because Allah is the All-exalted, the All-great. (62)

Occasion of Revelation

It is mentioned in some narrations that a group of the polytheists of Mecca encountered the Muslims when only two nights remained from the end of the month of Muharram. The polytheists said to one another: "The companions of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) do not engage in battle during the month of Muharram and consider war to be forbidden (Haram)." For this reason, they initiated an attack. The Muslims first earnestly requested them not to start a war during this sacred month, but they did not listen. Consequently, the Muslims were forced to take action to defend themselves; they fought bravely, and Allah granted them victory. (The first verse mentioned above was revealed here) [1]

Commentary

Who are the Victorious?

In the preceding verses, the discussion was about the emigrants (Muhajirin) in the way of Allah and the promises of great rewards that Allah has given them in the Hereafter.

Lest it be imagined that the divine promise is exclusive to the Hereafter, the first verse under discussion speaks of their victory—under the shadow of divine grace—in this world. It states: "That is so; and whoever retaliates with the like of that with which he was afflicted, and then he is oppressed [again], Allah will surely help him" (ذلِكَ وَ مَنْ عاقَبَ بِمِثْلِ ما عُوقِبَ بِهِ ثُمَّ بُغِيَ عَلَيْهِ لَيَنْصُرَنَّهُ اللَّهُ‌).

This is an indication that defense against oppression and tyranny is a natural right, and everyone is permitted to undertake it.

However, the expression "with the like" (بِمِثْلِ) is an emphasis on the fact that one must not exceed the limits.

The phrase "then he is oppressed [again]" (ثُمَّ بُغِيَ عَلَيْهِ‌) also indicates that if a person, while in the position of defending themselves, is subjected to further oppression, Allah has promised to help them. Accordingly, one who remains silent from the beginning, submits to oppression and tyranny, and takes no effective step toward self-defense, has not been promised help by Allah.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan and al-Durr al-Manthur, under the verses being discussed.

The divine promise is reserved for those who mobilize all their strength to defend themselves against oppressors and tyrants, yet are still subjected to oppression by the enemy.

And since retribution and punishment must always be tempered with pardon and mercy—so that those who have become regretful of their actions and have surrendered may come under its cover—the verse concludes by saying: "Indeed, Allah is Pardoning and Forgiving" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ).

This is exactly like the verses of Qisas (retribution), which on one hand allow the heirs of the victim to seek retribution, and on the other hand, place the instruction of forgiveness alongside it as a virtue (for those who are worthy of pardon).

And since the promise of victory and help is encouraging and effective only when it comes from a powerful and capable person, the next verse recounts a glimpse of Allah's infinite power in the expanse of the universe: "That is because Allah causes the night to enter the day and causes the day to enter the night" (He constantly decreases from one and increases the other according to a specific system—an enduring and perfectly calculated system that has been established for thousands, rather than millions, of years). (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ وَ يُولِجُ النَّهارَ فِي اللَّيْلِ‌).

"Yuliju" is derived from the root "Iylaj," which originally comes from "Wuluj," meaning "to enter." This expression, as we have said, refers to the gradual, perfectly organized, and calculated changes of night and day throughout the different seasons of the year, where one is diminished and the other is increased. However, there is also the possibility that it refers to the issue of sunrise and sunset, which, due to the specific atmospheric conditions (the air surrounding the Earth), does not occur suddenly. Rather, from the beginning of dawn, the sun's rays hit the upper layers of the atmosphere and slowly transfer to the lower layers; it is as if the day gradually enters the night, and the army of light overcomes the forces of darkness. Conversely, at sunset, the light first withdraws from the lower layer of the atmosphere and the air becomes slightly dark, and gradually from the higher layers, until the last ray of the sun is withdrawn and the army of darkness conquers everywhere. If this were not the case, sunrise and sunset would occur in a fleeting moment, and the sudden transition from night to day and day to night would be harmful to humans both physically and mentally, and from the perspective of the social order, this rapid and unannounced change would create numerous problems.

There is no obstacle to the above verse referring to both interpretations.

At the end of the verse, it says: "And that Allah is All-Hearing, All-Seeing" (وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ).

He hears the plea for help from the believers and is aware of their state and their work, and when necessary, His grace hastens to their aid, just as He is aware of the actions and intentions of the enemies of Truth.

The last verse under discussion is, in fact, a reason for what preceded it. It says: "That is because Allah is the Truth, and that what they invoke besides Him is falsehood, and that Allah is the Most High, the Grand" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ وَ أَنَّ ما يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ هُوَ الْباطِلُ وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ).

If you see the armies of Truth becoming victorious, falsehood retreating, and the grace of Allah hastening to help the believers while leaving the disbelievers alone, it is because they are falsehood and these [believers] are Truth; they are contrary to the system of the universe and their fate is annihilation and non-existence, while these are in harmony with the laws of the universe.

Fundamentally, Allah is the Truth and everything other than Him is falsehood, and all humans and beings who are in some way connected to Allah are "truthful" to the same extent, and to the extent they are estranged from Him, they are "falsehood." [1] The word "Ali" (High), which is derived from the root "Uluw," means high in status; it is also applied to one who is powerful and dominant, such that no one has the power to resist His will.

"Kabir" (Grand) is also an indication of the greatness of the Lord's knowledge and power. One who possesses these attributes is well able to help His friends and crush the enemies; therefore, His friends must be heartened by His promises.

[1]: In Tafsir al-Mizan, we read: The application of "Truth" to Allah and "Falsehood" to others is either because He is the Truth that is in no way mixed with falsehood, or because He is the Truth who is independent in His truthfulness, while others take on the color of truth because of their relationship with Him.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَتُصْبِحُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مُخْضَرَّةً ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ (٦٣)
Have you not regarded that Allah sends down water from the sky, whereupon the earth turns green? Indeed Allah is all-attentive, all-aware. (63)
لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٦٤)
To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, Indeed Allah is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (64)
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلْفُلْكَ تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ وَيُمْسِكُ ٱلسَّمَآءَ أَن تَقَعَ عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِٱلنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٦٥)
Have you not regarded that Allah has disposed for you [r benefit] whatever there is in the earth, and [that] the ships sail at sea by His command, and He sustains the sky lest it should fall on the earth, excepting [when it does so] by His leave? Indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to mankind. (65)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْيَاكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ (٦٦)
It is He who gave you life then He makes you die, then He brings you to life. Indeed man is very ungrateful. (66)

Commentary

Signs of Allah in the Arena of Existence

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the infinite power of Allah and His Truth. In the verses under discussion, various signs of this extensive power and absolute Truth are described.

First, it says: "Do you not see that Allah sends down water from the sky, and the earth becomes green and lush because of it?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَتُصْبِحُ الْأَرْضُ مُخْضَرَّةً).

The earth, from which the signs of life had departed and which possessed a grim, ugly, and dark countenance, is brought back to life by the descent of life-giving raindrops; the signs of life appear within it, and the smile of existence becomes manifest upon its face.

Indeed, "Allah, who creates all this life and vitality with such simplicity, is All-Subtle and All-Aware" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ).

"Latif" (All-Subtle) is derived from the root lutf, meaning a very delicate and fine action; if special divine mercies are called lutf, it is because of this inherent delicacy.

"Khabir" (All-Aware) refers to One who is cognizant of the most minute details.

Allah being Latif necessitates that He nurtures the tiny and seemingly insignificant embryos of plants hidden within seeds deep inside the soil. He brings them—which are the pinnacle of delicacy and softness—out from the depths of the dark earth against the law of gravity, exposing them to the radiance of the sun and the blowing of the breeze, eventually transforming them into a fruitful plant or a sturdy tree.

If He did not send the raindrops, and if the environment of the seed and the surrounding soil did not become soft, mild, and "subtle" (latif), they would never have the power to germinate and grow. However, through this rain, He granted grace and softness to that dry and harsh environment so it would be ready for movement and growth.

At the same time, He is Aware (Khabir) of all the needs and requirements of this weak seed, from the beginning of its movement under the soil until the time it reaches toward the sky.

Allah sends the rain according to His Subtlety (Lutf), and according to His being All-Aware (Khabir), He ordains a measure for it; for if it exceeds the limit, it results in floods and destruction, and if it is less than the limit, it results in drought and withering. This is the meaning of Allah being Latif and Khabir.

In Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 18, we also read: وَ أَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً بِقَدَرٍ فَأَسْكَنَّاهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ: "And We sent down water from the sky in a precise measure, then We caused it to settle in the earth." [1]

Another sign cited for His infinite power and the Truth of His Pure Essence is: "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth" (لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ). He is the Creator of all, and He is the Owner of all; for this reason, He has power over all things.

[1]: In Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 386, under verse 103 of Surah Al-An'am, we had an interesting discussion regarding "Allah being Latif"; please refer there.

And for this same reason, "He alone is the Self-Sufficient and Needless in the universe, and worthy of all praise and glorification" (وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ).

The connection between these two attributes (Ghani - Self-Sufficient and Hamid - Praiseworthy) is a very calculated one. Firstly, there are many who are wealthy (ghani) but are stingy, exploitative, monopolistic, and drowned in negligence and pride; therefore, the title of being "wealthy" sometimes evokes these qualities. However, Allah’s being Self-Sufficient is coupled with His grace, generosity, and bounty toward His servants, which makes Him worthy of praise and glorification.

Secondly, the self-sufficiency of others is superficial; if they possess any bounty or generosity, it is not truly from themselves, as Allah has placed all blessings and means at their disposal. The One who is Self-Sufficient by Essence (Ghani bid-Dhat) and worthy of every type of praise and laudation is only His Pure Essence.

Thirdly, when other "needless" beings do something, they ultimately derive some benefit from it for themselves. The only One who gives without measure and gains no benefit for Himself, but rather desires to bestow bounty upon His servants, is Him; for this reason, He is the most deserving of all for praise and laudation.

Again, pointing to another example of this infinite power in the context of subjecting creatures for the benefit of humans, He says: "Do you not see that Allah has subjected to you whatever is on the earth?" and placed all its blessings and possibilities at your disposal so that you may benefit from them however you wish? (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ).

"And also the ships, as they sail upon the sea by His command, cleaving the waters and moving toward their destinations?" (وَ الْفُلْكَ تَجْرِي فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ).

Furthermore, "Allah holds the sky in its place so that it does not fall upon the earth except by His permission" (وَ يُمْسِكُ السَّماءَ أَنْ تَقَعَ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ).

On one hand, He has set each of the celestial bodies in motion in its own orbit and made the "centrifugal" force exactly equal to their "gravitational" force, so that each moves in its orbit without any change in their distances occurring, and no collision happens between the spheres.

On the other hand, He created the Earth's atmosphere in such a way that it does not allow wandering meteors to strike the earth and cause distress or destruction for its inhabitants.

Indeed, it is His mercy and grace toward His servants that He has created the cradle of the earth so safe, secure, and free from any danger, to be a place of comfort and tranquility for His servants; neither do wandering celestial stones fall upon the earth, nor do other spheres collide with it.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed, Allah is Kind and Merciful toward the people" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِالنَّاسِ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Finally, in the last verse, it speaks of the Lord's power regarding the most important issue of the universe—the issue of life and death—and says: "He is the One who gave you life" (you were lifeless dust, and He clothed you in the garment of life) (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي أَحْياكُمْ). "Then, after the completion of your life cycle, He causes you to die" (and you return to the same dust from which you arose) (ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ).

"And once again, on the Day of Resurrection, He will grant you a new life" (and you will rise from the dead earth, ready for accounting and recompense) (ثُمَّ يُحيِيكُمْ).

Yet, despite this, "this human being is ungrateful and thankless in the face of all these blessings that Allah has bestowed upon him in the earth and the sky, in body and soul; and despite seeing all these clear signs, they deny His Pure Essence" (إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَكَفُورٌ).

Key Points

1. The Unique Attributes of the Lord

In the above verses and the two verses preceding them, fourteen sections of Allah's attributes are mentioned in sequence (two attributes at the end of each verse): All-Knowing and Forbearing—All-Pardoning and All-Forgiving—All-Hearing and All-Seeing—Most High and Most Great—All-Subtle and All-Aware—Self-Sufficient and Praiseworthy—Kind and Merciful. Each pair of these attributes is harmonious and complementary to the other: Allah’s pardon with His forgiveness, His being All-Hearing with His being All-Seeing, His high station with His greatness, His subtlety with His awareness, His self-sufficiency with His being praiseworthy, and finally, His kindness with His mercy—all are harmonious. At the same time, each of them is perfectly suited to the specific discussion raised in that verse. Since we have previously discussed them under the verses themselves, we see no need for repetition.

2. The Above Verses are Evidence of Allah's Power

Just as the above verses are evidence of Allah's power and an emphasis on the divine promises of victory for the believing servants, they are also a sign of the Truth of His Pure Essence, which was emphasized in previous verses. They are also evidence for Monotheism (Tawhid) and evidence for the Resurrection (Ma'ad); for the issue of the revival of dead lands through green plants in the light of falling rain, as well as the issue of the initial life and death of man, is a living witness that He is capable of bringing man to life once more and clothing him in the garment of existence, just as many verses of the Quran argue for the Resurrection using these same matters.

Furthermore, the phrase إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَكَفُورٌ, considering that kafur is an intensive form, refers to the disbelief and denial of stubborn humans who, even after witnessing all these signs of Allah's greatness, still choose the path of denial. Or, it refers to the extreme ungratefulness of such individuals whose existence is drowned in His blessings, yet they are neither in a state of thanking the Benefactor nor recognizing Him.

3. The Subjection of Creatures of the Earth and Sky

As we have previously pointed out, the subjection of these matters for man is in the sense that Allah has made them servants to man and placed them in the path of his interests (a detailed explanation of this subject was provided in Volume 11 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 167 onwards—under verses 12 to 14 of Surah An-Nahl, and also in Volume 10, page 120 onwards, under verse 2 of Surah Ar-Ra'd).

And if we see that among the earthly blessings, the movement of ships upon the surface of the oceans is specifically mentioned, it is because these ships, in the past and present, have been and are the most important means of communication and transport for people and goods from one point to another. No means of transport has yet been able to take the place of ships in this regard.

Certainly, if one day all ships on the surface of the oceans were to stop moving, human life would be completely disrupted, as land routes do not have the capacity or reach to transport all these materials and goods. Especially in our age and time, considering that the most important means for the movement of human industrial life is oil, and the most important means for transporting oil from one point to others are those very ships, the importance of this great divine blessing becomes more apparent; for sometimes "ten thousand vehicles" cannot do the work of a single giant oil tanker! And the transport of oil through pipelines is only possible for limited parts of the world.

Ayahs

لِّكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنسَكًا هُمْ نَاسِكُوهُ ۖ فَلَا يُنَـٰزِعُنَّكَ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ ۚ وَٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٦٧)
For every nation We have appointed rites [of worship] which they observe; so let them not dispute with you concerning your religion, and invite to your Lord. Indeed, you are on a straight guidance. (67)
وَإِن جَـٰدَلُوكَ فَقُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٦٨)
But if they dispute with you, say, ‘Allah knows best what you are doing. (68)
ٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ (٦٩)
Allah will judge between you on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which you used to differ. (69)
أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ فِى كِتَـٰبٍ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (٧٠)
Do you not know that Allah knows whatever there is in the heaven and the earth? All that is in a Book. That is indeed easy for Allah.’ (70)

Commentary

Every Nation Has a Rite of Worship

In the preceding discussions, we had conversations regarding the polytheists. Since the polytheists in particular, and the opponents of Islam in general, engaged in disputes and arguments with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the new issues and rulings of Islam—viewing the abrogation and alteration of parts of the rulings of previous religions as a point of weakness for the Islamic Sharia—whereas these changes were not only not a weakness but were considered part of the program for the evolution of religions, the first verse under discussion states: "For every nation We have appointed a rite of worship which they are to observe" (لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنا مَنْسَكاً هُمْ ناسِكُوهُ) [^1].

Manasik: As we have mentioned previously, it is the plural of mansak, meaning worship in an absolute sense. Here, it may encompass all divine and religious programs. Therefore, the verse speaks to the reality that previous nations each had a program specific to themselves, which was the most perfect program under those specific conditions of time, place, and other factors. However, certainly, with the changing of those conditions, it was necessary for newer rulings to take their place.

Consequently, following this statement, it adds: "So let them not dispute with you in this matter" (فَلا يُنازِعُنَّكَ فِي الْأَمْرِ).

"And invite [them] to your Lord; indeed, you are upon a straight guidance" (وَ ادْعُ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ إِنَّكَ لَعَلى‌ هُدىً مُسْتَقِيمٍ).

Never let their baseless conversations and objections have the slightest effect on your morale, for your invitation is toward Allah, your path is guidance, and your way is straight! Describing "guidance" as being "straight" either serves an aspect of emphasis or points to the fact that guidance toward a destination can occur through various ways—paths near and far, straight and crooked—but divine guidance is through the nearest and straightest path.

"But if they still continue to argue and dispute with you, and your words do not affect their hearts, say in response to them: Allah is most knowing of what you do" (وَ إِنْ جادَلُوكَ فَقُلِ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ).

"Allah will judge between you on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which you used to differ" (And on the scene of the Resurrection, which is the scene of returning to monotheism and unity and the removal of differences, He will make the realities manifest to all of you) (اللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كُنْتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ) [^1].

And since judgment and arbitration on the Day of Resurrection regarding the differences and deeds of the servants requires vast knowledge and awareness of all of them, the final verse under discussion points to the infinite knowledge of Allah, saying: "Do you not know that Allah knows what is in the heaven and the earth?" (أَ لَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

[^1]: A group of commentators have considered these verses to be an allusion to the response to an objection raised by the polytheists, who said: "How is it that you eat the meat of animals that have been slaughtered, but you do not eat the meat of carrion (dead animals), while you killed the first and Allah killed the second!" These verses were revealed to answer them. However, it seems very unlikely that the above verses refer to such a matter, because eating carrion meat—which is spoiled and harmful meat—was apparently not permitted or lawful in any Sharia for the Quran to endorse it and say that every Sharia had its own program.

Yes, "Indeed, all that is in a Record" (إِنَّ ذلِكَ فِي كِتابٍ).

The book of Allah's infinite knowledge, the book of the world of existence and the world of cause and effect—a world in which nothing is lost or destroyed, but rather constantly changes form. Even the waves of a faint voice that rose from a human throat thousands of years ago have not been completely destroyed and always exist in this space. This is a very precise and comprehensive book in which everything is recorded.

In other words, all of these are recorded in the "Preserved Tablet" (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz), the tablet of Divine Knowledge, and all these beings with all their characteristics and details are present before Him.

Therefore, in the final sentence, He says: "Indeed, that is easy for Allah," because all beings with all their characteristics are present before Him (إِنَّ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ).

[^1]: The address in this verse may be directed specifically toward the opponents of Islam and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; therefore, the phrase "Allah will judge between you..." is part of what the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is told to say. It is also possible that the audience includes both the Muslims and the opponents, and thus, this verse is an independent statement from Allah directed toward everyone.

Ayahs

وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا وَمَا لَيْسَ لَهُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۗ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِن نَّصِيرٍ (٧١)
They worship besides Allah that for which He has not sent down any authority, and of which they have no knowledge. The wrongdoers will have no helper. (71)
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ تَعْرِفُ فِى وُجُوهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلْمُنكَرَ ۖ يَكَادُونَ يَسْطُونَ بِٱلَّذِينَ يَتْلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ۗ قُلْ أَفَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِشَرٍّ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمُ ۗ ٱلنَّارُ وَعَدَهَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٧٢)
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, you perceive denial on the faces of the faithless: they would almost pounce upon those who recite Our signs to them. Say, ‘Shall I inform you about something worse than that? The Fire which Allah has promised the faithless, and it is an evil destination.’ (72)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ضُرِبَ مَثَلٌ فَٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَن يَخْلُقُوا۟ ذُبَابًا وَلَوِ ٱجْتَمَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ ۖ وَإِن يَسْلُبْهُمُ ٱلذُّبَابُ شَيْـًٔا لَّا يَسْتَنقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ ۚ ضَعُفَ ٱلطَّالِبُ وَٱلْمَطْلُوبُ (٧٣)
O people! Listen to a parable that is being drawn: Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah will never create [even] a fly even if they all rallied to do so! And if a fly should take away something from them, they can not recover that from it. Feeble is the seeker and the sought! (73)
مَا قَدَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَقَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ (٧٤)
They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. (74)

Commentary

Deities Weaker Than a Fly!

In these verses—consistent with the previous discussions regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) and polytheism (Shirk)—the discourse returns to the polytheists and their erroneous practices.

Since one of the clearest proofs of the invalidity of polytheism and idol worship is that there is no intellectual or traditional evidence to justify such an act, the first verse states: "And they worship besides Allah that for which He has sent down no authority" (وَ يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ سُلْطاناً).

In reality, this is a refutation of the belief of the idolaters who claimed that God had granted them permission for idol worship and that these idols were intercessors before Him.

Then it adds: "and that of which they have no knowledge" (وَ ما لَيْسَ لَهُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ).

This means they possess absolutely no proof or argument for their actions, neither through Divine instruction nor through the path of reason.

It is evident that anyone who does not rely on clear evidence in their beliefs and actions is an oppressor; they have oppressed both themselves and others. When they are caught in the grip of Divine punishment, no one will have the power to defend them. Therefore, at the end of the verse, it says:

"And for the wrongdoers there is no helper" (وَ ما لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ نَصِيرٍ).

Some commentators have suggested that "Nasir" (helper) here means "proof and evidence," as the true helper is indeed the truth itself [^1]. It is also possible that "Nasir" refers to a "guide," serving as a complement to the preceding discussion—meaning they have neither Divine authority, nor intellectual proof they reached themselves, nor a leader, guide, or teacher to assist them on this path, because they are oppressors and are not submissive to the Truth.

These three interpretations are not mutually exclusive, although the first interpretation appears more evident.

Next, the verse points to the reaction of the idolaters toward the signs of Allah and the intensity of their stubbornness and prejudice in a short sentence, saying: "And when Our clear verses—the benefit of which is easy for any person of intellect—are recited to them, you will recognize denial in the faces of those who disbelieve" (وَ إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ تَعْرِفُ فِي وُجُوهِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الْمُنْكَرَ) [^2]. In fact, upon hearing these clear verses, a conflict arises between the living logic of the Quran and their ignorant prejudices. Since they are unwilling to submit to the Truth, the signs of this conflict involuntarily appear on their faces. Furthermore, due to the intensity of their prejudice and stubbornness, "they are almost ready to spring upon those who recite Our verses to them!" (يَكادُونَ يَسْطُونَ بِالَّذِينَ يَتْلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا).

"Yastun" is derived from the root "Satwat," meaning to raise the hand and attack the opponent. Originally—as Raghib states in Al-Mufradat—it referred to a horse rising on its hind legs and lifting its forelegs, and was later applied to any act of rising up in aggression.

However, if a person thinks logically, when they hear something they disagree with, they neither scowl nor respond with clenched fists; rather, they refute it with logical discourse. These irrational reactions of the disbelievers are themselves clear proof that they follow no logic or reason; only ignorance and fanaticism govern their existence.

It is noteworthy that the phrase "يَكادُونَ يَسْطُونَ" (they are almost ready to spring), consisting of two present-tense verbs, indicates the continuity of this "state of aggression and hostility" within them. Sometimes circumstances allowed them to manifest it physically, and at other times when conditions did not permit, the readiness to attack remained within them, and as we say, they would writhe in frustration because they were unable to strike.

In the face of these illogical people, the Quran commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Say: Shall I inform you of something worse than that? The Fire!" (قُلْ أَ فَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِشَرٍّ مِنْ ذلِكُمُ النَّارُ) [^1].

[^1]: Al-Mizan and Tafsir al-Fakhr al-Razi under the verse in question. [^2]: The word "Munkar" is a mim-infinitive meaning "denial." Since denial is an internal state and cannot be directly seen, it refers here to the signs and symptoms of denial.

That is to say: if in your view these clear Divine verses are "evil" because they do not align with your deviant and incorrect thoughts, I will introduce to you something truly worse: the painful Divine punishment that will eventually seize you because of this stubbornness and enmity.

"Which Allah has promised to those who disbelieve" (وَعَدَهَا اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

"And wretched is the destination!" (وَ بِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ).

In truth, for these fire-tempered aggressors whose inner selves are constantly ablaze with the flames of fanaticism and stubbornness, there is no response but the Fire of Hell! For Divine punishment always bears a close resemblance to the nature of the sin and rebellion.

In the next verse, it presents a fascinating and eloquent depiction of the state of idols and fabricated deities, their weakness and inability, revealing the falsehood of the polytheists' beliefs in the clearest way. Addressing all people, it says: "O mankind, an example is presented, so listen to it" (and reflect upon it deeply) (يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ ضُرِبَ مَثَلٌ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ).

"Indeed, those you invoke besides Allah will never create a fly, even if they gathered together for that purpose" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ لَنْ يَخْلُقُوا ذُباباً وَ لَوِ اجْتَمَعُوا لَهُ).

All the idols, all their deities, and even all the scientists, thinkers, and inventors of humanity, if they were to join hands, are incapable of creating a single fly.

Therefore, how can you place these on the same level as the Great Lord, who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth and thousands upon thousands of species of living beings in the seas, deserts, forests, and the depths of the earth? The God who has placed life in various forms and exquisite, diverse appearances, each of which leaves man in awe and admiration—where are those weak deities compared to this Powerful and Wise Creator?

Then it adds that not only are they unable to create a fly, but they are also helpless in confronting one, for "if the fly should steal anything from them, they cannot recover it from him!" (وَ إِنْ يَسْلُبْهُمُ الذُّبابُ شَيْئاً لا يَسْتَنْقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ).

A creature so weak and helpless that it is defeated even in a struggle with a fly—what place does it have to be considered the master of one's destiny and the solver of problems?

Indeed, "Weak are the seeker and the sought" (ضَعُفَ الطَّالِبُ وَ الْمَطْلُوبُ).

We read in narrations: The polytheists of the Quraish would smear the idols they had gathered around the Kaaba with musk, ambergris, and sometimes with saffron or honey. They would circle them shouting, "Labbayk Allahumma Labbayk, Labbayk, You have no partner, except a partner who is Yours; You possess him and what he possesses!"—which expressed their polytheism and their distortion of the monotheists' Talbiyah. They considered these lowly and worthless beings as partners to Allah. Yet, flies would come and sit upon them, stealing that honey, saffron, musk, and ambergris, and the idols had no power to take it back! The Holy Quran uses this very scene as a theme to express the weakness and inability of the idols and the fragility of the polytheists' logic. It says: Look closely and see how your deities are under the feet of flies and are unable to offer the slightest defense for themselves! What kind of incompetent and worthless deities are these from whom you seek the resolution of your problems?!

Regarding what is meant by "the seeker" (al-talib) and "the sought" (al-matlub), the correct view is what we mentioned above: "the seeker" refers to the worshippers of the idols, and "the sought" refers to the idols themselves—both are weak and helpless.

Some commentators have also suggested that "the seeker" refers to the fly and "the sought" refers to the idols (because flies go to the idols to benefit from the food substances on them).

Others have considered "the seeker" to be the idols and "the sought" to be the fly (because even if the idols intended to create a weak fly, they would be unable). However, the first interpretation seems more accurate.

After presenting the vivid example above, the Quran concludes: "They have not appraised Allah with His true appraisal" (ما قَدَرُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ). They are so weak and incapable in their recognition and knowledge of God that they reduced Allah, with all His greatness, to the level of these weak and insignificant deities and considered them His partners. If they had the slightest knowledge of God, they would laugh at this comparison of theirs.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ).

Unlike the idols who cannot create a small creature and cannot even defend themselves against a fly, He is Powerful and Capable over all things, and no one has the power to oppose Him.

Key Points

A Clear Example to Illustrate Weaknesses

Although a group of commentators believe that the Quran mentions a "parable" (mathal) in the above verses but does not explicitly state the parable itself—instead referring to other instances in the Quran, or that "parable" here simply means the proof and clarification of a matter or something wondrous—this is undoubtedly an incorrect view. The Quran, in the verses above, explicitly states the instance of this parable for which it invited everyone to reflect: this parable is the "fly," both in terms of its creation and its act of stealing food particles!

Although this example was mentioned in the presence of the Arab polytheists, given that the audience is all of humanity (يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ), it is not restricted to stone and wooden idols. Rather, all deities worshipped besides Allah are included in this example, whether they be Pharaohs, Nimrods, idols of false personalities, hollow powers, and the like.

They too fit this example; if they were to join hands, gather all their armies and soldiers, and invite their thinkers and sages, they would be unable to create a fly. And even if a fly were to take a morsel from their table, they would lack the power to retrieve it.

Answer to a Question

It might be said here that modern man, with the power of his science and knowledge, has been able to make inventions that are far superior to a fly.

He has built high-speed vehicles and aircraft that cover vast distances in the blink of an eye. He has invented precise electronic brains that solve the most complex mathematical equations in an instant. Do these statements still hold true for the man of our era?

In response, we say: Building these marvelous tools is undoubtedly proof of the extraordinary progress of human industry. However, all of these are simple and trivial matters compared to the issue of creating a living being and the creation of life.

If we carefully examine books discussing the physiology of living organisms and the biological and vital activities of a small insect like a fly, we will see that the structure of a fly's brain, its nervous system, and its digestive apparatus are far superior to the structure of the most advanced aircraft and are not comparable at all.

Fundamentally, the "issue of life," the sensation and movement of living beings, and their growth and reproduction remain a mystery to scientists. The intricacies and delicacies used in the construction of these beings are themselves further mysteries—mysteries that have not been solved in any way.

According to natural scientists, the incredibly small eyes of some of these insects are themselves composed of hundreds of eyes! That is, the very eye we see with difficulty, which is perhaps no larger than a pinhead, is made up of several hundred smaller eyes, the collection of which is called a "compound eye." Even if man could create a living cell from inanimate matter, who could assemble hundreds of tiny eyes—each of which has its own delicate camera, layers, and mechanisms—side by side, link their connection strings to the insect's brain, and transmit information through them to the brain so that the insect can react appropriately to events occurring around it?

If all humans gathered together, would they have the power to create such a seemingly insignificant but actually very complex and mysterious creature?! Furthermore, even if man were to make all these things possible, could it be called "creation"? Or is it a combination and assembly of materials already existing in this world of creation? Are those who assemble pre-fabricated parts of a car considered inventors, and can their action be called "origination" and "invention"?

وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ فِى ٱللَّهِ حَقَّ جِهَادِهِۦ ۚ هُوَ ٱجْتَبَىٰكُمْ وَمَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ ۚ مِّلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۚ هُوَ سَمَّىٰكُمُ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ مِن قَبْلُ وَفِى هَـٰذَا لِيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ ۚ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱعْتَصِمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ هُوَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ فَنِعْمَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ ٱلنَّصِيرُ (٧٨)
And wage jihad for the sake of Allah, a jihad which is worthy of Him. He has chosen you and has not placed for you any obstacle in the religion, the faith of your father, Abraham. He named you ‘Muslims’ before, and in this, so that the Apostle may be a witness to you, and that you may be witnesses to mankind. So maintain the prayer, give the zakat, and hold fast to Allah. He is your master—an excellent master and an excellent helper. (78)

Occasion of Revelation

As a group of commentators has narrated, some of the polytheists, such as "Walid ibn Mughirah" (who was considered their mastermind), would say with astonishment and denial upon the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) appointment: "أَ أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ مِنْ بَيْنِنا" (Has the divine revelation been sent down to Muhammad—this poor and destitute orphan of our nation—from among all of us?). The first of the above verses were revealed and answered them (stating that the selection of prophets and angels for the mission was based on the criteria of merit and spiritual standing). [^1]

Commentary

Five Constructive and Important Commands

Given that the previous verses discussed monotheism, polytheism, and the imaginary deities of the polytheists, and considering that a group of people chose angels or some prophets for worship, the Quran states in the first of the verses under discussion:

All divine messengers are His obedient servants: "Allah chooses messengers from among the angels and from among men" (اللَّهُ يَصْطَفِي مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ رُسُلًا وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ).

From the angels, messengers like Gabriel, and from humans, emissaries like the great divine prophets. The use of "min" (from), which is partitive here, shows that not all divine angels were His messengers to humanity; rather, a group of them held this position. This expression does not contradict the first verse of Surah Fatir, which says: "جاعِلِ الْمَلائِكَةِ رُسُلًا" (Who made the angels messengers), because that verse also intends to state the genus (type), not the universality of every individual.

At the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed, Allah is All-Hearing, All-Seeing" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ).

That is to say, it is not as if Allah, like humans, is unaware of the work of His messengers in their absence; rather, He is aware of their situation at every moment, hears their words, and sees their actions.

Then, referring to the responsibility of the prophets in conveying the message on one hand, and divine supervision over them on the other, it says: "He knows what is before them and what is behind them" (يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ). He is aware of both their future and their past and what they have left behind.

"And to Allah all matters are returned, and all are responsible before Him" (وَ إِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ).

This is so people may know that the angels and divine prophets are also servants obedient to Allah's command, holding responsibility in His presence, and possessing nothing of their own except what Allah has given them—not that they are deities or gods alongside Allah.

Therefore, the phrase "يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ..." is actually an allusion to the duty and responsibility of divine messengers and the monitoring of their actions by the Lord, similar to what appears in Surah Al-Jinn, verses 27 and 28: (فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى‌ غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى‌ مِنْ رَسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَ مِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَ أَحاطَ بِما لَدَيْهِمْ): "He does not disclose His unseen to anyone, except to a messenger He has chosen and is pleased with, and He sends before him and behind him observers, so that He may know if they have conveyed the messages of their Lord; and He encompasses all that is with them." [^1] Additionally, it becomes clear that "what is before them" refers to future events and "what is behind them" refers to past events.

In the next two verses, which constitute the final verses of Surah Al-Hajj, the address is directed toward the believers, presenting a series of general and comprehensive instructions that preserve their religion, their worldly life, and their victory in all arenas. With this beautiful conclusion, "Surah Al-Hajj" ends. First, it points to four important commands, saying: "O you who have believed, bow down, prostrate yourselves, worship your Lord, and do good, so that you may succeed."

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Tafsir Fakhr Razi, and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani under the verse in question. [^1]: Allamah Tabataba'i in "Tafsir al-Mizan," under the verses in question, considers the phrase "يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ..." an allusion to the issue of infallibility (Ismah) and divine protection of the prophets; however, given what we mentioned above, this interpretation does not seem as close.

(يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ارْكَعُوا وَ اسْجُدُوا وَ اعْبُدُوا رَبَّكُمْ وَ افْعَلُوا الْخَيْرَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ).

The mention of the two pillars of bowing (Ruku') and prostration (Sujud) from among all the pillars of prayer is due to their extraordinary importance in this great act of worship.

The command for servitude (ubudiyyah) in an absolute sense, mentioned after these two, encompasses every form of worship and servitude to Allah.

The expression "your Lord" (ربكم) is, in fact, an allusion to His worthiness of servitude and the unworthiness of others, for He alone is the Owner, Master, and Nurturer.

The command to "do good" (فعل خيرات) includes every kind of righteous deed—without any condition or restriction—and what is narrated from Ibn Abbas that it refers to maintaining family ties and noble character is, in fact, the mention of a vivid example of this general concept.

Then, it issues the fifth command regarding Jihad—in the broad sense of the word—and says: "And strive for Allah with the striving due to Him" (وَ جاهِدُوا فِي اللَّهِ حَقَّ جِهادِهِ). Most Islamic commentators have not taken "Jihad" here to mean specifically armed struggle against enemies; rather, as derived from its linguistic meaning, they have considered it to mean every kind of struggle and effort in the way of Allah, striving to perform good deeds, battling against rebellious desires (the Greater Jihad), and fighting against oppressive enemies (the Lesser Jihad).

The late Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan" narrates from most commentators that "the striving due to Him" (haqq jihadihi) means sincerity of intention and performing deeds for Allah.

Undoubtedly, "the striving due to Him" also has a broad meaning that encompasses quality, quantity, place, time, and other aspects. However, since the stage of "sincerity" (ikhlas) is the most difficult stage in the Jihad of the self, this stage is emphasized, because the infiltration of non-divine thoughts and motives into the human heart and actions is so hidden, subtle, and concealed that none but the special servants of Allah find salvation from it!

In truth, the Holy Quran begins with simple stages in these five commands and leads to the final and highest stages of servitude: first, it speaks of bowing, then higher than that, it speaks of prostration, then worship in general, then performing good deeds whether they are acts of worship or otherwise, and in the final stage, it speaks of Jihad and individual and collective effort and striving in the internal and external realms, in deeds, words, ethics, and intention.

And this is a comprehensive instruction that success and salvation will undoubtedly follow.

And since the thought might arise: how are all these heavy instructions—each more comprehensive and broader than the last—placed upon the shoulders of us weak servants? In the subsequent phrases, there are various expressions showing that these are evidence of divine grace toward you and a sign of the greatness and status of your personality as believers in His presence.

In the first expression, He says: "He has chosen you" (هُوَ اجْتَباكُمْ).

If you were not the chosen ones of Allah, these responsibilities would not have been placed upon your shoulders.

In the next expression, He says: "He has not placed upon you in religion any hardship" (وَ ما جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ).

That is, if you look correctly, these are not arduous duties; rather, they are harmonious and compatible with your pure primordial nature (fitrah). Fundamentally, because they are the means of your perfection and each has a clear philosophy and benefit that returns to yourselves, they will not be bitter to the palate of your soul; rather, they are completely sweet and pleasant.

In the third expression, it says: Furthermore, "this is the religion of your father Abraham" (مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْراهِيمَ).

The application of "father" to "Abraham" is either because the Arabs and Muslims of that day were mostly from the lineage of Ishmael, or because they all held Abraham in high esteem and respected him as a spiritual and intellectual father, even though his pure religion had become contaminated with various superstitions.

Then it uses another expression in this regard, saying: "He named you Muslims in the previous scriptures and in this [Book]" (the Quran) (هُوَ سَمَّاكُمُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَ فِي هذا).

And a Muslim is one who has the honor of being surrendered to all divine commands.

Regarding who the pronoun "He" (هو) refers to, there is discussion among commentators. Some have said it refers to "Allah," meaning Allah named you by this honorable name both in previous books and in the Quran. However, others consider it an allusion to "Abraham," because in verse 128 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we read that after finishing the construction of the Kaaba, Abraham made requests to Allah, among which was: "Our Lord, and make us Muslims [submissive] to You and from our descendants a Muslim nation [submissive] to You" (رَبَّنا وَ اجْعَلْنا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِنا أُمَّةً مُسْلِمَةً لَكَ).

However, the first interpretation seems more correct as it is more compatible with the end of the verse, which says: "He named you Muslims in the previous scriptures and in this Book (the Quran)," and this expression is not suitable for Abraham, but rather suitable for Allah. [^1]

Finally, it uses the fifth and last inspiring expression regarding Muslims and introduces them as the model and example for all nations, saying: "So that the Messenger may be a witness over you and you may be witnesses over the people!" (لِيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ شَهِيداً عَلَيْكُمْ وَ تَكُونُوا شُهَداءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ).

"Shahid" (witness) comes from the root "shuhud," meaning awareness coupled with presence. The meaning of this statement is that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) being a witness over all Muslims means his awareness of the deeds of his nation. This is completely consistent with the narrations of the "presentation of deeds" (ard al-a'mal) and some Quranic verses that allude to it, for according to these narrations, the deeds of the entire nation are presented to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) during the week, and his pure soul becomes aware and informed of all of them; therefore, he is the witness and observer of this nation.

As for this nation being a witness and observer, according to some narrations, it refers to the infallible ones (Ma'sumin) of this nation and the Imams, who are also witnesses over the deeds of the people.

In a tradition from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), we read that he said: "We are the proofs of Allah among His creation, and we are the witnesses of Allah and His signs among His creatures." [^2]

In truth, the addressee in the phrase "so that you may be" (لتكونوا) is apparently the entire nation, but in reality, it refers to a group of their leaders and greats. Addressing the whole for the sake of the part is common in everyday expressions. For example, in verse 20 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, we read that Allah, while enumerating His blessings upon the Children of Israel, addresses them saying: "He made you kings," while we know only a limited number of them reached this position.

Of course, "shuhud" has another meaning as well, which is "practical witnessing"—meaning being the standard of measurement and the model of the deeds of an exemplary and prominent individual for the deeds of others. In this case, all true Muslims will be such, for they are an exemplary nation with the highest religion, who can be a standard and model for measuring character and virtue among all nations.

In a tradition from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read that Allah has given several virtues and superiorities to the Muslim nation, including that in previous nations, their prophet was the witness over his people, but Allah has made my entire nation witnesses over the creation, for He says: "So that the Messenger may be a witness over you and you may be witnesses over the people." [^1] That is, just as the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is the example and model for his nation, you too are examples and models for the people of the world.

This interpretation, at the same time, does not contradict the previous interpretation; it is possible that the entire nation are witnesses, and the Imams are the exemplary and distinguished witnesses. [^2]

At the end of this verse, it once again recounts the five previous duties in more condensed expressions, summarized in three phrases, as an emphasis: "Now that it is so and you possess these distinctions and honors, establish prayer, give zakat, and hold fast to the true religion and the shade of the Lord's grace."

[^1]: In the Holy Quran, in verse 3 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, the name of this religion is explicitly called "Islam": "And I have completed My favor upon you and have approved for you Islam as religion." In numerous verses, the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) is mentioned as "the first of the Muslims" (Al-An'am: 14, Az-Zumar: 12). [^2]: From the book "Kamal al-Din" by Saduq, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 526. Other narrations with the same theme have also been narrated in this regard. [^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 3, p. 105. [^2]: We have discussed this in detail in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 143 of Surah Al-Baqarah, and also in the third volume under verse 41 of Surah An-Nisa.

(فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ آتُوا الزَّكاةَ وَ اعْتَصِمُوا بِاللَّهِ).

For "He is your Patron and Guardian" (هُوَ مَوْلاكُمْ).

"What an excellent Patron, and what an excellent Helper" (فَنِعْمَ الْمَوْلى‌ وَ نِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ).

In truth, the phrase "فَنِعْمَ الْمَوْلى‌ وَ نِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ" is a proof for "وَ اعْتَصِمُوا بِاللَّهِ هُوَ مَوْلاكُمْ"; that is, the command given to you to hold fast only to the shade of the Lord's grace is because He is the best and most superior Patron and the most worthy Helper.

O Lord! Grant us the success and felicity that, by holding fast to Your Pure Essence and connecting with the creation and the Creator, we may be an exemplary people and a model and witness for others, and that we may write a comprehensive and exemplary commentary on this Great Book of Yours until the end.

O Allah! Just as You named us Muslims in Your Heavenly Book, the Quran, and in the books of the predecessors, grant us the success to be entirely submissive to Your command.

O Lord! Grant us victory over the enemies who today, in every corner, intend to assault the Quran and Islam, for You are the best Patron and Helper (فَنِعْمَ الْمَوْلى‌ وَ نِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ).
